Illustrate with an example where BCNF decomposition *loses* an FD. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Armstrong's Axioms** - **Reflexivity:** If $Y \subseteq X$, then $X \rightarrow Y$. *Example:* $\{A,B\} \rightarrow A$. - **Augmentation:** If $X \rightarrow Y$, then $XZ \rightarrow YZ$. *Example:* From $A \rightarrow B$, derive $AC \rightarrow BC$. - **Transitivity:** If $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Y \rightarrow Z$, then $X \rightarrow Z$. **Closure computation $(\text{AB})^{+}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} (\text{AB})^{(0)} &= \{A, B\} \\ (\text{AB})^{(1)} &= \{A, B\} \cup \{C\} \quad &&[\text{using } B \rightarrow C] \\ (\text{AB})^{(2)} &= \{A, B, C\} \cup \{D\} \quad &&[\text{using } C \rightarrow D] \\ (\text{AB})^{(3)} &= \{A, B, C, D\} \cup \{E\} \quad &&[\text{using } D \rightarrow E] \\ (\text{AB})^{+} &= \{A, B, C, D, E\} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) — BCNF & Dependency Preservation** > **BCNF Definition:** A relation $R$ is in BCNF iff for every non-trivial FD $X \rightarrow Y$ in $F^{+}$, $X$ is a superkey of $R$. **Why dependency preservation is not always guaranteed in BCNF:** When we decompose a relation into BCNF, we may have to split an FD across two different relations. After decomposition, the FD's left-hand side and right-hand side may lie in *different* sub-relations, making the FD **unenforceable** without a costly join — i.e., the FD is *not preserved* in the decomposed schema. **Counter-example:** Consider $R(\text{Teacher},\ \text{Subject},\ \text{Department})$ with FDs: $$ \text{Teacher} \rightarrow \text{Subject}, \quad \text{Subject} \rightarrow \text{Department} $$ Candidate key: $\{$Teacher$\}$. - $\text{Teacher} \rightarrow \text{Subject}$ — OK (Teacher is a superkey). - $\text{Subject} \rightarrow \text{Department}$ — Violates BCNF (Subject is not a superkey). **BCNF decomposition:** $$ R_1(\underline{\text{Subject}},\ \text{Department}), \quad R_2(\underline{\text{Teacher}},\ \text{Subject}) $$ The FD $\text{Teacher} \rightarrow \text{Subject}$ spans only $R_2$ (preserved ✅), but the FD $\text{Subject} \rightarrow \text{Department}$ spans only $R_1$ (preserved ✅). Now suppose we had an additional FD $\text{Teacher} \rightarrow \text{Department}$ (derived by transitivity). This **derived FD crosses both relations** and cannot be enforced in either single relation — it is **lost**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** > Examiners deduct marks for the following common mistakes: > 1. **Confusing "prime" with "primary key attribute."** A prime attribute is one that belongs to *any* candidate key — not just the chosen primary key. Many students only check the chosen PK and miss alternate keys. > 2. **Skipping the closure computation.** Jumping directly to a normal-form verdict without first computing $X^{+}$ to confirm a candidate key is a frequent 2-to-3-mark loss. > 3. **Forgetting 1NF before checking 2NF/3NF/BCNF.** A relation with multi-valued cells is automatically *not* in 2NF, 3NF, or BCNF — the student must first resolve 1NF violations. > 4. **Stating "BCNF is always not dependency-preserving."** It is *not always*, only *not guaranteed*. A blank statement loses the precision marks. > 5. **Mixing up partial and transitive dependencies.** Partial = non-prime depends on *part* of a composite key. Transitive = key → non-key → non-key. Examiners check this distinction explicitly. > 6. **Not verifying lossless-join and dependency-preservation.** A complete KTU answer *always* ends with these two verifications for the produced decomposition. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Normalization** = systematic decomposition of relations to remove redundancy and anomalies. - [x] Three **anomalies**: insertion, deletion, update. - [x] **Normal form hierarchy**: 1NF ⊂ 2NF ⊂ 3NF ⊂ BCNF ⊂ 4NF ⊂ 5NF ⊂ DKNF. - [x] **1NF rule:** every cell atomic, no repeating groups. - [x] **2NF rule:** eliminate *partial* dependencies on composite keys. - [x] **3NF rule:** eliminate *transitive* dependencies on non-prime attributes. - [x] **BCNF rule:** for every non-trivial FD, LHS must be a superkey. - [x] **Attribute closure $X^{+}$** is the *central computational tool* — used to find candidate keys and test FD membership. - [x] **Armstrong's axioms** (reflexivity, augmentation, transitivity) are the inference engine for FDs. - [x] **Prime attribute** = belongs to *any* candidate key. **Non-prime** = belongs to *none*. - [x] **Lossless-join test:** $R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_1$ OR $R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_2$. - [x] **Dependency preservation:** $(F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \dots \cup F_n)^{+} = F^{+}$. - [x] **3NF guarantees** dependency preservation; **BCNF does not always**. - [x] **Use 3NF** when you need guaranteed FD enforcement; **use BCNF** when you need maximal redundancy elimination. - [x] **OLTP** systems = fully normalized (BCNF/3NF). **OLAP/data warehouses** = often denormalized for speed. - [x] Always **state the candidate key, prime/non-prime split, the violating FD, and the decomposition rationale** in KTU exam answers. - [x] Always **verify lossless-join and dependency-preservation** at the end of every decomposition answer. > [!TIP] > **One-line mnemonic for the BCNF vs 3NF difference:** *"3NF forgives a non-superkey determinant **only if the dependent is a prime attribute**; BCNF never forgives."*

topics.title: SQL Queries: Develop a set of SQL queries to demonstrate CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete), complex joins, aggregations, and any specialized queries required by your project
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT304 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SQL Queries: Develop a set of SQL queries to demonstrate CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete), complex joins, aggregations, and any specialized queries required by your project

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of SQL

**Structured Query Language (SQL)** is the standard declarative, set-oriented, high-level database language defined by **ISO/IEC 9075** and used to define, manipulate, retrieve, and control data in a **Relational Database Management System (RDBMS)**. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the relevant sub-languages are:

- **DDL (Data Definition Language):** `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`
- **DML (Data Manipulation Language):** `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`
- **DCL (Data Control Language):** `GRANT`, `REVOKE`
- **TCL (Transaction Control Language):** `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRUD** is the universal acronym that maps directly onto SQL DML operations:
> - **C — Create** → `INSERT`
> - **R — Read** → `SELECT`
> - **U — Update** → `UPDATE`
> - **D — Delete** → `DELETE`

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bank Locker Room"

Imagine a bank's **locker room** as your database:

| Locker Room Component | SQL Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The room itself with rows of lockers | **Database** (collection of tables) |
| Each locker | A **Table / Relation** |
| Each locker has a numbered key | A **Primary Key** |
| Your signed authorization slip | A **SQL Query / Transaction** |
| Putting a new document inside | **INSERT** (Create) |
| Reading the document to know balance | **SELECT** (Read) |
| Editing the name on the document | **UPDATE** |
| Tearing up the document and removing it | **DELETE** |

Just as a bank teller must follow strict protocols to ensure the locker integrity, SQL must respect **ACID properties** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) when running CRUD operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in your KTU Mini-Project:** Every module in your project (registration, login, marks entry, inventory tracking) will eventually boil down to these four verbs. Mastering them guarantees clean DBMS viva answers and a working project.

## 1.3 Reference Schema Used Throughout These Notes

For uniform explanation, all examples use the canonical **Student–Course–Enrollment** schema.

**STUDENT** (*StudentID*, Name, DeptID, DOB, CGPA, Email)
**COURSE** (*CourseID*, CourseName, Credits, DeptID)
**ENROLLMENT** (*EnrollID*, StudentID, CourseID, Semester, Grade)
**DEPARTMENT** (*DeptID*, DeptName, HOD)

Primary keys are **bold-italic**, foreign keys are plain. This is the exact standard ER notation followed in KTU exam answer scripts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CRUD Operational Stack

Each CRUD operation is a pipeline with three logical layers:

1. **Authorization layer** — checked via DCL grants
2. **Parsing & Optimization layer** — query rewritten by the optimizer
3. **Execution layer** — runs against the storage engine, with `COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK` controlling visibility

### 2.1.1 CREATE → `INSERT`
Logical steps:
- Verify the target table exists (or use `CREATE TABLE` first)
- Validate **NOT NULL** and **CHECK** constraints
- Validate **FOREIGN KEY** references
- Insert the tuple, update indexes, log to **write-ahead log (WAL)**
- Commit or roll back the transaction

### 2.1.2 READ → `SELECT`
Logical steps:
- **FROM** clause resolves tables/views
- **JOIN** merges rows using predicate (ON/USING)
- **WHERE** filters rows
- **GROUP BY** partitions into groups
- **HAVING** filters groups
- **SELECT** projects columns and applies expressions
- **DISTINCT** removes duplicates
- **ORDER BY** sorts
- **LIMIT/OFFSET** slices the result

### 2.1.3 UPDATE → `UPDATE`
Logical steps:
- Locate target rows via `WHERE` (omitting `WHERE` updates **all** rows — a classic KTU viva trap!)
- Evaluate `SET` expressions
- Apply referential integrity checks
- Log to WAL
- Commit

### 2.1.4 DELETE → `DELETE`
Logical steps:
- Locate target rows via `WHERE`
- Check **ON DELETE CASCADE / RESTRICT / SET NULL** constraints
- Either mark row as tombstone or physically delete
- Commit

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield SQL Reference Table

| Operation | SQL Keyword | Mandatory Clauses | Optional Clauses | Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Create row | `INSERT INTO … VALUES …` | Table name, column list or VALUES | `RETURNING` (PostgreSQL) | Column-count mismatch |
| Read rows | `SELECT` | `SELECT` list, `FROM` | `WHERE`, `GROUP BY`, `HAVING`, `ORDER BY`, `LIMIT` | Cartesian explosion if join missing |
| Update row | `UPDATE … SET …` | Table name, SET clause | `WHERE` | Forgetting `WHERE` |
| Delete row | `DELETE FROM …` | Table name | `WHERE` | Forgetting `WHERE` |
| Combine tables | `JOIN` | `ON` predicate | `USING`, NATURAL | Wrong join type |
| Aggregate | `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX` | Function in SELECT | `GROUP BY` | Mixing aggregates with non-grouped columns |
| Subquery | `(SELECT …)` | Enclosing parentheses | `IN`, `EXISTS`, `=` | Correlated subquery perf |
| Set op | `UNION`, `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT` | Same column count | `ALL` | Type mismatches |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** CRUD operations power almost every real-world software — from ATM withdrawals (`UPDATE` balance) to UPI transactions (`INSERT` into transaction log) to Amazon product listings (`SELECT` with pagination).

## 2.3 Theoretical Foundations of Joins

A **join** is the relational algebra operator $\Join$ that produces a combined relation from two operands using a predicate $P$.

$$
R \Join_{P} S \;=\; \sigma_{P}(R \times S)
$$

- **INNER JOIN** returns rows where predicate holds in both tables.
- **LEFT OUTER JOIN** preserves all left rows, padding unmatched right columns with `NULL`.
- **RIGHT OUTER JOIN** is the mirror.
- **FULL OUTER JOIN** preserves rows from both sides.
- **CROSS JOIN** is the unrestricted Cartesian product $R \times S$.
- **SELF JOIN** joins a table to itself (e.g., employee–manager).

## 2.4 Aggregation Theory

The SQL aggregate operators are defined over a multiset of values $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$:

$$
\text{COUNT}(V) = n
$$

$$
\text{SUM}(V) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i
$$

$$
\text{AVG}(V) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i
$$

$$
\text{MIN}(V) = \min_{i} v_i,\qquad \text{MAX}(V) = \max_{i} v_i
$$

All aggregates (except `COUNT(*)`) **ignore NULL** values — a viva favourite.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

The following walkthrough uses the **Student–Course–Enrollment** schema. Every query is explained line-by-line to ensure 100% comprehension for KTU 2024 examinations.

## 3.1 Schema Definition (DDL)

```sql
CREATE TABLE Department (
    DeptID   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    DeptName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    HOD      VARCHAR(50)
);

CREATE TABLE Student (
    StudentID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Name      VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    DeptID    INT,
    DOB       DATE,
    CGPA      DECIMAL(4,2) CHECK (CGPA BETWEEN 0 AND 10),
    Email     VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE,
    FOREIGN KEY (DeptID) REFERENCES Department(DeptID)
);

CREATE TABLE Course (
    CourseID   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    CourseName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    Credits    INT CHECK (Credits > 0),
    DeptID     INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (DeptID) REFERENCES Department(DeptID)
);

CREATE TABLE Enrollment (
    EnrollID  INT PRIMARY KEY,
    StudentID INT NOT NULL,
    CourseID  INT NOT NULL,
    Semester  VARCHAR(10),
    Grade     CHAR(2) CHECK (Grade IN ('A+','A','B+','B','C+','C','D','F')),
    FOREIGN KEY (StudentID) REFERENCES Student(StudentID) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (CourseID)  REFERENCES Course(CourseID)
);
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Line-by-line logic:** `PRIMARY KEY` enforces entity integrity. `CHECK` enforces domain integrity. `FOREIGN KEY … ON DELETE CASCADE` enforces referential integrity with cascading deletes — a frequently asked viva question.

## 3.2 CREATE — Inserting Data

```sql
-- Single-row insert
INSERT INTO Student (StudentID, Name, DeptID, DOB, CGPA, Email)
VALUES (101, 'Ananya Nair', 1, '2004-05-12', 9.12, 'ananya@ktu.in');
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
1. The `INSERT INTO` clause specifies the target table.
2. The column list dictates the order of values in `VALUES`.
3. The DBMS validates `CGPA` against the `CHECK (0..10)` constraint.
4. `DeptID = 1` is verified against the `Department` table.
5. Upon success, the row is written to disk and the unique index on `Email` is updated.

```sql
-- Multi-row insert (bulk loading)
INSERT INTO Student (StudentID, Name, DeptID, DOB, CGPA, Email) VALUES
(102, 'Rahul Menon', 1, '2003-11-04', 8.45, 'rahul@ktu.in'),
(103, 'Sneha Pillai', 2, '2004-01-22', 8.95, 'sneha@ktu.in'),
(104, 'Arjun Das',    3, '2003-07-18', 7.20, 'arjun@ktu.in'),
(105, 'Meera Joseph', 1, '2004-03-09', 9.55, 'meera@ktu.in');
```

```sql
-- Insert using a subquery (create-then-load pattern)
INSERT INTO TopPerformers (StudentID, Name, CGPA)
SELECT StudentID, Name, CGPA FROM Student WHERE CGPA > 9.0;
```

## 3.3 READ — Select Queries of Increasing Complexity

### 3.3.1 Projection
```sql
SELECT Name, Email FROM Student;
```
Returns only the requested columns. If the table has 10,000 rows, all 10,000 are produced unless a `WHERE` clause filters them.

### 3.3.2 Selection with predicate
```sql
SELECT Name, CGPA FROM Student
WHERE DeptID = 1 AND CGPA >= 8.5;
```
**Logic:** the `WHERE` clause is evaluated **before** projection. Combining conditions with `AND`/`OR` requires parentheses for clarity.

### 3.3.3 Sorting and limiting
```sql
SELECT Name, CGPA FROM Student
ORDER BY CGPA DESC
LIMIT 5 OFFSET 0;
```
Returns the **top 5** students by CGPA. `OFFSET 5` would return students ranked 6–10 — essential for **pagination** in your project.

### 3.3.4 DISTINCT
```sql
SELECT DISTINCT DeptID FROM Student;
```
Removes duplicate `DeptID` values to show which departments currently have at least one student.

### 3.3.5 LIKE pattern matching
```sql
SELECT * FROM Student WHERE Name LIKE 'A%';
```
The `%` wildcard matches any sequence of zero or more characters. `_` matches exactly one character.

## 3.4 UPDATE — Modifying Data

```sql
-- Single-row update
UPDATE Student SET CGPA = 9.30 WHERE StudentID = 101;
```

```sql
-- Bulk update with computation
UPDATE Student SET CGPA = CGPA + 0.05 WHERE DeptID = 1;
```
Adds a 0.05 grace-point bonus to all CS students. **Explanation:** the right-hand side is evaluated first using the **old** value, then the new value is written.

```sql
-- Update with subquery (cross-table)
UPDATE Enrollment
SET Grade = 'A+'
WHERE CourseID = (SELECT CourseID FROM Course WHERE CourseName = 'DBMS')
  AND Semester = 'S5';
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical pitfall:** `UPDATE Student SET CGPA = 9.5;` (no `WHERE`) modifies **every** row. In MySQL, the safe-update mode can be enabled with `SET SQL_SAFE_UPDATES = 1;`.

## 3.5 DELETE — Removing Data

```sql
-- Single-row delete
DELETE FROM Student WHERE StudentID = 110;
```

```sql
-- Conditional delete
DELETE FROM Enrollment WHERE Semester = 'S3' AND Grade = 'F';
```

```sql
-- Truncate vs. Delete
TRUNCATE TABLE TopPerformers;
```
**Difference:** `DELETE` is DML, row-by-row, transaction-logged, can use `WHERE`. `TRUNCATE` is DDL, resets the table wholesale, cannot be rolled back in many RDBMSs, and is much faster for emptying large tables.

## 3.6 Complex Joins

### 3.6.1 INNER JOIN — three-table join
```sql
SELECT s.Name, c.CourseName, e.Grade, d.DeptName
FROM   Student s
JOIN   Enrollment e ON s.StudentID = e.StudentID
JOIN   Course     c ON e.CourseID  = c.CourseID
JOIN   Department d ON s.DeptID    = d.DeptID
WHERE  e.Semester = 'S5'
ORDER  BY s.Name;
```
**Logic:**
1. Join `Student` with `Enrollment` on `StudentID`.
2. Join the result with `Course` on `CourseID`.
3. Join the result with `Department` on `DeptID`.
4. Filter rows where `Semester = 'S5'`.
5. Sort by student name alphabetically.

### 3.6.2 LEFT OUTER JOIN — find students with no enrollment
```sql
SELECT s.StudentID, s.Name
FROM   Student s
LEFT   JOIN Enrollment e ON s.StudentID = e.StudentID
WHERE  e.EnrollID IS NULL;
```
**Logic:** A `LEFT JOIN` keeps every student. If no matching enrollment exists, the right side is `NULL`. The `IS NULL` filter retains only those unmatched rows — the canonical "anti-join" pattern.

### 3.6.3 SELF JOIN — students from the same department
```sql
SELECT a.Name AS Student1, b.Name AS Student2, a.DeptID
FROM   Student a
JOIN   Student b ON a.DeptID = b.DeptID AND a.StudentID < b.StudentID;
```
The `a.StudentID < b.StudentID` predicate prevents pairing a student with themselves and avoids duplicate pairs.

### 3.6.4 FULL OUTER JOIN — students OR courses with no link
```sql
SELECT s.Name, c.CourseName
FROM   Student s
FULL   OUTER JOIN Enrollment e ON s.StudentID = e.StudentID
FULL   OUTER JOIN Course c     ON e.CourseID  = c.CourseID
WHERE  s.StudentID IS NULL OR c.CourseID IS NULL;
```

## 3.7 Aggregations & GROUP BY

### 3.7.1 Simple aggregation
```sql
SELECT COUNT(*)        AS TotalStudents,
       AVG(CGPA)       AS AvgCGPA,
       MAX(CGPA)       AS TopCGPA,
       MIN(CGPA)       AS LowestCGPA
FROM   Student;
```

### 3.7.2 GROUP BY with HAVING
```sql
SELECT d.DeptName,
       COUNT(s.StudentID) AS StudentCount,
       AVG(s.CGPA)        AS AvgDeptCGPA
FROM   Department d
LEFT   JOIN Student s ON d.DeptID = s.DeptID
GROUP  BY d.DeptName
HAVING COUNT(s.StudentID) > 5
ORDER  BY AvgDeptCGPA DESC;
```
**Logic:**
- `GROUP BY` partitions the joined rows by department.
- `COUNT(s.StudentID)` counts non-NULL matches (a `LEFT JOIN` puts `NULL` for empty depts).
- `HAVING` filters **groups** (use `WHERE` for rows, `HAVING` for groups — common viva trap!).

### 3.7.3 Aggregation with subquery — top scorers per course
```sql
SELECT c.CourseName, s.Name, e.Grade
FROM   Enrollment e
JOIN   Student s ON e.StudentID = s.StudentID
JOIN   Course  c ON e.CourseID  = c.CourseID
WHERE  (e.CourseID, e.Grade) IN (
        SELECT CourseID, MIN(Grade)    -- assuming A+ sorts lowest alphabetically
        FROM   Enrollment
        GROUP  BY CourseID);
```

## 3.8 Subqueries & Set Operations

```sql
-- Students who took DBMS OR TOC
SELECT Name FROM Student
WHERE StudentID IN (
    SELECT StudentID FROM Enrollment e
    JOIN Course c ON e.CourseID = c.CourseID
    WHERE c.CourseName IN ('DBMS','TOC'))
UNION
SELECT Name FROM Student WHERE CGPA > 9.0;
```

**Logic:** `UNION` combines and de-duplicates; `UNION ALL` retains duplicates; `INTERSECT` keeps only common rows; `EXCEPT` keeps rows in the first query but not the second.

## 3.9 Analytical (Window) Functions — Project Favourite

```sql
SELECT s.Name, c.CourseName, e.Grade,
       RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY c.CourseID ORDER BY e.Grade ASC) AS Position
FROM   Enrollment e
JOIN   Student s ON e.StudentID = s.StudentID
JOIN   Course  c ON e.CourseID  = c.CourseID;
```

**Logic:**
- `PARTITION BY` resets the rank for each course.
- `ORDER BY Grade ASC` ranks grade-wise (A+ is best).
- `RANK()` ties and skips; `DENSE_RANK()` ties but does not skip; `ROW_NUMBER()` is unique.

## 3.10 Transaction Control (CRUD safety net)

```sql
START TRANSACTION;
    UPDATE Student SET CGPA = CGPA + 0.10 WHERE StudentID = 101;
    INSERT INTO AuditLog (Action, StudentID, Time) VALUES ('GRACE',101,NOW());
COMMIT;
-- ROLLBACK;  -- uncomment to undo
```
This guarantees that the grade update and the audit log are **atomic** — both happen or neither happens.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CRUD-to-Operations Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application / User Request] --> B{Operation Type}
    B -- C --> C1[INSERT INTO Table]
    B -- R --> C2[SELECT with WHERE/JOIN/GROUP BY]
    B -- U --> C3[UPDATE with SET and WHERE]
    B -- D --> C4[DELETE FROM with WHERE]
    C1 --> D[Constraint Validation]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D -- Pass --> E[Query Optimizer]
    D -- Fail --> F[Return Error]
    E --> G[Execution Engine]
    G --> H[Write-Ahead Log]
    H -- COMMIT --> I[Persistent Storage]
    H -- ROLLBACK --> J[Undo Log Restore]
```

## 4.2 SQL Logical Query Processing Order

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: FROM & JOINs] --> S2[Step 2: WHERE]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: GROUP BY]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: HAVING]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: SELECT projection]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: DISTINCT]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: ORDER BY]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: LIMIT / OFFSET]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Students often write `SELECT` first and `WHERE` last. SQL is **declarative** but the engine processes clauses in the order shown above. Aliases defined in `SELECT` cannot be used in `WHERE` for this reason.

## 4.3 Entity-Relationship Overview of Reference Schema

```mermaid
erDiagram
    DEPARTMENT ||--o{ STUDENT : "has"
    DEPARTMENT ||--o{ COURSE : "offers"
    STUDENT ||--o{ ENROLLMENT : "registers"
    COURSE  ||--o{ ENROLLMENT : "is taken in"
    DEPARTMENT {
        int DeptID PK
        string DeptName
        string HOD
    }
    STUDENT {
        int StudentID PK
        string Name
        int DeptID FK
        date DOB
        decimal CGPA
        string Email
    }
    COURSE {
        int CourseID PK
        string CourseName
        int Credits
        int DeptID FK
    }
    ENROLLMENT {
        int EnrollID PK
        int StudentID FK
        int CourseID FK
        string Semester
        string Grade
    }
```

## 4.4 Project-Ready CRUD Function Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UI[Frontend UI Form] --> API[Backend API Endpoint]
    API --> V[Input Validation]
    V --> P[Prepared SQL Statement]
    P --> DB[(RDBMS Engine)]
    DB --> R[Result Set / Rowcount]
    R --> M[Map to JSON / View]
    M --> UI
    DB -.Error.-> EH[Exception Handler]
    EH --> UI
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** In your KTU mini-project (e.g., Library, Hospital, or Inventory), every UI button maps to one CRUD verb, which maps to one prepared statement. Using **parameterized queries** (`?` placeholders) prevents **SQL Injection** — a guaranteed viva question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP in SQL.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `DELETE` | `TRUNCATE` | `DROP` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | DML | DDL | DDL |
| Removes | Specific rows (`WHERE`) | All rows | Entire table (structure + data) |
| Rollback | Possible (transactional) | Usually not | Not possible |
| Triggers fire? | Yes | No | No |
| Speed | Slow (row-by-row) | Fast | Fastest |

*Mnemonic:* **D**ELETE deletes **D**ata, **D**ROP deletes **D**efinition, **T**RUNCATE **T**akes everything.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING clause? Give an example.**

**Model Answer:**
- `WHERE` filters **rows** **before** aggregation; cannot use aggregate functions.
- `HAVING` filters **groups** **after** aggregation; can use aggregate functions.

**Example:**
```sql
SELECT DeptID, AVG(CGPA)
FROM   Student
WHERE  CGPA > 5.0            -- row filter (no aggregate)
GROUP  BY DeptID
HAVING AVG(CGPA) > 8.0;      -- group filter (aggregate allowed)
```

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

(a) *For the Student–Course–Enrollment schema above, write SQL queries to perform the following:* **[7 Marks]**
1. Insert a new student record.
2. List the names of all students who scored 'A+' in any course along with the course name.
3. Update the grade of all students in 'DBMS' course to 'B+' for Semester 'S4'.
4. Delete all enrollment records where the grade is 'F' and the semester is 'S1'.

(b) *Demonstrate a three-table INNER JOIN, a LEFT OUTER JOIN, and a GROUP BY query with HAVING on the same schema. Show the output for each.* **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q A (a)

**(i) Insert a new student:**
```sql
INSERT INTO Student (StudentID, Name, DeptID, DOB, CGPA, Email)
VALUES (106, 'Vivek Kumar', 2, '2004-09-15', 8.75, 'vivek@ktu.in');
```
**Explanation:** The columns and values must align positionally. Constraints on `CGPA` and `Email` will be auto-validated.

**(ii) List students with 'A+' grades:**
```sql
SELECT s.Name, c.CourseName
FROM   Student s
JOIN   Enrollment e ON s.StudentID = e.StudentID
JOIN   Course     c ON e.CourseID  = c.CourseID
WHERE  e.Grade = 'A+';
```

**(iii) Update grades:**
```sql
UPDATE Enrollment
SET    Grade = 'B+'
WHERE  Semester = 'S4'
  AND  CourseID = (SELECT CourseID FROM Course WHERE CourseName = 'DBMS');
```

**(iv) Delete failed records:**
```sql
DELETE FROM Enrollment
WHERE  Grade = 'F' AND Semester = 'S1';
```

---

#### Model Solution for Q A (b)

**Three-table INNER JOIN — S5 result sheet:**
```sql
SELECT s.StudentID, s.Name, c.CourseName, e.Grade, d.DeptName
FROM   Student s
INNER  JOIN Enrollment e ON s.StudentID = e.StudentID
INNER  JOIN Course     c ON e.CourseID  = c.CourseID
INNER  JOIN Department d ON s.DeptID    = d.DeptID
WHERE  e.Semester = 'S5'
ORDER  BY s.StudentID;
```
**Output (sample):**
| StudentID | Name | CourseName | Grade | DeptName |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | Ananya Nair | DBMS | A+ | CSE |
| 102 | Rahul Menon | DBMS | A  | CSE |
| 105 | Meera Joseph | DBMS | A+ | CSE |

**LEFT OUTER JOIN — courses with no enrolments:**
```sql
SELECT c.CourseID, c.CourseName
FROM   Course c
LEFT   JOIN Enrollment e ON c.CourseID = e.CourseID
WHERE  e.EnrollID IS NULL;
```
**Logic:** preserves every course; the `IS NULL` filter reveals orphans.

**GROUP BY with HAVING — departments with avg CGPA > 8.0:**
```sql
SELECT d.DeptName,
       COUNT(s.StudentID) AS NumStudents,
       ROUND(AVG(s.CGPA),2) AS AvgCGPA
FROM   Department d
JOIN   Student s ON d.DeptID = s.DeptID
GROUP  BY d.DeptName
HAVING AVG(s.CGPA) > 8.0
ORDER  BY AvgCGPA DESC;
```

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

(a) *Explain the ACID properties of a transaction with a banking-style example demonstrating two concurrent `UPDATE` operations on the same row.* **[7 Marks]**

(b) *Write SQL queries for:* **[7 Marks]**
1. The second-highest CGPA student in each department (use subquery or window function).
2. Students who have enrolled in **all** courses offered by the CSE department.
3. Compute, for each course, the total credits earned by students who got an 'A' or 'A+'.

---

#### Model Solution for Q B (a)

**ACID Properties:**

| Letter | Property | Banking Example |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Atomicity | Transfer ₹5000 from A→B: debit and credit must both happen. |
| **C** | Consistency | Total balance remains constant; constraints not violated. |
| **I** | Isolation | Two parallel transfers of A→B and A→C must not interleave incorrectly. |
| **D** | Durability | Once committed, the transfer survives a power failure. |

**Concurrent UPDATE demonstration:**
```sql
-- Session 1
START TRANSACTION;
UPDATE Account SET Balance = Balance - 5000 WHERE AccID = 'A';
-- ... (delay)
UPDATE Account SET Balance = Balance + 5000 WHERE AccID = 'B';
COMMIT;

-- Session 2 (concurrent)
START TRANSACTION;
UPDATE Account SET Balance = Balance - 3000 WHERE AccID = 'A';
COMMIT;
```
Without proper isolation (e.g., `SERIALIZABLE`), the lost-update problem can occur. The engine uses **row-level locks** and **write-ahead logging** to prevent it.

---

#### Model Solution for Q B (b)

**(i) Second-highest CGPA per department (window function):**
```sql
SELECT Name, DeptID, CGPA
FROM (
    SELECT s.Name, s.DeptID, s.CGPA,
           DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY s.DeptID ORDER BY s.CGPA DESC) AS rnk
    FROM   Student s
) ranked
WHERE rnk = 2;
```

**Alternative using correlated subquery:**
```sql
SELECT Name, DeptID, CGPA
FROM   Student s1
WHERE  1 = (
    SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT s2.CGPA)
    FROM   Student s2
    WHERE  s2.DeptID = s1.DeptID AND s2.CGPA > s1.CGPA);
```

**(ii) Students enrolled in ALL CSE courses:**
```sql
SELECT s.Name
FROM   Student s
JOIN   Enrollment e ON s.StudentID = e.StudentID
JOIN   Course     c ON e.CourseID  = c.CourseID
WHERE  c.DeptID = (SELECT DeptID FROM Department WHERE DeptName = 'CSE')
GROUP  BY s.StudentID, s.Name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT c.CourseID) =
       (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Course
        WHERE DeptID = (SELECT DeptID FROM Department WHERE DeptName='CSE'));
```
**Logic:** A student is "enrolled in all" if the count of distinct courses they take equals the total number of CSE courses. This is the classic **relational division** pattern — a KTU favourite.

**(iii) Total credits earned by 'A' and 'A+' students per course:**
```sql
SELECT c.CourseName,
       SUM(c.Credits) AS TotalCreditsByToppers
FROM   Course c
JOIN   Enrollment e ON c.CourseID = e.CourseID
WHERE  e.Grade IN ('A','A+')
GROUP  BY c.CourseID, c.CourseName;
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting `WHERE` in `UPDATE` / `DELETE`** — wipes entire table; expect 0 marks.
> 2. **Mixing aggregate and non-aggregate columns in `SELECT`** without `GROUP BY` — strict-mode SQL will throw an error; loose-mode MySQL hides the bug but examiner deducts.
> 3. **Wrong join type** — using `INNER JOIN` where `LEFT JOIN` is required (e.g., for orphan rows) loses conceptual marks.
> 4. **Confusing `UNION` (dedup) with `UNION ALL` (no dedup)** — read the question carefully.
> 5. **Quoting string literals incorrectly** — single quotes for SQL strings, double quotes for identifiers (ANSI).
> 6. **In viva:** Not knowing the **logical processing order** (FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → DISTINCT → ORDER BY → LIMIT) costs easy marks.
> 7. **Schema missing in the answer sheet** — always draw the **schema/ER diagram first** before writing queries; examiners award 1–2 marks just for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **CRUD = INSERT, SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE.** All four are DML except `TRUNCATE` (DDL).
- Always draw the **schema/ER diagram** before writing queries in the answer sheet.
- SQL is **declarative** — you say *what* you want, the engine decides *how*.
- **Logical order of execution:** `FROM` → `JOIN` → `WHERE` → `GROUP BY` → `HAVING` → `SELECT` → `DISTINCT` → `ORDER BY` → `LIMIT`.
- `WHERE` filters **rows**; `HAVING` filters **groups**.
- `COUNT(*)`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX` are the five aggregate functions.
- **Joins:** INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS, SELF — choose by what you want to keep.
- `INNER JOIN` = intersection, `LEFT JOIN` = all left + matched right, `FULL JOIN` = union of both.
- **Relational division pattern** (`HAVING COUNT = total`) is used for "all/enrolled-in-every" questions.
- **Window functions** (`RANK`, `DENSE_RANK`, `ROW_NUMBER`) outperform correlated subqueries for ranking.
- `UNION` removes duplicates; `UNION ALL` does not.
- **Transactions** must follow **ACID**; use `COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK` to enforce atomicity.
- In projects, always use **prepared/parameterized statements** to prevent **SQL injection**.
- `TRUNCATE` resets the table, `DELETE` removes rows, `DROP` removes the table itself.
- **NULL handling:** aggregates (except `COUNT(*)`) skip NULLs; use `IS NULL`, not `= NULL`.
- **Indexes** on `WHERE`/`JOIN` columns dramatically improve `SELECT` performance.
- For your KTU mini-project: structure the **backend** around CRUD endpoints — registration = `INSERT`, dashboard = `SELECT`, edit-profile = `UPDATE`, delete-account = `DELETE`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timeline: Create a timeline or project plan with milestones to ensure timely completion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT304 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Timeline: Create a timeline or project plan with milestones to ensure timely completion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **PBITT304 – Database Management System** course (KTU 2024 Scheme), a **Project Timeline** is a formally structured, chronological representation of all tasks, deliverables, and checkpoints required to complete the DBMS mini-project or course project. It is constructed using the principles of **Project Management Scheduling (PMS)**, integrating the **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)**, **Gantt Charts**, and the **Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT)** to allocate finite time, manpower, and computational resources.

According to the **Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK) 7th Edition**, a milestone is formally defined as a **significant point or event in the project schedule that has zero duration**, marking the completion of a major deliverable. In the DBMS course, milestones typically correspond to the end of each SDLC phase: *Requirement Gathering → ER Modeling → Relational Schema Mapping → Normalization → SQL Implementation → Front-end Integration → Testing & Deployment*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Every course project submitted without a formally documented **Project Timeline with Milestones** is liable for a **5-mark penalty** in the internal evaluation rubric, and may be rejected outright by the project coordinator if not approved during the *Phase 0 – Project Proposal Review*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **wedding ceremony**. You wouldn't just wake up one morning and start the wedding — that would be chaotic. Instead, you create a checklist:
- **3 months before:** Book the venue, send invitations.
- **2 months before:** Finalize the menu, order the cake.
- **1 month before:** Send reminders, do dress fittings.
- **1 week before:** Confirm all vendors.
- **Day 0 (Milestone):** 🎉 The Wedding Day!

A **Database Project Timeline** works exactly the same way. The *wedding day* is your final submission deadline, and every checklist item is a *task*. The major checkpoints (3 months, 2 months, 1 month before) are your **milestones**. Without this plan, you risk delivering an incomplete or buggy database application on submission day.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Parallel:** In the IT industry, companies like **TCS, Infosys, and Oracle** use enterprise tools such as **Jira, Microsoft Project, and Asana** to track DBMS-based application development. Every sprint in their **Agile-Scrum methodology** has a sprint review milestone, mirroring the milestones you define in your KTU DBMS project.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus-Aligned)

| Term | Formal Definition | DBMS Project Context |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Task** | A discrete unit of work with defined start and end dates | Designing the ER Diagram |
| **Milestone** | A zero-duration checkpoint marking completion of a phase | End of Normalization Phase |
| **Gantt Chart** | A horizontal bar chart visualizing tasks against time | Visual timeline for the entire DBMS project |
| **Critical Path** | The longest sequence of dependent tasks determining the minimum project duration | The bottleneck phase that delays the project |
| **Slack/Float** | The amount of time a task can be delayed without delaying the project | Buffer time for non-critical tasks like documentation |
| **PERT** | Program Evaluation and Review Technique for time estimation | Estimating uncertain tasks like "User Testing" |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

The **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum** emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. The ability to plan, schedule, and deliver a technical project on time directly maps to:

- **CO2 (Module 2):** *Design and implement a relational database schema for a real-world problem.*
- **CO5 (Project Component):** *Demonstrate professional project management skills including planning, documentation, and timely delivery.*
- **PO5 (Modern Tool Usage):** *Create, select, and apply appropriate techniques, resources, and modern engineering tools.*

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** Questions on project planning and timeline creation are frequently asked in **Part A (3 marks)** of the End Semester Examination (ESE) under the course project evaluation module. Students who can articulate *what* a milestone is, *why* it is needed, and *how* to compute critical path duration score significantly higher.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The DBMS Project Lifecycle

A DBMS course project, in alignment with the **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** and the **KTU 2024 Project Rubric**, is decomposed into **six core phases**. Each phase produces a tangible deliverable and concludes with a **milestone**.

### Phase 1: Problem Identification & Requirement Gathering
- **Objective:** Identify a real-world problem (e.g., *Library Management System*, *Hospital Database*, *E-Commerce Backend*).
- **Deliverable:** Software Requirements Specification (SRS) document.
- **Key Activities:** Stakeholder interviews, use case identification, functional & non-functional requirement listing.
- **Milestone M1:** *SRS Document Approved by Guide.*

### Phase 2: Conceptual Design (ER Modeling)
- **Objective:** Translate textual requirements into an Entity-Relationship (ER) diagram.
- **Deliverable:** ER Diagram + Entity, Attribute, Relationship inventory.
- **Key Activities:** Identifying entities, attributes, primary keys, relationships (1:1, 1:N, M:N), cardinality, participation (total/partial).
- **Milestone M2:** *ER Diagram Validated & Frozen.*

### Phase 3: Logical Design (Relational Schema Mapping)
- **Objective:** Convert the ER model into a set of well-defined relational tables.
- **Deliverable:** Relational schema with primary keys, foreign keys, and constraints.
- **Key Activities:** Applying mapping rules, eliminating M:N relationships, handling inheritance (if any), defining referential integrity.
- **Milestone M3:** *Relational Schema Finalized.*

### Phase 4: Normalization & Refinement
- **Objective:** Eliminate data redundancy and update anomalies.
- **Deliverable:** Normalized tables (up to **3NF** or **BCNF** as per KTU 2024 syllabus).
- **Key Activities:** Functional dependency analysis, identifying candidate keys, applying 1NF → 2NF → 3NF → BCNF transformations.
- **Milestone M4:** *Database Schema in 3NF/BCNF Approved.*

### Phase 5: Physical Implementation (SQL + DDL/DML)
- **Objective:** Implement the schema in an actual RDBMS (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle).
- **Deliverable:** SQL script file (.sql) with `CREATE TABLE`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`, complex `SELECT` with joins, subqueries, and aggregate functions.
- **Key Activities:** Writing DDL, DML, DCL, TCL commands; creating indexes and views; writing stored procedures (advanced).
- **Milestone M5:** *Working Database with Sample Data Demonstrated.*

### Phase 6: Front-End Integration, Testing & Deployment
- **Objective:** Connect the database to a front-end (PHP, Python-Django, Java Swing, Node.js) and test the system.
- **Deliverable:** Fully functional application + Test cases + Test report.
- **Key Activities:** UI design, API/database connectivity, unit testing, integration testing, user acceptance testing (UAT).
- **Milestone M6:** *Final Project Submission & Demo.*

## 2.2 PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) – Three-Point Estimation

In real-world DBMS projects, task durations are **uncertain**. The **Optimistic Time (O)**, **Most Likely Time (M)**, and **Pessimistic Time (P)** estimates are used to compute the **Expected Time (TE)** using the beta-distribution formula:

$$T_E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$$

The **Variance (σ²)** of each task is computed as:

$$\sigma^2 = \left( \frac{P - O}{6} \right)^2$$

The **Standard Deviation (σ)** is:

$$\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6}$$

For a project with multiple parallel paths, the **Project Standard Deviation** is:

$$\sigma_{project} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_{critical\_path}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Critical Path** is identified as the path with the **maximum sum of expected times**. Any delay along the critical path directly delays the entire project.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Examinations)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | Units / Remarks |
|---|---------|---------------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Expected Time (PERT) | $T_E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$ | O, M, P in days | Beta-distribution approximation |
| 2 | Variance of a Task | $\sigma^2 = \left( \frac{P - O}{6} \right)^2$ | days² | Measure of uncertainty |
| 3 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6}$ | days | Square root of variance |
| 4 | Project Std. Deviation | $\sigma_{project} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_{CP}^2}$ | days | Sum over critical path |
| 5 | Earliest Start (ES) | $ES_j = \max(ES_i + D_i)$ for all predecessors i | days | Forward pass |
| 6 | Earliest Finish (EF) | $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$ | days | ES + Duration |
| 7 | Latest Finish (LF) | $LF_i = \min(LS_j) - D_i$ for successors j | days | Backward pass |
| 8 | Latest Start (LS) | $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$ | days | LF − Duration |
| 9 | Total Float / Slack | $Slack_i = LS_i - ES_i$ | days | If 0 → Critical |
| 10 | Free Float | $FF_i = \min(ES_{successors}) - EF_i$ | days | Independent delay allowed |
| 11 | Critical Path Duration | $T_{CP} = \sum D_{critical\_tasks}$ | days | Project minimum duration |
| 12 | Project Completion Probability | $Z = \frac{T_S - T_E}{\sigma_{project}}$ | dimensionless | Z-score for given deadline $T_S$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In the IT industry, these formulas are embedded in tools like **Microsoft Project**, **Primavera P6**, and **Open Source alternatives like ProjectLibre**. KTU students are expected to understand the math, even if they use software to compute the values.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Database Engineering

1. **Sprint Planning in Agile-Scrum:** DBMS development teams allocate story points and time-boxed sprints (typically **2 weeks**). The expected time formula helps in committing realistic sprint goals.
2. **Database Migration Projects:** When migrating from Oracle to PostgreSQL, the project manager uses PERT to estimate migration duration across heterogeneous environments.
3. **Capstone & Final Year Projects:** KTU final year projects (often involving full-stack DBMS applications) require Gantt charts in the project proposal to secure guide approval.
4. **Industry Certifications:** PMI-PMP, PRINCE2, and CSM certifications all test these formulas — your KTU project gives you a head start.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: PERT Computation for a DBMS Mini-Project

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
You are the project lead for a DBMS-based *Student Attendance Management System*. The project has been decomposed into **7 tasks (A through G)** with the following three-point estimates (in days):

| Task | Description | Predecessor | Optimistic (O) | Most Likely (M) | Pessimistic (P) |
|------|-------------|-------------|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| A | Requirement Gathering & SRS | — | 4 | 6 | 14 |
| B | ER Diagram Design | A | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| C | Relational Schema Mapping | B | 3 | 4 | 11 |
| D | Normalization (up to 3NF) | C | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| E | SQL Implementation (DDL+DML) | D | 5 | 7 | 15 |
| F | Front-End Integration | E | 4 | 6 | 8 |
| G | Testing & Documentation | F | 3 | 5 | 13 |

**Required:** Compute the expected time, variance, identify the critical path, project duration, and project standard deviation.

### Step 1: Compute Expected Time (TE) for Each Task

Using the formula $T_E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$:

**Task A:**
$$T_E(A) = \frac{4 + 4(6) + 14}{6} = \frac{4 + 24 + 14}{6} = \frac{42}{6} = 7 \text{ days}$$

**Task B:**
$$T_E(B) = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 4}{6} = \frac{2 + 12 + 4}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days}$$

**Task C:**
$$T_E(C) = \frac{3 + 4(4) + 11}{6} = \frac{3 + 16 + 11}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days}$$

**Task D:**
$$T_E(D) = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 4}{6} = \frac{2 + 12 + 4}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days}$$

**Task E:**
$$T_E(E) = \frac{5 + 4(7) + 15}{6} = \frac{5 + 28 + 15}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8 \text{ days}$$

**Task F:**
$$T_E(F) = \frac{4 + 4(6) + 8}{6} = \frac{4 + 24 + 8}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days}$$

**Task G:**
$$T_E(G) = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 13}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 13}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days}$$

### Step 2: Compute Variance (σ²) for Each Task

Using the formula $\sigma^2 = \left( \frac{P - O}{6} \right)^2$:

**Task A:** $\sigma^2(A) = \left( \frac{14 - 4}{6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{10}{6} \right)^2 = (1.667)^2 = 2.78$ days²

**Task B:** $\sigma^2(B) = \left( \frac{4 - 2}{6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{2}{6} \right)^2 = (0.333)^2 = 0.11$ days²

**Task C:** $\sigma^2(C) = \left( \frac{11 - 3}{6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{8}{6} \right)^2 = (1.333)^2 = 1.78$ days²

**Task D:** $\sigma^2(D) = \left( \frac{4 - 2}{6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{2}{6} \right)^2 = (0.333)^2 = 0.11$ days²

**Task E:** $\sigma^2(E) = \left( \frac{15 - 5}{6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{10}{6} \right)^2 = (1.667)^2 = 2.78$ days²

**Task F:** $\sigma^2(F) = \left( \frac{8 - 4}{6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{4}{6} \right)^2 = (0.667)^2 = 0.44$ days²

**Task G:** $\sigma^2(G) = \left( \frac{13 - 3}{6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{10}{6} \right)^2 = (1.667)^2 = 2.78$ days²

### Step 3: Construct the Network & Identify Paths

Since the project has a **linear dependency chain** (A → B → C → D → E → F → G), there is **only ONE path** from start to finish. This is automatically the critical path.

**Path:** A → B → C → D → E → F → G

### Step 4: Compute Project Duration (T_E)

$$T_E(\text{Project}) = 7 + 3 + 5 + 3 + 8 + 6 + 6 = 38 \text{ days}$$

### Step 5: Compute Project Standard Deviation

Since the critical path contains all tasks:

$$\sigma_{project} = \sqrt{2.78 + 0.11 + 1.78 + 0.11 + 2.78 + 0.44 + 2.78}$$

$$\sigma_{project} = \sqrt{10.78} = 3.28 \text{ days}$$

### Step 6: Probability of Meeting a 42-Day Deadline

If the project guide sets a **42-day hard deadline**, the Z-score is:

$$Z = \frac{T_S - T_E}{\sigma_{project}} = \frac{42 - 38}{3.28} = \frac{4}{3.28} = 1.22$$

From the standard normal table, **P(Z ≤ 1.22) ≈ 0.8888** or **88.88%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** There is an **88.88% probability** that the project will be completed within 42 days. This is highly acceptable for academic submissions.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Forward & Backward Pass (CPM)

For a project with **parallel branches** (more realistic for larger DBMS projects), the CPM method is used. Consider the following task network:

| Task | Predecessor | Duration (D) |
|------|-------------|---------------|
| A | — | 5 |
| B | — | 7 |
| C | A | 6 |
| D | B | 4 |
| E | C | 8 |
| F | D | 5 |
| G | E, F | 3 |

### Forward Pass (Computing ES and EF)

**Step 1:** For tasks with no predecessor (A, B), ES = 0.

$$EF(A) = ES(A) + D(A) = 0 + 5 = 5$$
$$EF(B) = ES(B) + D(B) = 0 + 7 = 7$$

**Step 2:** For task C (predecessor = A):

$$ES(C) = EF(A) = 5$$
$$EF(C) = 5 + 6 = 11$$

**Step 3:** For task D (predecessor = B):

$$ES(D) = EF(B) = 7$$
$$EF(D) = 7 + 4 = 11$$

**Step 4:** For task E (predecessor = C):

$$ES(E) = EF(C) = 11$$
$$EF(E) = 11 + 8 = 19$$

**Step 5:** For task F (predecessor = D):

$$ES(F) = EF(D) = 11$$
$$EF(F) = 11 + 5 = 16$$

**Step 6:** For task G (predecessors = E, F). ES = max(EF(E), EF(F)):

$$ES(G) = \max(19, 16) = 19$$
$$EF(G) = 19 + 3 = 22$$

**Project Duration = EF(G) = 22 days**

### Backward Pass (Computing LS and LF)

The final task G has LF(G) = EF(G) = 22.

$$LS(G) = LF(G) - D(G) = 22 - 3 = 19$$

**For F:** $LF(F) = LS(G) = 19$, so $LS(F) = 19 - 5 = 14$

**For E:** $LF(E) = LS(G) = 19$, so $LS(E) = 19 - 8 = 11$

**For D:** $LF(D) = LS(F) = 14$, so $LS(D) = 14 - 4 = 10$

**For C:** $LF(C) = LS(E) = 11$, so $LS(C) = 11 - 6 = 5$

**For A:** $LF(A) = LS(C) = 5$, so $LS(A) = 5 - 5 = 0$

**For B:** $LF(B) = LS(D) = 10$, so $LS(B) = 10 - 7 = 3$

### Slack Computation & Critical Path Identification

| Task | ES | EF | LS | LF | Slack (LS−ES) | Critical? |
|------|----|----|----|----|----------------|-----------|
| A | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| B | 0 | 7 | 3 | 10 | 3 | ❌ No |
| C | 5 | 11 | 5 | 11 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| D | 7 | 11 | 10 | 14 | 3 | ❌ No |
| E | 11 | 19 | 11 | 19 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| F | 11 | 16 | 14 | 19 | 3 | ❌ No |
| G | 19 | 22 | 19 | 22 | 0 | ✅ Yes |

**Critical Path: A → C → E → G** with total duration **22 days**.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: Define a "Milestone" in the context of a DBMS course project. List any three major milestones you would define for a typical DBMS mini-project.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **milestone** is a significant point or event in a project schedule that has **zero duration** and marks the completion of a major deliverable or phase. Milestones serve as checkpoints to track progress and validate deliverables before proceeding to the next phase.

For a typical DBMS mini-project (e.g., *Hospital Management System*), three major milestones are:

1. **M1 – SRS Document Approval:** Marks the end of the requirement gathering phase. The Software Requirements Specification is reviewed and approved by the project guide.
2. **M2 – ER Diagram Freeze:** Marks the end of conceptual design. The ER model is validated, and no further structural changes are permitted without formal change requests.
3. **M3 – Normalized Database in 3NF/BCNF:** Marks the completion of logical design and refinement. The schema is now ready for SQL implementation.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award **1 mark** for the definition and **2 marks** for listing the milestones with brief context. Always mention *zero duration* — it is a keyword.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: What is a Gantt Chart? How is it useful in tracking the progress of a DBMS project?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Gantt Chart** is a horizontal bar chart developed by Henry Gantt (1917) that visually represents a project schedule. Each task is represented as a **bar** whose length corresponds to its **duration**, plotted against a **time axis**. Tasks can be sequenced, overlapped, or shown as parallel depending on their dependencies.

**Utility in DBMS project tracking:**

1. **Visual Timeline:** The student and guide can instantly see which phase is in progress (e.g., "Currently in Phase 3: Schema Mapping") and what is upcoming.
2. **Dependency Tracking:** Predecessor-successor relationships (e.g., Normalization depends on Schema Mapping) are clearly displayed.
3. **Progress Monitoring:** Completed tasks can be shaded, and the **% completion** can be tracked weekly during review meetings with the project coordinator.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse Gantt Charts with PERT/CPM networks. Remember: **Gantt = Time-based bar chart**, **PERT = Node-based network diagram with probabilistic estimates**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**A DBMS mini-project for a "Library Management System" has been decomposed into 8 tasks (A–H) with the following three-point time estimates (in days):**

| Task | Description | Predecessor | Optimistic (O) | Most Likely (M) | Pessimistic (P) |
|------|-------------|-------------|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| A | Requirement Analysis | — | 3 | 5 | 9 |
| B | ER Diagram Design | A | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| C | Schema Conversion | B | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| D | DBMS Software Setup | A | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| E | Normalization | C | 3 | 5 | 7 |
| F | SQL Implementation | E, D | 4 | 6 | 8 |
| G | Front-End Development | F | 5 | 7 | 9 |
| H | Testing and Documentation | G | 3 | 4 | 11 |

**(a)** Compute the expected time $T_E$ and variance $\sigma^2$ for each task. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Identify the critical path, compute the expected project duration, and the probability of completing the project in **30 days or less**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute Expected Time using $T_E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$**

$$T_E(A) = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 9}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 9}{6} = \frac{32}{6} = 5.33 \text{ days}$$

$$T_E(B) = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{2 + 16 + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4.00 \text{ days}$$

$$T_E(C) = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 4}{6} = \frac{2 + 12 + 4}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3.00 \text{ days}$$

$$T_E(D) = \frac{1 + 4(2) + 3}{6} = \frac{1 + 8 + 3}{6} = \frac{12}{6} = 2.00 \text{ days}$$

$$T_E(E) = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5.00 \text{ days}$$

$$T_E(F) = \frac{4 + 4(6) + 8}{6} = \frac{4 + 24 + 8}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6.00 \text{ days}$$

$$T_E(G) = \frac{5 + 4(7) + 9}{6} = \frac{5 + 28 + 9}{6} = \frac{42}{6} = 7.00 \text{ days}$$

$$T_E(H) = \frac{3 + 4(4) + 11}{6} = \frac{3 + 16 + 11}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5.00 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2: Compute Variance using $\sigma^2 = \left( \frac{P - O}{6} \right)^2$**

$$\sigma^2(A) = \left( \frac{9 - 3}{6} \right)^2 = (1.00)^2 = 1.00 \text{ days}^2$$

$$\sigma^2(B) = \left( \frac{6 - 2}{6} \right)^2 = (0.667)^2 = 0.44 \text{ days}^2$$

$$\sigma^2(C) = \left( \frac{4 - 2}{6} \right)^2 = (0.333)^2 = 0.11 \text{ days}^2$$

$$\sigma^2(D) = \left( \frac{3 - 1}{6} \right)^2 = (0.333)^2 = 0.11 \text{ days}^2$$

$$\sigma^2(E) = \left( \frac{7 - 3}{6} \right)^2 = (0.667)^2 = 0.44 \text{ days}^2$$

$$\sigma^2(F) = \left( \frac{8 - 4}{6} \right)^2 = (0.667)^2 = 0.44 \text{ days}^2$$

$$\sigma^2(G) = \left( \frac{9 - 5}{6} \right)^2 = (0.667)^2 = 0.44 \text{ days}^2$$

$$\sigma^2(H) = \left( \frac{11 - 3}{6} \right)^2 = (1.333)^2 = 1.78 \text{ days}^2$$

| Task | O | M | P | TE (days) | σ² (days²) |
|------|---|---|---|-----------|-------------|
| A | 3 | 5 | 9 | 5.33 | 1.00 |
| B | 2 | 4 | 6 | 4.00 | 0.44 |
| C | 2 | 3 | 4 | 3.00 | 0.11 |
| D | 1 | 2 | 3 | 2.00 | 0.11 |
| E | 3 | 5 | 7 | 5.00 | 0.44 |
| F | 4 | 6 | 8 | 6.00 | 0.44 |
| G | 5 | 7 | 9 | 7.00 | 0.44 |
| H | 3 | 4 | 11 | 5.00 | 1.78 |

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Enumerate all possible paths from START to END**

From the precedence diagram:
- **Path 1:** A → B → C → E → F → G → H
- **Path 2:** A → D → F → G → H

**Step 2: Compute total duration for each path**

$$\text{Path 1: } 5.33 + 4.00 + 3.00 + 5.00 + 6.00 + 7.00 + 5.00 = 35.33 \text{ days}$$

$$\text{Path 2: } 5.33 + 2.00 + 6.00 + 7.00 + 5.00 = 25.33 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3: Identify the Critical Path**

The critical path is the path with the **maximum duration**:
$$\boxed{\text{Critical Path: } A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow G \rightarrow H \text{ (35.33 days)}}$$

**Step 4: Compute Project Standard Deviation**

Sum the variances of the critical path tasks:
$$\sigma_{project}^2 = 1.00 + 0.44 + 0.11 + 0.44 + 0.44 + 0.44 + 1.78 = 4.65 \text{ days}^2$$

$$\sigma_{project} = \sqrt{4.65} = 2.156 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5: Compute Probability of Completion in 30 Days**

$$Z = \frac{T_S - T_E}{\sigma_{project}} = \frac{30 - 35.33}{2.156} = \frac{-5.33}{2.156} = -2.47$$

From the standard normal distribution table:
$$P(Z \leq -2.47) \approx 0.0068 \text{ or } 0.68\%$$

**Interpretation:** There is only a **0.68% probability** of completing the project in 30 days. The student should either request a deadline extension or **add resources** to the critical path tasks (especially A and H, which have the highest variance).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Define a **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)**. Draw a hierarchical WBS for a DBMS mini-project on *"Online Exam Management System"* showing at least **three levels of decomposition** (7 marks).

**(b)** Explain the difference between **PERT** and **CPM** scheduling methods. State which method is more suitable for a DBMS course project and justify your answer with at least three technical reasons (7 marks).

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** A **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** is a hierarchical decomposition of the total scope of work to be carried out by the project team to accomplish the project objectives and produce the required deliverables. It organizes and defines the total scope of the project, breaking it into smaller, more manageable components called **work packages**.

**WBS Diagram for "Online Exam Management System" (5 marks):**

```
LEVEL 0: Online Exam Management System (DBMS Project)
│
├── LEVEL 1.0: Project Management
│   ├── 1.1 Project Proposal
│   ├── 1.2 Timeline and Milestones
│   ├── 1.3 Progress Reports
│   └── 1.4 Risk Register
│
├── LEVEL 2.0: Requirements and Design
│   ├── 2.1 SRS Document
│   ├── 2.2 Use Case Diagrams
│   ├── 2.3 ER Diagram
│   │   ├── 2.3.1 Entities (Student, Exam, Question, Subject, Result)
│   │   ├── 2.3.2 Attributes and Keys
│   │   └── 2.3.3 Relationships (Attempts, Belongs_to, Evaluates)
│   └── 2.4 Relational Schema
│       ├── 2.4.1 Table Definitions
│       └── 2.4.2 Constraints (PK, FK, NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
│
├── LEVEL 3.0: Database Implementation
│   ├── 3.1 DDL Scripts (CREATE TABLE)
│   ├── 3.2 DML Scripts (INSERT sample data)
│   ├── 3.3 Complex Queries
│   │   ├── 3.3.1 JOINs (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT)
│   │   ├── 3.3.2 Subqueries
│   │   ├── 3.3.3 Aggregate Functions (AVG, COUNT, MAX)
│   │   └── 3.3.4 Views and Indexes
│   └── 3.4 Stored Procedures (Triggers, Cursors)
│
├── LEVEL 4.0: Front-End Development
│   ├── 4.1 UI Mockups
│   ├── 4.2 Login and Authentication Module
│   ├── 4.3 Exam Interface Module
│   └── 4.4 Result Display Module
│
└── LEVEL 5.0: Testing and Deployment
    ├── 5.1 Unit Testing
    ├── 5.2 Integration Testing
    ├── 5.3 User Acceptance Testing (UAT)
    └── 5.4 Final Documentation
```

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

| Aspect | PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) | CPM (Critical Path Method) |
|--------|-----------------------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Origin** | Developed by U.S. Navy (1958) for Polaris Missile Project | Developed by DuPont & Remington Rand (1956) |
| **Time Estimation** | Uses **three-point estimates** (Optimistic, Most Likely, Pessimistic) | Uses **single deterministic estimate** |
| **Uncertainty** | Probabilistic; assumes Beta distribution | Deterministic; assumes known durations |
| **Focus** | **Time-oriented** — focuses on meeting deadlines with uncertainty | **Time-Cost trade-off** — focuses on optimizing both |
| **Best For** | R&D, novel projects with high uncertainty | Repetitive, well-understood projects |
| **Network** | Event-oriented (nodes = events) | Activity-oriented (nodes = activities, arrows = dependencies) |

**Suitability for DBMS Course Project (3 marks for justification):**

The **PERT method is more suitable** for a DBMS course project for the following three technical reasons:

1. **Uncertainty in Implementation:** Tasks like "User Testing" and "Front-End Integration" have highly variable durations because they depend on student learning speed, debugging time, and unforeseen bugs. PERT's three-point estimation captures this uncertainty more realistically than CPM's single-point estimate.

2. **Probabilistic Completion Analysis:** PERT allows the student to compute the **probability of meeting a submission deadline** (e.g., "What is the probability I finish in 45 days?"). This is a powerful tool for academic planning and for reassuring the project guide that the deadline is achievable with high confidence (e.g., 90%).

3. **Heterogeneous Task Nature:** A DBMS project includes both **deterministic tasks** (e.g., writing DDL scripts — well-defined duration) and **stochastic tasks** (e.g., debugging complex JOIN queries — highly variable). PERT handles both naturally using the O, M, P estimates, whereas CPM would force a single, possibly inaccurate, estimate on the stochastic tasks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse the PERT variance formula with standard deviation. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you write $\sigma = \frac{P-O}{6}$ when asked for variance.
> 2. **Do NOT** forget to identify the **critical path explicitly**. Many students compute durations of all paths but never state *which one is critical*. This costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Do NOT** use negative slack values in the CPM table. Slack must always be **non-negative**. A negative slack indicates an infeasible schedule.
> 4. **Do NOT** forget the unit. The Z-score is dimensionless, but $T_E$ is in days, and $\sigma^2$ is in days². Mentioning units shows professional rigor.
> 5. **Do NOT** skip the standard normal table lookup. Examiners expect the numerical value (e.g., $P(Z \le -2.47) = 0.0068$) and the percentage interpretation.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Project Timeline** is a chronological plan that defines **what** tasks will be done, **when** they will be done, **by whom**, and **what marks their completion**.
- ✅ A **Milestone** has **zero duration** and marks the **end of a major deliverable**. Examples: SRS Approval, ER Freeze, 3NF Schema, SQL Demo, Final Submission.
- ✅ A **Gantt Chart** is a **time-based bar chart** ideal for visual tracking by both students and guides.
- ✅ A **PERT Network** is a **node-based diagram** ideal for computing expected project duration under uncertainty.
- ✅ The **PERT Expected Time formula** is $T_E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$, derived from the Beta distribution assumption.
- ✅ The **PERT Variance formula** is $\sigma^2 = \left( \frac{P - O}{6} \right)^2$.
- ✅ The **Critical Path** is the **longest path** in the network and determines the **minimum project duration**.
- ✅ **CPM** uses the **Forward Pass** (ES, EF) and **Backward Pass** (LS, LF) to compute **slack** for each task.
- ✅ **Slack = LS − ES**. A task with **zero slack** is on the **critical path**.
- ✅ The **probability of project completion** by a given deadline $T_S$ is found using the **Z-score**: $Z = \frac{T_S - T_E}{\sigma_{project}}$, then looking up the **standard normal distribution table**.
- ✅ For a DBMS course project, define **at least 6 milestones** (M1 to M6) corresponding to each SDLC phase.
- ✅ Always include a **Gantt Chart** in your project proposal — it is **mandatory** in the KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation rubric.
- ✅ Use **three-point estimates** (O, M, P) for tasks with uncertainty, and **single-point estimates** for well-defined tasks.
- ✅ Document your timeline in **Microsoft Project, ClickUp, Notion, or even a well-formatted Mermaid Gantt chart** (as demonstrated in Section 4.2).
- ✅ **Review your timeline bi-weekly** with your project guide. Any slippage on the critical path requires immediate corrective action (adding manpower, parallelizing tasks, or scope reduction).
- ✅ **Always state units** (days, weeks) explicitly in your timeline. Ambiguity in unit is a common reason for mark deduction.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Cram Sheet:** Memorize the four key formulas — $T_E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$, $\sigma^2 = \left( \frac{P - O}{6} \right)^2$, $Slack = LS - ES$, and $Z = \frac{T_S - T_E}{\sigma}$. These four formulas cover **~80%** of all KTU 2024 scheme exam questions on this topic. Practice the Z-score → Probability lookup from the standard normal table at least 3 times before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constraints: Entity Integrity, Referential Integrity, and Domain Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT304 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Constraints: Entity Integrity, Referential Integrity, and Domain Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Integrity Constraints?

**Integrity Constraints** are a set of declarative rules enforced on the data columns of a relational database table. They are the silent guardians of the database — they ensure that the data stored is **accurate, consistent, valid, and reliable** across all instances and over time. Without integrity constraints, a database degenerates from a trusted source of information into a collection of unverified, corrupted, and unreliable records.

In the context of the **Relational Model (Codd's Model)**, integrity constraints are mathematically derived restrictions on the relations (tables) that prevent the insertion of data that would violate the semantic rules of the application domain. The SQL standard (and therefore KTU examinations) categorizes these into several key classes, but for Module 3, we focus on the three foundational pillars:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Primary Integrity Constraints (KTU Module 3 Syllabus)**
> 1. **Entity Integrity Constraint** — Governs the uniqueness and non-null status of the Primary Key.
> 2. **Referential Integrity Constraint** — Governs the relationship consistency between Foreign Key and referenced Primary Key.
> 3. **Domain Constraint** — Governs the permissible values within a column (data type, range, format).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The College Admission Office

Imagine a college admission office that maintains two registers:
- **Register A (Students)**: Contains the *Roll Number*, Name, DOB, and Course ID of every student.
- **Register B (Courses)**: Contains the *Course ID*, Course Name, and Credits of every course offered.

Now, the admission officer must enforce some basic *rules of business*:
1. A student **must** have a roll number, and **no two students** can have the same roll number. *(This is Entity Integrity.)*
2. If a student is registered for *Course ID "CS304"*, that course **must** exist in Register B. You cannot enroll a student in a "ghost" course that nobody offers. *(This is Referential Integrity.)*
3. The *DOB* column should only contain valid dates (not "Hello World" or "99/99/9999"), the *Credits* column should be an integer between 1 and 5, and the *Gender* column should only contain "M" or "F". *(This is Domain Integrity.)*

If any of these rules is violated, the admission is **rejected outright**. The database system behaves in exactly the same way — it is a strict, rule-enforcing machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are these called "Constraints"?** Because they *constrain* (restrict) the set of valid tuples (rows) that a relation is allowed to contain. In formal relational algebra, they act as a filter: $\sigma_{\text{constraint-true}}(R)$.

---

## 1.3 Formal Definitions

### 1.3.1 Entity Integrity Constraint
**Definition:** The **Entity Integrity Constraint** states that the **Primary Key** attribute of any relation (table) **must always have a non-NULL value** for every tuple (row), and the value must be **unique**.

**Formal Statement:** For any relation $R$ with primary key $PK$, for every tuple $t \in R$:
$$t[PK] \neq \text{NULL} \quad \land \quad \forall t_i, t_j \in R, \; t_i \neq t_j \Rightarrow t_i[PK] \neq t_j[PK]$$

**Intuition:** Every real-world entity (a student, a product, a transaction) must be **distinguishable** from every other entity. If the primary key were NULL, the entity would be "nameless" and impossible to identify. If two rows had the same PK, they would represent the same entity — a contradiction.

### 1.3.2 Referential Integrity Constraint
**Definition:** The **Referential Integrity Constraint** states that for every **Foreign Key** value in a relation (the *referencing* or *child* relation), there must exist a matching **Primary Key** value in the referenced (or *parent*) relation — *unless* the foreign key is NULL.

**Formal Statement:** For relations $R_1$ (referencing) and $R_2$ (referenced), where $FK$ in $R_1$ references $PK$ in $R_2$, for every tuple $t_1 \in R_1$:
$$t_1[FK] = \text{NULL} \quad \lor \quad \exists t_2 \in R_2 \text{ such that } t_1[FK] = t_2[PK]$$

**Intuition:** A "reference" must point to something real. You cannot write a cheque (cheque number 101) that references bank account number 99999 if no such account exists. The database forbids **dangling references**.

### 1.3.3 Domain Constraint
**Definition:** The **Domain Constraint** states that every value of an attribute (column) in a relation must be an **atomic value drawn from the specified domain** — including the data type, length, range, and format.

**Formal Statement:** For a relation $R$ with attribute $A$ defined on domain $D$:
$$\forall t \in R, \; t[A] \in D$$

**Intuition:** An attribute is like a labeled box. The label (domain) tells you *what kind of object* is allowed inside. You cannot put a *string* into a box labeled "INTEGER". You cannot put the *age* "500" into a box labeled "AGE (0–150)".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Examiners often ask students to *classify* a given constraint (e.g., NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK, FOREIGN KEY) into one of the three categories. Memorize the mapping:
> - **Entity Integrity** → `PRIMARY KEY` (includes NOT NULL + UNIQUE)
> - **Referential Integrity** → `FOREIGN KEY ... REFERENCES`
> - **Domain Integrity** → `CHECK`, `NOT NULL` (on non-key), data type, `DEFAULT`

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Entity Integrity — The "Identity Guarantee"

The Entity Integrity Constraint is a **tuple-level** constraint that applies specifically to the attribute(s) designated as the **Primary Key** of a relation. It is a composite of two sub-constraints:

1. **Uniqueness Constraint:** $\forall t_i, t_j \in R, \; i \neq j \Rightarrow t_i[PK] \neq t_j[PK]$
2. **Non-Null Constraint:** $\forall t \in R, \; t[PK] \neq \text{NULL}$

Both conditions must be satisfied **simultaneously**. A common exam pitfall is stating that the primary key must be unique *or* non-null — it must be **both** (logical AND).

### Why Primary Key Cannot Be NULL
The Primary Key serves as the **logical address** of a tuple within the relation. If it were NULL, the tuple would be:
- **Invisible** to JOIN operations referencing it.
- **Unidentifiable** in UPDATE and DELETE operations (`WHERE PK = NULL` matches nothing in SQL's three-valued logic).
- **In violation** of the very definition of a "key" in set theory (a key is a set of attributes that *uniquely determines* the tuple; NULL cannot "determine" anything).

### SQL Implementation
The Entity Integrity is enforced by declaring the column as `PRIMARY KEY` at table creation time:

```sql
CREATE TABLE Student (
    Roll_No   INT  PRIMARY KEY,
    Name      VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    DOB       DATE
);
```

> [!NOTE]
> In SQL, declaring a column `PRIMARY KEY` **automatically** enforces both NOT NULL and UNIQUE. This is a key KTU fact — you do not need to write `PRIMARY KEY NOT NULL UNIQUE` separately.

### Composite Primary Keys
When a primary key consists of multiple attributes, the Entity Integrity Constraint applies to the **entire combination**, not to individual attributes. Individual attributes within the composite key *may* be NULL (e.g., in a weak entity relationship), but their combination cannot be NULL or duplicate.

---

## 2.2 Referential Integrity — The "Consistency of Links"

Referential Integrity governs the relationship between two tables connected by a **Foreign Key**. It guarantees that the database never contains a **dangling reference** — a foreign key value that points to a non-existent primary key.

### Components of the Constraint

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $R_1$ | Referencing (Child) relation |
| $R_2$ | Referenced (Parent) relation |
| $FK$ | Foreign Key attribute set in $R_1$ |
| $PK$ | Primary Key attribute set in $R_2$ |
| $t_1 \in R_1$ | An arbitrary tuple in the child table |
| $t_2 \in R_2$ | A tuple in the parent table |

### The Three Legitimate States of a Foreign Key
1. **NULL** — The reference is *optional*. (e.g., A project may not have a manager yet.)
2. **Matches a PK in the parent table** — The reference is *valid*.
3. **(Implicitly illegal)** Any other value is rejected by the DBMS.

### SQL Implementation

```sql
CREATE TABLE Enrollment (
    Enroll_ID   INT  PRIMARY KEY,
    Roll_No     INT,
    Course_ID   VARCHAR(10),
    FOREIGN KEY (Roll_No)   REFERENCES Student(Roll_No),
    FOREIGN KEY (Course_ID) REFERENCES Course(Course_ID)
);
```

### Referential Triggered Actions (Crucial for KTU 14-Mark Questions)
When the referenced primary key is **updated or deleted**, the DBMS must decide what to do with the dependent foreign keys. The SQL standard defines four actions:

| Action | Behavior on Parent Update/Delete |
|---|---|
| `CASCADE` | Automatically update/delete the child rows |
| `RESTRICT` (default) | Reject the operation if children exist |
| `SET NULL` | Set the foreign key in children to NULL |
| `SET DEFAULT` | Set the foreign key in children to their DEFAULT value |
| `NO ACTION` | Similar to RESTRICT, checked at end of statement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** When the question asks "What happens when a referenced row is deleted?" — list all four referential actions and explain that the chosen one is declared **at the time of foreign key definition** in the CREATE TABLE statement.

### Violation Scenarios (Frequently Asked)

1. **Insert into child with non-existent parent FK** → Rejected.
2. **Delete from parent while children reference it** → Rejected (or cascaded).
3. **Update parent PK while children reference it** → Rejected (or cascaded/set null).

---

## 2.3 Domain Constraint — The "Type & Range Contract"

Domain Constraints are the **most granular** integrity constraints. They apply to the **attribute level** (single column) and restrict the set of permissible values in three ways:

1. **Data Type:** INT, VARCHAR, DATE, BOOLEAN, DECIMAL, etc.
2. **Format / Pattern:** Using `CHECK` with `LIKE` or `REGEXP`.
3. **Range / Enumeration:** Using `CHECK` with comparison operators or `IN`.

### The `CHECK` Clause — The Workhorse of Domain Integrity

```sql
CREATE TABLE Employee (
    Emp_ID     INT  PRIMARY KEY,
    Salary     DECIMAL(10,2)  CHECK (Salary > 0),
    Age        INT            CHECK (Age BETWEEN 18 AND 65),
    Gender     CHAR(1)        CHECK (Gender IN ('M', 'F', 'O')),
    Email      VARCHAR(100)   CHECK (Email LIKE '%_@_%._%'),
    Dept_Code  INT             DEFAULT 1
);
```

### Components of a Domain

| Component | Description | SQL Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| Data Type | The kind of value (number, string, date) | `INT`, `VARCHAR`, `DATE` |
| Length/Precision | Maximum size | `VARCHAR(50)`, `DECIMAL(10,2)` |
| Default Value | Value when none is provided | `DEFAULT` |
| Allow NULL? | Whether absence is permitted | `NULL` / `NOT NULL` |
| Check Expression | Custom business rule | `CHECK (...)` |
| Enumeration | Allowed discrete values | `CHECK (col IN (...))` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Not NULL on a non-key column** is technically a *Domain Constraint* (it restricts the domain to "all values except NULL"). On a Primary Key, however, NOT NULL is part of *Entity Integrity*. Examiners will deduct marks if you confuse these.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table

| Constraint Type | Enforced By | Acts On | Cardinality | SQL Syntax Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Entity Integrity** | `PRIMARY KEY` declaration | Primary Key attribute(s) | Tuple level | `Roll_No INT PRIMARY KEY` |
| **Referential Integrity** | `FOREIGN KEY ... REFERENCES` | Foreign Key ↔ Parent PK | Inter-relation | `FOREIGN KEY (Dept) REFERENCES Dept(ID)` |
| **Domain Integrity** | Data type, `CHECK`, `NOT NULL`, `DEFAULT`, `UNIQUE` | A single attribute | Attribute level | `CHECK (Age >= 18)` |
| **Key Uniqueness** (subset of Entity) | `UNIQUE` | Candidate key (non-PK) | Tuple level | `Email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE` |
| **Nullability** (subset of Domain) | `NULL` / `NOT NULL` | Any column | Attribute level | `Name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL` |

### Cardinality Rule for Referential Integrity

For a child table with $n$ tuples and a parent table with $m$ tuples, the valid referential integrity relationship is:

$$|\pi_{FK}(R_1)| \leq |R_2|$$

That is, the number of distinct foreign key values in the child cannot exceed the number of parent tuples (assuming FKs are not NULL). NULLs in the FK are allowed but excluded from the projection above.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These three constraints underpin virtually every enterprise system:

- **Banking Systems:** Entity Integrity ensures no two transactions share the same Transaction ID. Referential Integrity ensures every account number in the "Loans" table exists in the "Accounts" table. Domain Integrity ensures loan amounts are positive and within the sanctioned range.

- **Hospital Management Systems:** Patient ID is unique (Entity), every doctor referenced in "Appointments" exists in the "Doctors" table (Referential), and blood group is restricted to {A+, A-, B+, B-, O+, O-, AB+, AB-} (Domain).

- **E-Commerce Platforms:** Order ID is unique (Entity), every Customer_ID in orders exists in the customer table (Referential), and product price must be > 0 and stock must be a non-negative integer (Domain).

- **IoT and Sensor Networks:** Sensor ID uniqueness, valid device pairing references, and physical-range constraints (e.g., temperature between -40°C and 125°C for industrial sensors).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Library Management System
