| **S-Lock** | Granted | Granted | Blocked |
| **X-Lock** | Granted | Blocked | Blocked |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks]** — Locking-Based Protocols

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** protocol. Discuss the limitations of basic 2PL and how **Strict 2PL** and **Conservative 2PL** overcome them. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following schedule $S$ of three transactions. Determine whether the schedule is **2PL-compliant**. If not, modify it to make it 2PL-compliant. **(7 Marks)**

$$S:\ r_1(A),\ r_2(B),\ w_1(A),\ r_3(A),\ w_2(B),\ w_3(A),\ c_1,\ c_2,\ c_3$$

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation of 2PL and its variants:**

The **Two-Phase Locking Protocol** requires every transaction to issue lock and unlock requests in two distinct phases:
1. **Growing Phase:** Only `lock-S` and `lock-X` operations are allowed.
2. **Shrinking Phase:** Only `unlock` operations are allowed.

**Lock Point** = moment when the last lock is acquired (transition point).

**Limitations of Basic 2PL:**
- Does not prevent **cascading rollbacks** (a problem where a transaction reads data written by an uncommitted transaction, and then both must roll back).
- Does not guarantee **recoverability**.
- Suffers from **deadlocks**.

**Conservative 2PL (Pre-declaration 2PL):**
- Transaction acquires **ALL** required locks **before** it begins executing.
- **Advantage:** Deadlock-free.
- **Disadvantage:** Often impractical; lock-acquisition may be impossible in advance.

**Strict 2PL:**
- All **exclusive (X) locks are held until the transaction commits or aborts**.
- **Advantage:** Guarantees both serializability and recoverability; eliminates cascading rollbacks.
- **Disadvantage:** Reduced concurrency; may still suffer deadlocks.

**Rigorous 2PL:**
- Both S and X locks are held until commit/abort.
- **Advantage:** Produces schedules whose serial order is the commit order.

---

**(b) 2PL Analysis of the given schedule:**

Original operations:
$$S:\ r_1(A),\ r_2(B),\ w_1(A),\ r_3(A),\ w_2(B),\ w_3(A),\ c_1,\ c_2,\ c_3$$

Let us annotate this with explicit lock and unlock operations following 2PL rules.

**Augmented schedule $S'$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S' :\ & \text{lock-S}_1(A),\ r_1(A), \\
     & \text{lock-S}_2(B),\ r_2(B), \\
     & \text{lock-X}_1(A),\ w_1(A), \\
     & \text{lock-S}_3(A),\ r_3(A), \\
     & \text{lock-X}_2(B),\ w_2(B), \\
     & \text{lock-X}_3(A),\ w_3(A), \\
     & \text{unlock}_1(A),\ c_1, \\
     & \text{unlock}_2(B),\ c_2, \\
     & \text{unlock}_3(A),\ c_3
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification for each transaction:**

| Transaction | Locks Acquired (in order) | Unlocks Begin After | 2PL? |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | S(A), X(A) | Both acquired before unlock | ✅ Yes |
| T2 | S(B), X(B) | Both acquired before unlock | ✅ Yes |
| T3 | S(A), X(A) | Both acquired before unlock | ✅ Yes |

**Conclusion:** The schedule $S$ **is 2PL-compliant** as no transaction releases a lock and then acquires a new one. It is therefore **conflict-serializable**.

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks]** — Timestamp-Based Protocols

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Timestamp Ordering (TSO)** algorithm for concurrency control. Describe how the **Read Timestamp** and **Write Timestamp** of a data item are maintained and used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following schedule with timestamps $TS(T_1)=10$, $TS(T_2)=20$, $TS(T_3)=15$. Apply **Basic TSO** and determine the order of operations. Identify any transactions that need to be **aborted and restarted**. Use the initial values: $R\text{-}TS(X)=R\text{-}TS(Y)=0$, $W\text{-}TS(X)=W\text{-}TS(Y)=0$. **(7 Marks)**

$$S:\ r_1(X),\ w_2(X),\ r_3(Y),\ w_3(X),\ w_1(Y),\ r_2(Y)$$

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation of TSO Algorithm:**

Each transaction $T_i$ is assigned a **unique timestamp** $TS(T_i)$ at the start. Two timestamps are maintained per data item $X$:
- **W-TS(X):** Highest timestamp of any transaction that has written $X$.
- **R-TS(X):** Highest timestamp of any transaction that has read $X$.

**Rules for TSO:**

| Operation | Condition for ALLOW | Action if Fails |
|---|---|---|
| `READ(X)` by $T_i$ | $TS(T_i) \ge W\text{-}TS(X)$ | Abort $T_i$, restart with new TS |
| `WRITE(X)` by $T_i$ | $TS(T_i) \ge W\text{-}TS(X)$ **and** $TS(T_i) \ge R\text{-}TS(X)$ | Abort $T_i$, restart with new TS |

**On success:**
- For `READ(X)`: set $R\text{-}TS(X) = \max(R\text{-}TS(X), TS(T_i))$.
- For `WRITE(X)`: set $W\text{-}TS(X) = TS(T_i)$.

**Key Property:** TSO guarantees that the resulting schedule is **conflict-serializable** in the order of timestamps.

---

**(b) Step-by-step TSO Evaluation:**

| Step | Operation | $TS(T_i)$ | Compare With | Decision | Update |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_1(X)$ | 10 | $W\text{-}TS(X)=0$ | $10 \ge 0$ ✅ Allowed | $R\text{-}TS(X) = 10$ |
| 2 | $w_2(X)$ | 20 | $R\text{-}TS(X)=10,\ W\text{-}TS(X)=0$ | $20 \ge 10$ ✅, $20 \ge 0$ ✅ Allowed | $W\text{-}TS(X) = 20$ |
| 3 | $r_3(Y)$ | 15 | $W\text{-}TS(Y)=0$ | $15 \ge 0$ ✅ Allowed | $R\text{-}TS(Y) = 15$ |
| 4 | $w_3(X)$ | 15 | $R\text{-}TS(X)=10,\ W\text{-}TS(X)=20$ | $15 \ge 10$ ✅, $15 \ge 20$ ❌ **ABORT T3** | T3 restarts with new TS |
| 5 | $w_1(Y)$ | 10 | $R\text{-}TS(Y)=15,\ W\text{-}TS(Y)=0$ | $10 \ge 15$ ❌ **ABORT T1** | T1 restarts with new TS |
| 6 | $r_2(Y)$ | 20 | $W\text{-}TS(Y)=0$ | $20 \ge 0$ ✅ Allowed | $R\text{-}TS(Y) = 20$ |

**Result:**
- T3 is **aborted** at step 4 (its write would overwrite a newer write by T2).
- T1 is **aborted** at step 5 (its write would conflict with a read by T3).
- After restart with new timestamps, both may proceed.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Concurrency Control Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing 2PL with Strict 2PL:** Students often write "2PL guarantees recoverability" — this is **WRONG**. Only **Strict 2PL** guarantees recoverability. **Basic 2PL only guarantees conflict-serializability.**
> 2. **Forgetting Lock Upgrade:** When a transaction does `READ` then `WRITE` on the same item, students must show a `lock-S` followed by a `lock-X` (upgrade), not a fresh lock.
> 3. **Mixing up R-TS and W-TS updates:** In TSO, after a **READ**, only R-TS is updated (W-TS is **not** touched). After a **WRITE**, only W-TS is updated.
> 4. **Thomas' Write Rule misapplication:** Thomas' rule is **only** applied to **WRITE** operations that fail because of $W\text{-}TS$, **not** because of $R\text{-}TS$.
> 5. **Forgetting cycle detection in Deadlock:** Always draw a **Wait-For Graph** to show the deadlock cycle. Verbal description alone is insufficient for full marks.
> 6. **Not stating the Lock Point explicitly:** The Lock Point is the **last lock acquired**, not the first lock released.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Concurrency Control** ensures that concurrent transactions leave the database in a **consistent state** equivalent to some serial execution.
- ✅ The **three main problems** of uncontrolled concurrency are: **Lost Update, Dirty Read, Unrepeatable Read**.
- ✅ **Locks** are variables associated with data items; the two basic types are **S-Lock (Shared/Read)** and **X-Lock (Exclusive/Write)**.
- ✅ **S-S = compatible**, **S-X = incompatible**, **X-X = incompatible** — memorize the compatibility matrix.
- ✅ **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** has a **Growing Phase** (locks acquired) and a **Shrinking Phase** (locks released), separated by the **Lock Point**.
- ✅ **Basic 2PL** → conflict-serializable, but **not** recoverable. **Strict 2PL** → both serializable and recoverable.
- ✅ **Conservative 2PL** is deadlock-free but impractical. **Rigorous 2PL** has the strongest guarantees.
- ✅ **Timestamp Ordering (TSO)** assigns each transaction a unique timestamp; operations are allowed only if they respect the **R-TS** and **W-TS** of the data item.
- ✅ In TSO: **`READ` allowed** if $TS(T_i) \ge W\text{-}TS(X)$; **`WRITE` allowed** if $TS(T_i) \ge R\text{-}TS(X)$ **and** $TS(T_i) \ge W\text{-}TS(X)$.
- ✅ **Thomas' Write Rule** is an optimization for TSO: ignore (not abort) a late `WRITE` if it would overwrite a more recent value but is *not* read by anyone newer.
- ✅ **Deadlock** requires **four Coffman conditions**: Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait.
- ✅ **Deadlock Handling Strategies:** Prevention, Detection (Wait-For Graph), Avoidance (Wait-Die / Wound-Wait), Recovery (Victim Selection).
- ✅ A schedule is **conflict-serializable** iff its **Precedence Graph has no cycles**.
- ✅ **2PL is a sufficient but not necessary** condition for conflict-serializability.
- ✅ Commercial systems (Oracle, MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL) primarily use **Variants of 2PL** for concurrency control.
- ✅ **Multiversion CC** and **Optimistic (Validation) CC** are alternative techniques where reads do not block writes and vice versa.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backup and Recovery: Strategies for database backup and recovery in case of failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT304 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Backup and Recovery: Strategies for database backup and recovery in case of failures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Database Backup and Recovery** refers to the set of procedures, strategies, and protocols implemented by a Database Management System (DBMS) to protect data from loss, corruption, or inconsistency arising from various types of failures, and to restore the database to a consistent and correct state after such failures occur.

According to the KTU 2024 DBMS syllabus, backup and recovery mechanisms form the cornerstone of the **Transaction Support** and **Concurrency Control** sub-modules, ensuring the **ACID properties** — particularly **Durability** and **Consistency** — are preserved under all operational conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The 2024 scheme mandates that students must understand:
> 1. Different **types of failures** (transaction, system, media, disaster).
> 2. **Backup strategies** (full, incremental, differential).
> 3. **Recovery techniques** (log-based recovery, shadow paging, ARIES).
> 4. The **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)** protocol.
> 5. **Checkpointing** mechanisms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **scribbling journalist** writing a long, multi-page investigative report on your laptop.

- **Backup** is like photocopying your entire report at regular intervals (daily, weekly). If your laptop is stolen, you can retrieve the most recent photocopy.
- **Recovery** is the process of restoring from the latest photocopy *and* rewriting any missing pages using your **scribble notes** (transaction logs) that you kept side-by-side.
- The **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)** protocol is your personal rule: *"Always write your scribble notes BEFORE writing on the actual report page."* This ensures that if the laptop crashes mid-paragraph, you can reconstruct exactly what you were typing.
- **Checkpoints** are the moments when you stop, photocopy the current report, and mark your scribble notebook with a "✓" — saying "everything before this point is safely saved."

In DBMS terms, the *photocopy* = **backup**, the *scribble notes* = **log file**, and the *✓ moment* = **checkpoint**.

## 1.3 Why is Backup and Recovery Important?

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Database Failure:**
> A database failure is any event that causes the loss or inaccessibility of data or disrupts the normal operation of the DBMS. The fundamental goal of recovery is to ensure that **no committed transaction is lost** and **no uncommitted transaction's effects persist** in the database.

**Key Engineering Metrics in Recovery (industry-standard benchmarks):**
- **Recovery Time Objective (RTO)**: Maximum acceptable downtime, typically measured in **minutes** (e.g., 15 minutes for critical systems).
- **Recovery Point Objective (RPO)**: Maximum acceptable data loss, typically measured in **minutes** (e.g., 5 minutes of data loss tolerable).
- **Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR)**: Average time to restore service after a failure.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**: Average time interval between consecutive system failures.
- **Log Retention Period**: Standard industry value is **30 days to 7 years** depending on compliance (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA).

## 1.4 Types of Failures in DBMS

A robust KTU-level understanding requires classification of all possible failures. Below is the canonical failure taxonomy used in KTU board examinations:

| Failure Type | Cause | Impact Scope | Recovery Approach |
|--------------|-------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Transaction Failure** | Logical error (bad input), system error (deadlock) | Single transaction | Undo the transaction |
| **System Failure (Crash)** | Power outage, OS crash, software bug | All in-flight transactions, DB intact on disk | Restart + recovery from log |
| **Media Failure** | Disk head crash, bad sectors, controller failure | Portion or all of disk | Restore from backup + redo from log |
| **Natural Disaster** | Fire, flood, earthquake | Entire data center | Disaster recovery site + archived backup |
| **Human Error** | Accidental DELETE/UPDATE, wrong script execution | Variable | Point-in-time recovery (PITR) |
| **Network Failure** | Connectivity loss in distributed DB | Remote nodes | Distributed recovery protocol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:**
> Examiners frequently ask: *"Classify the different types of failures that can occur in a DBMS."* Always present this as a **3-tier classification** (Transaction → System → Media/Disaster) for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Storage Hierarchy and Its Role in Recovery

To understand recovery, you must first understand **where** data resides. The DBMS uses a **memory hierarchy** with vastly different speeds and volatility:

```
Fastest, Volatile (RAM)
   ↓
   Database Buffer Pool (Main Memory Cache)
   ↓
   Log Buffer (WAL buffer)
   ↓
Slowest, Non-Volatile (Disk)
   ↓
   Database Files (.dbf, .mdf, .ibd)
   ↓
   Log Files (Write-Ahead Log on disk)
   ↓
   Archived Logs / Backup Tapes
```

**Key Principle:** When the system crashes, everything in **RAM is lost**, but everything on **disk survives** (unless it's a media failure).

## 2.2 Write-Ahead Log (WAL) Protocol — The Golden Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Write-Ahead Log (WAL) Rule:**
> Before any modification is made to a database item on disk, the **log record** describing that modification **MUST** be written to stable (non-volatile) storage.
> 
> Formally: *A log record for transaction T must be flushed to disk BEFORE T modifies any database element on disk.*

**Why is WAL necessary?**
Without WAL, a crash could leave the database modified but the log missing the record of that modification — making recovery impossible.

## 2.3 Log Record Structure

Each log entry contains standardized fields used by every major DBMS:

$$
\text{LogRecord} = \langle \text{LSN}, \text{TID}, \text{PageID}, \text{OldValue}, \text{NewValue}, \text{PrevLSN} \rangle
$$

Where:
- **LSN (Log Sequence Number)**: Unique monotonically increasing identifier for each log record.
- **TID (Transaction ID)**: Identifier of the transaction that wrote the log record.
- **PageID**: Identifier of the affected database page.
- **OldValue** and **NewValue**: The before-image and after-image of the change.
- **PrevLSN**: Pointer to the previous log record of the same transaction (used for fast undo traversal).

## 2.4 Types of Log Records (KTU High-Yield)

| Log Record Type | Format | Purpose |
|-----------------|--------|---------|
| **Update Log** | `<TID, X, OldVal, NewVal>` | Records changes to data items |
| **Commit Log** | `<TID, COMMIT>` | Marks transaction as committed |
| **Abort Log** | `<TID, ABORT>` | Marks transaction as aborted |
| **Begin Log** | `<TID, BEGIN>` | Marks start of transaction |
| **Checkpoint Log** | `<Checkpoint, ActiveTIDs>` | Snapshot of active transactions |
| **CLR (Compensating Log Record)** | `<TID, X, NewVal, PrevLSN, UndoNextLSN>` | Used in ARIES for redoing undo operations |
| **End Log** | `<TID, END>` | Marks transaction completion (post-recovery) |

## 2.5 Checkpointing Mechanism

A **checkpoint** is a synchronization point in the log that establishes a known good state. During checkpointing:

1. The DBMS halts new transaction starts temporarily.
2. All log records in the main memory buffer are flushed to disk.
3. A `<CHECKPOINT>` record is written to the log, listing all currently active transactions.
4. Modified database buffers are forced to disk (optional, depending on policy).
5. The `<CHECKPOINT>` record is flushed to disk.

**Types of Checkpoints:**
- **Consistent (Fuzzy) Checkpoint**: ARIES-style, allows transactions to continue during checkpointing.
- **Sharp Checkpoint**: Blocks all activity until checkpoint completes.
- **Incremental Checkpoint**: Only dirty pages are written, reducing I/O overhead.

## 2.6 Recovery Strategies Overview (KTU Formula Sheet)

| Strategy | Description | When Used | Pros | Cons |
|----------|-------------|-----------|------|------|
| **Deferred Update** | All updates written to log first, applied to DB only after commit | Crash recovery | Simpler, no undo needed | Cannot provide updated data before commit |
| **Immediate Update** | Updates can be written to DB before commit | High-performance systems | Better response time | Requires both undo and redo |
| **Steal/No-Steal** | Whether updated pages can be written to disk before commit | Buffer management policy | Affects recovery complexity | Steal is more flexible |
| **Force/No-Force** | Whether all updated pages forced to disk at commit | Durability guarantee | Force ensures durability | Force is expensive I/O-wise |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard:** Most modern DBMS (Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB) use **Steal / No-Force** policy with **ARIES** recovery algorithm.

## 2.7 The Defer vs. Immediate Update Trade-off

**Deferred Update (No Undo, Redo Only):**
- All updates first recorded in log.
- Database updates deferred until transaction's partial commit point.
- On recovery: **Redo** all committed transactions; ignore uncommitted.

**Immediate Update (Undo + Redo):**
- Updates can be applied to database before commit.
- On recovery: **Undo** all uncommitted transactions; **Redo** all committed transactions.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Property | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Recovery Cost** | $C_{recovery} = T_{restore} + T_{redo} + T_{undo}$ | Seconds |
| **Redo Operation** | $\text{REDO}(T): X_i \leftarrow V_{new}$ | Sets value to new |
| **Undo Operation** | $\text{UNDO}(T): X_i \leftarrow V_{old}$ | Restores old value |
| **WAL Constraint** | $\text{LSN}_{log} < \text{LSN}_{page} \implies \text{Block}$ | Log must precede |
| **Checkpoint Frequency** | $f_{cp} = \frac{1}{T_{cp\_time}}$ | Per unit time |
| **Backup Size (Incremental)** | $S_{inc} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_{changed_i}$ | Sum of changes |
| **Backup Size (Full)** | $S_{full} = S_{database}$ | Entire DB |
| **Backup Size (Differential)** | $S_{diff}(t) = \sum_{i=\text{last\_full}}^{t} S_{changed_i}$ | Since last full |
| **MTTR** | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum T_{downtime}}{N_{failures}}$ | Seconds |
| **Availability** | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems**: Use **immediate update with WAL** to support real-time balance inquiries; recovery must be sub-second to avoid regulatory violations.
- **E-Commerce Platforms**: Use **incremental backups every 15 minutes** with full nightly backups; RPO typically 15 minutes.
- **Healthcare Systems**: Use **point-in-time recovery (PITR)** to comply with HIPAA audit requirements; logs retained for **6 years minimum**.
- **Aerospace & Defense**: Use **synchronous mirroring** to geographically separated disaster recovery sites; RTO measured in seconds.
- **Cloud Databases (AWS RDS, Azure SQL)**: Use **continuous archiving + point-in-time restore** with automatic log shipping.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Recovery Rules (Log-Based Recovery)

The fundamental recovery rules can be derived logically from the ACID properties:

### 3.1.1 Rule 1: Redo Rule

**Premise:** A transaction T has committed if and only if a `<T, COMMIT>` log record appears on disk.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Definition of Commit} \\
&\text{Commit}(T) \iff \text{LogDisk}(\langle T, COMMIT \rangle) \lor \text{LogDisk}(\langle T, END \rangle) \\
&\text{Step 2: ACID Durability Property} \\
&\text{If } T \text{ committed, then effects must persist} \\
&\therefore \text{Recovery} \Rightarrow \text{REDO}(T) \text{ for all committed } T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** During recovery, all committed transactions must be **redone** (their updates reapplied) to ensure durability.

### 3.1.2 Rule 2: Undo Rule

**Premise:** A transaction T is uncommitted if no `<T, COMMIT>` record exists on disk at the time of crash.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Definition of Uncommitted State} \\
&\neg \text{Commit}(T) \iff \neg \text{LogDisk}(\langle T, COMMIT \rangle) \\
&\text{Step 2: ACID Atomicity Property} \\
&\text{If } T \text{ uncommitted, all partial effects must be rolled back} \\
&\therefore \text{Recovery} \Rightarrow \text{UNDO}(T) \text{ for all uncommitted } T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** During recovery, all uncommitted transactions must be **undone** to preserve atomicity.

### 3.1.3 The Combined Recovery Rule

Combining both rules for the general case:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{For each transaction } T: \\
&\quad \text{If } \langle T, COMMIT \rangle \in \text{LogDisk} : \text{Apply REDO}(T) \\
&\quad \text{If } \langle T, COMMIT \rangle \notin \text{LogDisk} : \text{Apply UNDO}(T)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Checkpoint-Optimized Recovery

Without checkpointing, recovery would scan the **entire log** from the beginning, leading to a cost proportional to log size. With checkpointing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Define recovery cost without checkpointing} \\
&T_{recovery}^{no\_cp} = O(\vert \text{Log} \vert) \\
&\text{Step 2: Define recovery cost with checkpointing} \\
&T_{recovery}^{cp} = O(\vert \text{Log} \vert - \vert \text{Log}_{\text{since last cp}} \vert) \\
&\text{Step 3: Efficiency gain} \\
&\eta = \frac{T_{recovery}^{no\_cp} - T_{recovery}^{cp}}{T_{recovery}^{no\_cp}} \times 100\% \\
&\text{Step 4: If checkpoints every } \tau \text{ time units} \\
&\eta \approx \frac{\tau \cdot f_{transaction}}{T_{log\_total}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Frequent checkpoints dramatically reduce recovery time, but each checkpoint itself costs I/O — there is a **trade-off**.

## 3.3 ARIES Recovery Algorithm — Detailed Step-by-Step

The **ARIES (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics)** algorithm, developed at IBM, is the industry standard. It uses **Steal / No-Force** policy and consists of **three phases**:

### Phase 1: Analysis Phase

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Identify the start point of the Redo Pass} \\
&\text{StartLSN} = \text{LSN of dirty page with smallest RecLSN in Dirty Page Table} \\
&\text{Step 2: Scan log forward from last successful checkpoint} \\
&\text{Step 3: For each log record } \langle L \rangle: \\
&\quad \text{If } L = \langle T, COMMIT \rangle : \text{Move T from ActiveList to CommittedList} \\
&\quad \text{If } L = \langle T, ABORT \rangle : \text{Move T from ActiveList to AbortedList} \\
&\quad \text{If } L = \langle T, X, V_{old}, V_{new} \rangle : \\
&\quad\quad \text{Update DirtyPageTable} \quad \text{(mark page containing X as dirty)} \\
&\quad\quad \text{Update TransactionTable \quad (record T's lastLSN)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Phase 2: Redo Phase

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Repeat history by redoing all actions} \\
&\text{Start at } \text{RedoLSN} = \text{Min(RecLSN of all dirty pages)} \\
&\text{Step 2: For each log record } \langle T, X, V_{old}, V_{new} \rangle \text{ from RedoLSN onward:} \\
&\quad \text{If page containing X is NOT in DirtyPageTable: skip} \\
&\quad \text{If page is in DirtyPageTable but RecLSN > current LSN: skip} \\
&\quad \text{Otherwise: } \\
&\quad\quad \text{Read page from disk} \\
&\quad\quad \text{If pageLSN < current LSN: apply REDO (set X = V_{new})} \\
&\quad\quad \text{Set pageLSN = current LSN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Phase 3: Undo Phase

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Build the "loser" set} \\
&\text{LoserSet} = \text{Transactions that were active at crash time and not committed} \\
&\text{Step 2: For each loser transaction T, in reverse LSN order:} \\
&\quad \text{Generate CLR (Compensating Log Record)} \\
&\quad \text{UNDO(T) by traversing PrevLSN chain} \\
&\quad \text{Write CLR to log and flush to disk BEFORE modifying database} \\
&\text{Step 3: When all losers undone, write } \langle \text{T, END} \rangle \text{ for each}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Shadow Paging Recovery — Step-by-Step

**Shadow paging** is an alternative to log-based recovery that avoids the overhead of maintaining logs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Page Table Setup} \\
&\text{Maintain two page tables: Current PT and Shadow PT} \\
&\text{Step 2: On Transaction Start} \\
&\text{CurrentPT} \leftarrow \text{ShadowPT} \\
&\text{Step 3: On Update} \\
&\text{Create new page (copy-on-write)} \\
&\text{Update CurrentPT to point to new page} \\
&\text{Step 4: On Commit} \\
&\text{Free old pages referenced by ShadowPT} \\
&\text{Update ShadowPT} \leftarrow \text{CurrentPT} \\
&\text{Step 5: On Crash} \\
&\text{Restore ShadowPT} \Rightarrow \text{Database automatically consistent}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Disadvantage:** Causes **fragmentation** due to scattered page updates; not used in modern high-performance DBMS.

## 3.5 Backup Strategy Cost Analysis (Detailed Derivation)

Given a database of size $S$ GB with daily change rate $\delta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Full Backup Size} \\
&S_{full} = S \\
&\text{Step 2: Incremental Backup Size (per backup)} \\
&S_{inc} = S \cdot \delta \\
&\text{Step 3: Total Storage for Strategy with weekly full + daily incremental (7 days):} \\
&S_{total} = S_{full} + 6 \cdot S_{inc} + 7 \cdot S_{inc}^{archive} \\
&\text{Step 4: Differential Backup Size (day } k \text{ after full):} \\
&S_{diff}(k) = S \cdot \delta \cdot k \\
&\text{Step 5: Recovery Time for Differential Strategy} \\
&T_{recover} = T_{restore\_full} + T_{restore\_diff} + T_{apply\_logs}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A bank database has size **1 TB** with a daily change rate of **2%**. The bank uses a full backup every Sunday and incremental backups every day. Calculate the total backup storage per week, and the recovery time if each GB takes 2 seconds to restore.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Given: } S = 1024 \text{ GB}, \quad \delta = 0.02 \\
&\text{Daily change size} = 1024 \times 0.02 = 20.48 \text{ GB} \\
&\text{Weekly full backup (Sunday)} = 1024 \text{ GB} \\
&\text{6 daily incrementals (Mon-Sat)} = 6 \times 20.48 = 122.88 \text{ GB} \\
&\text{Total weekly storage} = 1024 + 122.88 = 1146.88 \text{ GB} \\
&\text{Restore time (assume all 7 backups needed)}: \\
&T_{recover} = (1024 + 122.88) \times 2 = 2293.76 \text{ seconds} \\
&T_{recover} = 2293.76 / 60 \approx 38.23 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$
