# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Part A — Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q: Explain the Write-Ahead Log (WAL) protocol. Why is it essential for database recovery?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Each Point = 1 Mark):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
The Write-Ahead Log (WAL) protocol is a fundamental rule in DBMS that mandates that **before any modification is made to a database item on disk, the corresponding log record describing that modification must first be written to stable (non-volatile) storage**.

**Working (1 Mark):**
The DBMS maintains a **log buffer** in main memory. When a transaction modifies a data item:
1. The log record containing the old value, new value, transaction ID, and LSN is first written to the log buffer.
2. The log buffer is **flushed (forced)** to the log file on disk.
3. **Only after** the log record is safely on disk, the actual database page modification is performed.

**Significance (1 Mark):**
WAL is essential because:
- It guarantees that **no committed transaction is ever lost** — even if the system crashes, the log contains the necessary redo information.
- It ensures **atomicity** — uncommitted transactions can be undone because their log records (with old values) are available.
- It allows **steal policy** in buffer management, improving performance without compromising recoverability.

---

### Part A — Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**Q: Differentiate between Full, Incremental, and Differential backup strategies.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular Comparison Format):**

| Parameter | Full Backup | Incremental Backup | Differential Backup |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Definition** | Copies the entire database | Copies only data changed since the **last backup of any type** | Copies only data changed since the **last full backup** |
| **Backup Size** | Largest (entire DB size) | Smallest (only recent changes) | Grows daily (cumulative changes) |
| **Backup Speed** | Slowest | Fastest | Moderate |
| **Recovery Speed** | Fastest (one backup to restore) | Slowest (must apply all backups in sequence) | Moderate (full + last differential) |
| **Storage Cost** | Highest | Lowest | Moderate |
| **Use Case** | Weekly/monthly baseline | Frequent (every 15 min–hour) | Daily mid-tier backup |
| **Example Size (1TB DB, 2% change)** | 1024 GB | ~20 GB/day | 20 GB, 40 GB, 60 GB... |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students may choose ONE out of TWO alternatives.

---

### PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q: (a) Explain the different types of failures that can occur in a database system. Describe how the system recovers from each type of failure.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
A database failure is any event that disrupts the normal operation of the DBMS, causes data loss, or violates the consistency of the database. Recovery mechanisms aim to restore the database to a consistent state.

**Types of Failures (4 Marks):**

**1. Transaction Failure (Logical/System):**
- **Causes:** Division by zero, constraint violation, deadlock victim selection.
- **Recovery:** The DBMS **rolls back** the transaction using log records (undo operations). All updates made by the failed transaction are reversed using old values stored in the log.

**2. System Failure (Software/Hardware Crash):**
- **Causes:** Power outage, OS crash, DBMS software bug.
- **Effect:** Main memory is lost; database on disk remains intact.
- **Recovery:** The DBMS restarts and applies the recovery algorithm (e.g., ARIES):
   - All uncommitted transactions at crash time → **UNDONE** (atomicity).
   - All committed transactions whose updates are not on disk → **REDONE** (durability).

**3. Media Failure:**
- **Causes:** Disk head crash, bad sectors, storage controller failure.
- **Effect:** Portion of database on disk is corrupted or lost.
- **Recovery:**
   - **Restore** the database from the most recent full backup.
   - **Apply** all subsequent incremental/differential backups.
   - **Redo** committed transactions using archived log files.

**4. Natural Disaster / Catastrophic Failure:**
- **Causes:** Fire, flood, earthquake, vandalism.
- **Recovery:** Activate **disaster recovery site** with replicated data; restore from offsite archived backups.

**5. Human Error:**
- **Causes:** Accidental DELETE, wrong UPDATE, malicious activity.
- **Recovery:** **Point-in-time recovery (PITR)** using archived logs to restore to a state before the error.

**Conclusion (2 Marks):**
Modern DBMS implements a layered recovery approach: transaction-level recovery uses local logs; system-level recovery uses checkpoints + ARIES; media-level recovery uses backup + log shipping; disaster-level recovery uses geographic replication. This defense-in-depth ensures that the **ACID properties** are preserved under all failure scenarios.

---

**Q: (b) Describe the ARIES recovery algorithm in detail. Explain its three phases.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
**ARIES (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics)** is an industry-standard recovery algorithm developed at IBM Research. It uses the **Steal / No-Force** policy, supports **fine-grained locking**, and uses **Log Sequence Numbers (LSNs)** for log record identification. ARIES consists of three sequential phases.

**Phase 1: Analysis Phase (2 Marks):**
- The system scans the log **forward**, starting from the last successful checkpoint.
- It builds two key in-memory structures:
  - **Transaction Table:** Lists all active transactions at crash time, with their lastLSN pointers.
  - **Dirty Page Table:** Lists all dirty (modified but unflushed) pages in the buffer pool, with their **RecLSN** (oldest LSN that could have modified the page).
- Determines the **RedoLSN** = minimum RecLSN in the dirty page table, marking the start point for the redo phase.

**Phase 2: Redo Phase (2 Marks):**
- Starts scanning the log forward from **RedoLSN**.
- **Repeats history**: re-applies all updates (even those of uncommitted transactions) to bring the database to the exact state it was in at crash time.
- For each log record:
  - If the affected page is **not in the Dirty Page Table** → skip (page is clean).
  - If the page's RecLSN > current LSN → skip (this update was already on disk).
  - Otherwise → read the page, compare pageLSN, and **REDO** the update.
- This phase ensures that all committed and uncommitted updates reflect in the database.

**Phase 3: Undo Phase (2 Marks):**
- Identifies the **Loser Set**: transactions that were active at crash time but did **not** commit.
- For each loser transaction T:
  - Traverse the log **backward** using the PrevLSN chain.
  - Generate **Compensating Log Records (CLRs)** to record the undo actions.
  - CLRs are written and **flushed to disk** before applying the undo (WAL).
  - Continue until T is fully rolled back; write an `<T, END>` record.
- Final state: only committed transactions have their effects in the database.

**Key ARIES Features (Bonus — 0 Marks but valuable):**
- **WAL compliance** ensures recoverability.
- **CLRs enable restart-safe undo**: if crash occurs during undo, recovery can resume.
- **Page-oriented redo** allows efficient I/O.

---

### PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**Q: (a) What is a log? Explain the structure of a log record. Describe the different types of log records used in database recovery.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Log (1 Mark):**
A **log** (or journal) is a sequence of records maintained by the DBMS that chronicles **all modifications** to the database. It is the **primary source of truth** for crash recovery and is stored on stable (non-volatile) storage.

**Structure of a Log Record (2 Marks):**
A log record typically contains the following fields:

$$
\text{LogRecord} = \langle \text{LSN}, \text{T\_ID}, \text{Type}, \text{PageID}, \text{Data}, \text{PrevLSN} \rangle
$$

- **LSN (Log Sequence Number):** Unique, monotonically increasing identifier.
- **T_ID:** Transaction identifier.
- **Type:** Record type (BEGIN, UPDATE, COMMIT, ABORT, CHECKPOINT, END, CLR).
- **PageID:** Affected database page.
- **Data:** Old value and new value (for updates).
- **PrevLSN:** Pointer to the previous log record of the same transaction.

**Types of Log Records (4 Marks):**

**1. `<T, BEGIN>` — Start Record:**
Marks the beginning of transaction T. Used to identify active transactions at crash time.

**2. `<T, X, V_old, V_new>` — Update Record:**
Records a modification to data item X by transaction T, including before-image and after-image.

**3. `<T, COMMIT>` — Commit Record:**
Marks successful commit of T. The transaction's effects are guaranteed to persist.

**4. `<T, ABORT>` — Abort Record:**
Marks the abort of T. The DBMS must undo all T's effects.

**5. `<CHECKPOINT, ActiveTransactions>` — Checkpoint Record:**
Snapshot of all transactions active at the time of checkpoint.

**6. `<T, X, V_new, UndoNextLSN>` — Compensating Log Record (CLR):**
Generated during undo operations; allows safe restart of recovery.

**7. `<T, END>` — End Record:**
Marks final completion of a transaction (used in deferred update and ARIES).

**Example (Bonus):**
A sample log sequence for transaction T1 updating X from 100 to 200:

```
<LSN=101, T1, BEGIN>
<LSN=102, T1, X, V_old=100, V_new=200>
<LSN=103, T1, COMMIT>
<LSN=104, T1, END>
```

---

**Q: (b) Explain the concept of checkpointing. Discuss how checkpointing improves recovery efficiency. Describe the actions performed during checkpointing.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A **checkpoint** is a synchronization point in the log that establishes a known good state of the database. It marks the point up to which all log records have been flushed to stable storage and all dirty pages have been written to disk (in sharp checkpoint).

**Why Checkpointing is Needed (1 Mark):**
Without checkpoints, recovery would need to scan the **entire log** from the beginning of time, which is prohibitively expensive for large databases. Checkpoints allow the recovery process to **start from a recent known state**, dramatically reducing recovery time.

**Actions During Checkpointing (3 Marks):**

**Sharp Checkpoint Procedure:**
1. **Halt** all new transaction starts temporarily.
2. **Flush** all log records currently in the log buffer to disk.
3. **Write** a `<CHECKPOINT, ActiveTIDs>` record to the log, listing all currently active transactions.
4. **Force** all modified (dirty) buffer pages to disk.
5. **Flush** the checkpoint record itself to disk.
6. **Resume** normal transaction processing.

**Fuzzy Checkpoint (ARIES-style) Procedure:**
1. Write a `<BEGIN_CHECKPOINT>` record.
2. Construct the Transaction Table and Dirty Page Table in memory.
3. Write a `<END_CHECKPOINT, TT, DPT>` record.
4. Continue processing transactions during the checkpoint — no halt required.

**How Checkpointing Improves Recovery (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Recovery cost without checkpointing: } O(\vert \text{Log} \vert) \\
&\text{Recovery cost with checkpointing: } O(\vert \text{Log} \vert - \vert \text{Log}_{cp} \vert) \\
&\text{Improvement ratio: } \eta = 1 - \frac{\text{Log since last checkpoint}}{\text{Total log size}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Benefits:**
- **Faster recovery**: Only need to redo/undo transactions since last checkpoint.
- **Reduced log scan time**: Recovery starts from checkpoint LSN, not log beginning.
- **Lower CPU/IO overhead**: Critical for large OLTP systems.
- **Better RTO**: Recovery Time Objective improves significantly.

**Trade-off:**
Checkpoints themselves cost I/O. Frequent checkpoints reduce recovery time but increase steady-state overhead. The optimal checkpoint interval balances:
$$
T_{optimal} \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot T_{cp} \cdot N_{tx}}{f_{tx}}}
$$

Where $T_{cp}$ is checkpoint cost, $N_{tx}$ is transactions between checkpoints, and $f_{tx}$ is transaction rate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing ARIES phases**: Students often mix up the order. **Mnemonic: "A.R.U." = Analyze, Redo, Undo.** The redo phase ALWAYS precedes the undo phase, even though undo is what we logically think of first.
> 
> 2. **Missing the WAL guarantee**: When explaining recovery, ALWAYS explicitly state the WAL rule — examiners award 1-2 marks specifically for this.
> 
> 3. **Confusing incremental vs. differential backups**: Incremental = changes since **last backup of any type**; Differential = changes since **last full backup**. This distinction is worth 1-2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting CLR purpose**: In ARIES, Compensating Log Records are NOT just for undo — they are for **restart-safe undo**. If the system crashes during undo, the CLR allows recovery to continue from the correct point.
> 
> 5. **Not listing active transactions in checkpoint**: The checkpoint record MUST include the list of active transactions. Forgetting this loses marks.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up REDO and UNDO semantics**: REDO sets value to **new** (after-image); UNDO sets value to **old** (before-image). Students frequently get this wrong.
> 
> 7. **Skipping the "Steal/No-Force" policy mention**: ARIES uses this specific combination. Stating only "ARIES uses log-based recovery" loses 1 mark.
> 
> 8. **Numerical calculation errors**: In backup storage problems, clearly state all given values, conversions (TB to GB), and final units. Always show intermediate steps.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Quick Revision Checklist

**Core Concepts to Memorize:**
- [ ] **WAL Protocol**: Log record MUST be on disk before database modification.
- [ ] **Three ARIES Phases**: Analysis → Redo → Undo (in that order).
- [ ] **REDO sets NewValue**; **UNDO sets OldValue**.
- [ ] **Transaction Failure** → local rollback (undo only).
- [ ] **System Failure** → ARIES algorithm (undo + redo).
- [ ] **Media Failure** → restore from backup + apply log.
- [ ] **Disaster Failure** → activate DR site with replicated data.

**Backup Strategy Differences (must-know):**
- [ ] **Full Backup**: Entire database; slow but fastest recovery.
- [ ] **Incremental Backup**: Since last backup (any type); smallest size but slowest recovery.
- [ ] **Differential Backup**: Since last full backup; cumulative size, moderate recovery.

**Log Record Types (must enumerate all 7):**
- [ ] BEGIN, UPDATE, COMMIT, ABORT, CHECKPOINT, END, CLR.

**Key Formulas to Remember:**
- [ ] $T_{recovery} = T_{restore} + T_{redo} + T_{undo}$
- [ ] $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$
- [ ] Incremental storage: $S_{inc} = S \cdot \delta$
- [ ] Differential storage at day k: $S_{diff}(k) = S \cdot \delta \cdot k$

**ARIES-Specific Facts:**
- [ ] Uses **Steal / No-Force** buffer management policy.
- [ ] Uses **LSN (Log Sequence Number)** for ordering.
- [ ] CLRs enable **restart-safe undo**.
- [ ] **RecLSN** = oldest LSN that modified a dirty page.
- [ ] **RedoLSN** = minimum RecLSN across all dirty pages.
- [ ] **Redo phase repeats history** (reapplies ALL updates).
- [ ] **Undo phase** only undoes uncommitted (loser) transactions.

**Industry Standard Values:**
- [ ] RTO for critical systems: **15 minutes** or less.
- [ ] RPO for financial systems: **near-zero** (synchronous replication).
- [ ] Log retention: **30 days minimum**, up to 7 years for compliance.
- [ ] Checkpoint frequency: **every 5-15 minutes** in OLTP systems.

**Shadow Paging (for contrast):**
- [ ] Uses **current** and **shadow** page tables.
- [ ] On commit, shadow PT is updated.
- [ ] On crash, shadow PT points to consistent state.
- [ ] **Disadvantage**: causes fragmentation, not used in modern high-perf DBMS.

**Common KTU Board Keywords (use these in answers):**
- [ ] "ACID properties"
- [ ] "Stable storage"
- [ ] "Atomicity and Durability"
- [ ] "Log-based recovery"
- [ ] "Forward and backward recovery"
- [ ] "Idempotent operations"
- [ ] "Compensating transactions"

### 📚 Final Exam Strategy Tip

> [!TIP]
> For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as:
> 1. **Introduction (1 mark)**: One-sentence definition.
> 2. **Main Body (10-12 marks)**: 3-5 well-explained points with diagrams.
> 3. **Conclusion or Real-world application (1-2 marks)**: Practical relevance.
> 4. **Always include** a **diagram** (mermaid-style or ASCII) for visual clarity — examiners reward it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing and Validation: Execute queries to validate the correctness of the schema and constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT304 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Testing and Validation: Execute queries to validate the correctness of the schema and constraints ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Schema and Constraint Validation** is the systematic process of executing structured query language (SQL) statements against a populated database instance to verify that the logical and physical schema definitions, integrity rules, business constraints, and data dependencies behave exactly as specified by the conceptual design (ER/EER) and the relational mapping rules. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme DBMS syllabus (PBITT304, Module 4), this phase is the final pre-deployment gate that ensures **Entity Integrity**, **Referential Integrity**, **Domain Integrity**, and **User-Defined Integrity** (CHECK constraints, triggers) are not only declared but also **enforced** correctly by the RDBMS engine. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard)** > *Testing and Validation in DBMS refers to the execution of DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) and DQL (SELECT) statements to verify that the implemented relational schema correctly enforces all declared constraints (KEY, FOREIGN KEY, UNIQUE, CHECK, NOT NULL, DEFAULT) and correctly preserves the functional dependencies and business rules derived from the original ER model.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have designed a **custom-made wooden bookshelf** (your database schema). Before you load it with heavy books (data), you need to **stress-test** it: - You push against each shelf to check it doesn't wobble (**constraint testing**). - You place a book that is too tall to see if the shelf physically rejects it (**CHECK constraint**). - You try to remove a critical support leg to ensure the system warns you (**referential integrity**). - You place identical books on every shelf to check the weight distribution works uniformly across all tables (**uniform validation across all relations**). In a database, the equivalent of "pushing and pulling" the bookshelf is running **SQL queries** — INSERT statements, JOINs, aggregate functions, and error-triggering queries — to ensure the schema *behaves correctly* under every realistic and edge-case scenario. > [!NOTE] > **Why this topic matters in KTU exams:** > The KTU 2024 Scheme places heavy emphasis on **NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. This topic directly tests **CO4 (Apply SQL constructs to validate schema design)**. Expect 7–14 mark questions asking you to write and explain validation queries for a given schema. ### 1.3 Key Metrics and Standards - **Constraint Coverage Rate (CCR)**: The ratio of validated constraints to total declared constraints. KTU expects **CCR = 1.0 (100%)** before declaring a schema "tested". - **Test Data Cardinality**: Minimum sample size recommended is **at least 10 rows per table** plus **3–5 boundary/edge-case rows** (NULL attempts, duplicate attempts, invalid FK attempts). - **Query Validation Types** — `DML Validation`, `DQL Validation`, `DCL Validation`, `Constraint Violation Testing`. - **Integrity Rule Count**: KTU expects students to validate at least **4 canonical integrity types** — *Entity, Referential, Domain, User-defined*. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Schema Validation When you test a schema, you must systematically attack it from **six different angles**. Missing any one of these is a guaranteed mark-loss zone in KTU valuations. #### Pillar 1: Structure Verification (Metadata-Level) Before touching data, verify the schema itself is structurally identical to the ER design. ```sql -- Verify all tables exist SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema = 'public'; -- Verify column definitions match ER mapping SELECT column_name, data_type, is_nullable, column_default FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_name = 'STUDENT'; -- Verify all primary keys are declared SELECT constraint_name, table_name, constraint_type FROM information_schema.table_constraints WHERE constraint_type = 'PRIMARY KEY'; ``` **Why this matters**: A missing PRIMARY KEY declaration silently converts your relation into a *bag* (multiset) instead of a *set*, breaking all functional dependency theory. This is a **favourite KTU Part A question** (3 marks). #### Pillar 2: Entity Integrity Testing Every tuple in a base relation must have a **unique, non-NULL primary key**. ```sql -- Test 1: Try to insert NULL primary key (must FAIL) INSERT INTO STUDENT (roll_no, name) VALUES (NULL, 'Anu'); -- Expected: ERROR - null value in column "roll_no" violates not-null constraint -- Test 2: Try to insert duplicate primary key (must FAIL) INSERT INTO STUDENT (roll_no, name) VALUES (101, 'Anu'); INSERT INTO STUDENT (roll_no, name) VALUES (101, 'Manu'); -- Expected: ERROR - duplicate key value violates unique constraint -- Test 3: Verify no NULLs exist in PK column SELECT COUNT(*) FROM STUDENT WHERE roll_no IS NULL; -- Expected Result: 0 ``` #### Pillar 3: Referential Integrity Testing Every foreign key value must either be **NULL** or **match an existing primary key** in the referenced table. ```sql -- Test 1: Insert child row with non-existent parent FK (must FAIL) INSERT INTO ENROLLMENT (enroll_id, roll_no, course_id) VALUES (1, 999, 'CS101'); -- Expected: ERROR - insert or update on table "enrollment" violates -- foreign key constraint "enrollment_roll_no_fkey" -- Test 2: Try to delete a parent row that has dependent children DELETE FROM STUDENT WHERE roll_no = 101; -- Expected (if ON DELETE NO ACTION): ERROR -- Expected (if ON DELETE CASCADE): All child rows auto-deleted -- Test 3: Verify no orphan records SELECT e.roll_no FROM ENROLLMENT e LEFT JOIN STUDENT s ON e.roll_no = s.roll_no WHERE s.roll_no IS NULL; -- Expected Result: 0 rows ``` #### Pillar 4: Domain Integrity Testing Every attribute value must belong to its declared **domain** (data type, format, range). ```sql -- Test 1: CHECK constraint violation INSERT INTO STUDENT (roll_no, name, age, cgpa) VALUES (102, 'Ravi', -5, 9.5); -- Expected: ERROR - violates check constraint "student_age_check" -- Test 2: Data type mismatch INSERT INTO STUDENT (roll_no, name, age) VALUES ('abc', 'Ravi', 20); -- Expected: ERROR - invalid input syntax for type integer -- Test 3: Verify domain boundaries SELECT MIN(age), MAX(age), MIN(cgpa), MAX(cgpa) FROM STUDENT; -- Cross-check against declared CHECK conditions ``` #### Pillar 5: User-Defined Constraint & Business Rule Testing Business logic encoded via `CHECK`, `UNIQUE`, `DEFAULT`, `TRIGGER`. ```sql -- Test UNIQUE constraint on non-key attribute INSERT INTO STUDENT (roll_no, email) VALUES (101, '[email protected]'); INSERT INTO STUDENT (roll_no, email) VALUES (102, '[email protected]'); -- Expected: ERROR - duplicate key value violates unique constraint -- Test DEFAULT values INSERT INTO STUDENT (roll_no, name) VALUES (103, 'Meera'); SELECT * FROM STUDENT WHERE roll_no = 103; -- Verify status column has its default value automatically ``` #### Pillar 6: Functional Dependency & Anomaly Testing Verify that the schema is in the **correct normal form** and the original FDs are preserved. ```sql -- Test transitive dependency removal (3NF compliance) -- If FD was: course_id -> course_name, instructor -- After decomposition, this should hold in COURSE table only SELECT course_id, COUNT(DISTINCT course_name) AS distinct_names FROM COURSE GROUP BY course_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT course_name) > 1; -- Expected: 0 rows (proves no transitive dependency remains) -- Test join dependency losslessness SELECT COUNT(*) FROM (STUDENT NATURAL JOIN ENROLLMENT); -- Compare with original STUDENT table row count ``` ### 2.2 The Validation Workflow (Sequential Pipeline) ``` ┌─────────────────────┐ │ 1. Schema Review │ (Compare CREATE TABLE vs ER Diagram) └──────────┬──────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ 2. Metadata Verify │ (information_schema queries) └──────────┬──────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ 3. Sample Data Load │ (Valid representative rows) └──────────┬──────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ 4. Negative Testing │ (Invalid data - should FAIL) └──────────┬──────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ 5. Positive Testing │ (Valid data - should SUCCEED) └──────────┬──────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ 6. Anomaly Check │ (NULL, duplicate, orphan detection) └──────────┬──────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ 7. Report & Fix │ (Document failures, alter schema) └─────────────────────┘ ``` ### 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet | **Concept** | **Test Query Type** | **Expected Outcome on Valid Schema** | |---|---|---| | Entity Integrity | `INSERT ... NULL PK` | **ERROR** raised | | Entity Integrity | `INSERT ... duplicate PK` | **ERROR** raised | | Referential Integrity | `INSERT ... invalid FK` | **ERROR** raised | | Referential Integrity | `DELETE parent with children` (NO ACTION) | **ERROR** raised | | Domain Integrity | `INSERT ... out-of-range value` | **ERROR** raised (CHECK) | | User Integrity | `INSERT ... duplicate UNIQUE attr` | **ERROR** raised | | Default Value | `INSERT ... omit column with DEFAULT` | **Auto-filled** value present | | FD Preservation | `GROUP BY ... HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT) > 1` | **0 rows** returned | | Lossless Join | `NATURAL JOIN` row count | Equals original table cardinality | | Orphan Detection | `LEFT JOIN ... IS NULL` | **0 rows** returned | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Engineering Utility**: In industry, this validation phase is part of **Database Regression Testing** in CI/CD pipelines. Tools like *DbUnit*, *pgTAP* (PostgreSQL), and *tSQLt* (SQL Server) automate exactly these query patterns. Banking systems (e.g., RBI's CKYC database) run **thousands** of such validation queries nightly to ensure regulatory compliance. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example: University Database Let us validate the following schema end-to-end using the standard KTU reference database (a favourite in past papers). **Given Schema:** ```sql CREATE TABLE DEPARTMENT ( dept_id CHAR(3) PRIMARY KEY, dept_name VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE NOT NULL, hod VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE STUDENT ( roll_no INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, age INTEGER CHECK (age >= 17 AND age <= 30), cgpa NUMERIC(3,2) CHECK (cgpa >= 0 AND cgpa <= 10), dept_id CHAR(3), email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE, FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES DEPARTMENT(dept_id) ON DELETE SET NULL ON UPDATE CASCADE ); CREATE TABLE COURSE ( course_id CHAR(5) PRIMARY KEY, course_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, credits INTEGER CHECK (credits > 0) ); CREATE TABLE ENROLLMENT ( roll_no INTEGER, course_id CHAR(5), enroll_date DATE DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE, grade CHAR(1) CHECK (grade IN ('A','B','C','D','F')), PRIMARY KEY (roll_no, course_id), FOREIGN KEY (roll_no) REFERENCES STUDENT(roll_no) ON DELETE CASCADE, FOREIGN KEY (course_id) REFERENCES COURSE(course_id) ON DELETE CASCADE ); ``` ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Validation Walkthrough #### Test 1: Verify Structural Integrity (Metadata) **Step 1.1** — List all tables in the schema. ```sql SELECT table_name, table_type FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema = 'public' ORDER BY table_name; ``` **Expected Result Set:** | table_name | table_type | |---|---| | course | BASE TABLE | | department | BASE TABLE | | enrollment | BASE TABLE | | student | BASE TABLE | > If any table is missing, schema is incomplete. **Step 1.2** — Verify primary keys. ```sql SELECT tc.table_name, kc.column_name, tc.constraint_type FROM information_schema.table_constraints tc JOIN information_schema.key_column_usage kc ON tc.constraint_name = kc.constraint_name WHERE tc.constraint_type = 'PRIMARY KEY' ORDER BY tc.table_name; ``` **Expected Output:** | table_name | column_name | constraint_type | |---|---|---| | course | course_id | PRIMARY KEY | | department | dept_id | PRIMARY KEY | | enrollment | course_id | PRIMARY KEY | | enrollment | roll_no | PRIMARY KEY | | student | roll_no | PRIMARY KEY | #### Test 2: Entity Integrity — Positive Test ```sql -- Insert valid students INSERT INTO STUDENT VALUES (1, 'Arjun', 20, 8.5, 'CSE', '[email protected]'); INSERT INTO STUDENT VALUES (2, 'Diya', 21, 9.2, 'CSE', '[email protected]'); INSERT INTO STUDENT VALUES (3, 'Rahul', 19, 7.8, 'ECE', '[email protected]'); ``` **Verification Query:** ```sql SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_students FROM STUDENT; -- Expected: 3 ``` #### Test 3: Entity Integrity — Negative Test (Must FAIL) ```sql -- Attempt 1: NULL PK INSERT INTO STUDENT (roll_no, name) VALUES (NULL, 'Test'); ``` **System Response:** `ERROR: null value in column "roll_no" violates not-null constraint` ✓ **Test PASSED** (constraint works correctly). ```sql -- Attempt 2: Duplicate PK INSERT INTO STUDENT VALUES (1, 'Duplicate', 20, 8.0, 'CSE', '[email protected]'); ``` **System Response:** `ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "student_pkey"` ✓ **Test PASSED**. #### Test 4: Domain Integrity (CHECK Constraint) ```sql -- Attempt 3: Age below minimum INSERT INTO STUDENT VALUES (4, 'Minor', 12, 7.0, 'CSE', '[email protected]'); ``` **System Response:** `ERROR: new row for relation "student" violates check constraint "student_age_check"` ✓ **Test PASSED**. ```sql -- Attempt 4: CGPA above maximum INSERT INTO STUDENT VALUES (5, 'Genius', 20, 15.0, 'CSE', '[email protected]'); ``` **System Response:** `ERROR: violates check constraint "student_cgpa_check"` ✓ **Test PASSED**. #### Test 5: Referential Integrity ```sql -- Insert parent rows INSERT INTO DEPARTMENT VALUES ('CSE', 'Computer Science', 'Dr. Sharma'); INSERT INTO DEPARTMENT VALUES ('ECE', 'Electronics', 'Dr. Verma'); -- Test 5.1: Insert child with valid FK INSERT INTO STUDENT VALUES (6, 'Valid', 20, 8.0, 'CSE', '[email protected]'); -- ✓ Should SUCCEED ``` ```sql -- Test 5.2: Insert child with invalid FK INSERT INTO STUDENT VALUES (7, 'Invalid', 20, 8.0, 'MEC', '[email protected]'); ``` **System Response:** `ERROR: insert or update on table "student" violates foreign key constraint "student_dept_id_fkey"` ✓ **Test PASSED**. ```sql -- Test 5.3: ON DELETE SET NULL behavior DELETE FROM DEPARTMENT WHERE dept_id = 'CSE'; -- Expected: SUCCEEDS, and STUDENT rows with dept_id='CSE' get dept_id=NULL SELECT roll_no, name, dept_id FROM STUDENT WHERE dept_id IS NULL; ``` **Verification:** | roll_no | name | dept_id | |---|---|---| | 1 | Arjun | NULL | | 2 | Diya | NULL | | 6 | Valid | NULL | ✓ **Cascade behaviour verified.** #### Test 6: User-Defined Integrity (UNIQUE on non-key) ```sql INSERT INTO STUDENT (roll_no, name, email) VALUES (10, 'A', '[email protected]'); INSERT INTO STUDENT (roll_no, name, email) VALUES (11, 'B', '[email protected]'); ``` **Expected Result:** `ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "student_email_key"` ✓ **Test PASSED**. #### Test 7: Composite Key Test (ENROLLMENT) ```sql INSERT INTO COURSE VALUES ('CS101', 'DBMS', 4); INSERT INTO COURSE VALUES ('CS102', 'OS', 3); INSERT INTO ENROLLMENT (roll_no, course_id, grade) VALUES (2, 'CS101', 'A'); INSERT INTO ENROLLMENT (roll_no, course_id, grade) VALUES (2, 'CS102', 'B'); INSERT INTO ENROLLMENT (roll_no, course_id, grade) VALUES (3, 'CS101', 'C'); -- Re-attempt the SAME composite key — must FAIL INSERT INTO ENROLLMENT (roll_no, course_id, grade) VALUES (2, 'CS101', 'A'); ``` **System Response:** `ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "enrollment_pkey"` ✓ **Test PASSED**. #### Test 8: Anomaly Detection (Comprehensive Audit Query) ```sql -- 8.1: Find orphan enrollments (referential violation - should be 0) SELECT COUNT(*) AS orphan_enrollments FROM ENROLLMENT e LEFT JOIN STUDENT s ON e.roll_no = s.roll_no LEFT JOIN COURSE c ON e.course_id = c.course_id WHERE s.roll_no IS NULL OR c.course_id IS NULL; -- 8.2: Find students in non-existent departments (after SET NULL, this is 0) SELECT COUNT(*) AS phantom_students FROM STUDENT s LEFT JOIN DEPARTMENT d ON s.dept_id = d.dept_id WHERE s.dept_id IS NOT NULL AND d.dept_id IS NULL; -- 8.3: Verify CGPA range SELECT MIN(cgpa) AS min_cgpa, MAX(cgpa) AS max_cgpa FROM STUDENT; -- Both must be within [0, 10] ``` **All must return 0 rows or values within declared domain.** ✓ #### Test 9: Functional Dependency Validation For FD `dept_id → dept_name` (in DEPARTMENT): ```sql SELECT dept_id, COUNT(DISTINCT dept_name) AS name_variations FROM DEPARTMENT GROUP BY dept_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT dept_name) > 1; ``` **Expected Result:** 0 rows (proves FD holds in current data). > If 1+ rows returned, either schema decomposition is incorrect OR data is corrupted. This is a **classic 14-mark KTU question** on validating normalization. #### Test 10: Lossless Join Verification ```sql -- Original count SELECT COUNT(*) FROM STUDENT; -- After joining with ENROLLMENT and back-projecting SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT s.roll_no) FROM STUDENT s JOIN ENROLLMENT e ON s.roll_no = e.roll_no; ``` If schema decomposition is lossless, both counts are **consistent with the 1:M relationship** (joined count $\leq$ original count, never inflated). --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Constraint-to-Query Mapping | **Constraint Type** | **Negative Test Query** | **Positive Test Query** | **Audit Query** | |---|---|---|---| | `PRIMARY KEY` | `INSERT NULL PK` | `INSERT valid PK` | `SELECT COUNT(*) ... GROUP BY PK HAVING COUNT>1` | | `FOREIGN KEY` | `INSERT invalid FK` | `INSERT valid FK` | `LEFT JOIN ... IS NULL` | | `UNIQUE` | `INSERT duplicate` | `INSERT distinct` | `SELECT ... GROUP BY ... HAVING COUNT>1` | | `NOT NULL` | `INSERT NULL` | `INSERT valid value` | `SELECT COUNT(*) WHERE col IS NULL` | | `CHECK` | `INSERT violating value` | `INSERT in-range value` | `SELECT MIN, MAX FROM table` | | `DEFAULT` | Omit column | Include explicit value | `SELECT ... WHERE col = default_val` | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Validation Testing Pyramid ```mermaid graph TD schemaP["Schema Validation Pyramid"] layer1["Layer 1: Metadata Audit
information_schema queries"] layer2["Layer 2: Constraint Enforcement Tests
PK, FK, UNIQUE, CHECK, NOT NULL"] layer3["Layer 3: Business Rule Tests
Triggers, stored procedures"] layer4["Layer 4: Normalization & FD Tests
Lossless join, dependency preservation"] layer5["Layer 5: Performance Tests
Index usage, query plans"] layer6["Layer 6: Concurrency Tests
Deadlocks, isolation levels"] schemaP --> layer1 layer1 --> layer2 layer2 --> layer3 layer3 --> layer4 layer4 --> layer5 layer5 --> layer6 style schemaP fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff style layer1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af style layer2 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af style layer3 fill:#93c5fd,stroke:#1e40af style layer4 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#1e40af,color:#fff style layer5 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e40af,color:#fff style layer6 fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1e40af,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Test Execution Flowchart ```mermaid graph TD startA["Start Validation"] loadData["Load Valid Sample Data
INSERT statements"] metaCheck{"Metadata
Matches ER?"} entityCheck{"Entity Integrity
Tests Pass?"} refCheck{"Referential Integrity
Tests Pass?"} domainCheck{"Domain Integrity
Tests Pass?"} userCheck{"User Constraints
Tests Pass?"} fdCheck{"FD & Normal Form
Tests Pass?"} report["Generate Validation Report
PASS/FAIL Summary"] fix["Fix Schema Bugs
ALTER TABLE"] startA --> loadData loadData --> metaCheck metaCheck -->|No| fix metaCheck -->|Yes| entityCheck entityCheck -->|No| fix entityCheck -->|Yes| refCheck refCheck -->|No| fix refCheck -->|Yes| domainCheck domainCheck -->|No| fix domainCheck -->|Yes| userCheck userCheck -->|No| fix userCheck -->|Yes| fdCheck fdCheck -->|No| fix fdCheck -->|Yes| report fix --> metaCheck style startA fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff style report fill:#22c55e,stroke:#000,color:#fff style fix fill:#ef4444,stroke:#000,color:#fff style metaCheck fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000 style entityCheck fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000 style refCheck fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000 style domainCheck fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000 style userCheck fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000 style fdCheck fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Constraint Validation Block Architecture ```mermaid graph LR testInput["Test Input:
SQL Query / Sample Data"] constraintEngine["RDBMS Constraint Engine"] pkBlock["PK Checker
UNIQUE + NOT NULL"] fkBlock["FK Checker
Parent-Child Match"] ukBlock["UNIQUE Checker
Per non-key attr"] chkBlock["CHECK Checker
Predicate evaluation"] nnBlock["NOT NULL Checker
NULL filter"] defBlock["DEFAULT Inserter
Auto-value provider"] trigBlock["Trigger Executor
Business rule handler"] resultValid["VALID: Row Inserted/Updated"] resultInvalid["INVALID: SQLSTATE error
Constraint name + violation message"] testInput --> constraintEngine constraintEngine --> pkBlock constraintEngine --> fkBlock constraintEngine --> ukBlock constraintEngine --> chkBlock constraintEngine --> nnBlock constraintEngine --> defBlock constraintEngine --> trigBlock pkBlock --> resultValid pkBlock --> resultInvalid fkBlock --> resultValid fkBlock --> resultInvalid ukBlock --> resultValid ukBlock --> resultInvalid chkBlock --> resultValid chkBlock --> resultInvalid nnBlock --> resultValid nnBlock --> resultInvalid defBlock --> resultValid trigBlock --> resultValid trigBlock --> resultInvalid style testInput fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#000,color:#fff style constraintEngine fill:#6366f1,stroke:#000,color:#fff style resultValid fill:#22c55e,stroke:#000,color:#fff style resultInvalid fill:#ef4444,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.4 Schema Validation Lifecycle (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD req["Step 1: Requirements
ER Diagram + Business Rules"] rel["Step 2: Relational Mapping
Tables + Constraints"] ddl["Step 3: DDL Execution
CREATE TABLE statements"] meta["Step 4: Metadata Verification
information_schema queries"] pos["Step 5: Positive Testing
Valid DML should SUCCEED"] neg["Step 6: Negative Testing
Invalid DML should FAIL"] aud["Step 7: Anomaly Audit
NULL/orphan/duplicate scans"] perf["Step 8: Performance Baseline
EXPLAIN ANALYZE on key queries"] sign["Step 9: Sign-off
Validation Report Generated"] req --> rel rel --> ddl ddl --> meta meta --> pos pos --> neg neg --> aud aud --> perf perf --> sign sign -.->|If Failed| rel style req fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#000,color:#fff style ddl fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#000,color:#fff style meta fill:#8b5cf6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style pos fill:#22c55e,stroke:#000,color:#fff style neg fill:#ef4444,stroke:#000,color:#fff style aud fill:#14b8a6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style perf fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style sign fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *List any four SQL queries that can be used to validate the **entity integrity constraint** of a relation. Explain what each query tests.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The following four queries validate entity integrity: 1. **NULL PK Test** — `INSERT INTO Student (roll_no, name) VALUES (NULL, 'X');` — Tests that primary key cannot be NULL. Expected: `ERROR: null value in column "roll_no" violates not-null constraint`. 2. **Duplicate PK Test** — Insert two rows with same `roll_no`. Tests that primary key values are unique. Expected: `ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint`. 3. **Audit for existing NULLs** — `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Student WHERE roll_no IS NULL;` — Should return 0. 4. **Audit for existing duplicates** — `SELECT roll_no, COUNT(*) FROM Student GROUP BY roll_no HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;` — Should return 0 rows. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *What is the difference between **positive testing** and **negative testing** in the context of database schema validation? Give one example query for each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Positive Testing**: Executes *valid* SQL statements to confirm that legitimate operations are accepted by the schema. *Example*: `INSERT INTO Student VALUES (1, 'Anu', 20, 8.5, 'CSE');` should succeed without error. - **Negative Testing**: Executes *invalid* SQL statements (violating constraints) to confirm that the RDBMS correctly **rejects** them. *Example*: `INSERT INTO Student VALUES (NULL, 'Anu', 20, 8.5, 'CSE');` should fail with a `not-null constraint` error. Together, they prove that the schema is neither too permissive (accepting garbage) nor too restrictive (rejecting valid data). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > Common mistakes students make in this topic: > 1. **Forgetting to specify the expected outcome** — KTU examiners award marks for stating *what the query should return* in addition to the query itself. > 2. **Mixing up CASCADE and SET NULL** — When asked about referential actions, students often write "cascade" everywhere. Read the schema's `ON DELETE` clause. > 3. **Not validating FDs after normalization** — A schema can satisfy all constraints yet violate 3NF. Always write FD verification queries. > 4. **Confusing UNIQUE constraint with PRIMARY KEY** — UNIQUE allows one NULL in standard SQL; PRIMARY KEY does not. Marks lost if this distinction is muddled. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Option 1* **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]** Consider the following schema for a Library Management System: ```sql CREATE TABLE AUTHOR ( author_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, author_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE BOOK ( book_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, title VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, price NUMERIC(8,2) CHECK (price > 0), author_id INTEGER, FOREIGN KEY (author_id) REFERENCES AUTHOR(author_id) ON DELETE CASCADE ); ``` **(a)** Write **SQL queries** to validate the following (7 marks): 1. Entity integrity of BOOK 2. Referential integrity between BOOK and AUTHOR 3. Domain integrity of price **(b)** Suppose the schema was decomposed from an unnormalized form where the original functional dependency was `book_id → title, price, author_id, author_name`. Write queries to **verify that the decomposition is lossless** and that **no transitive dependency remains** in the BOOK relation. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) — Part 1: Entity Integrity of BOOK (2 marks)** ```sql -- Test 1: Attempt to insert NULL primary key INSERT INTO BOOK (book_id, title, price) VALUES (NULL, 'Test', 100); -- Expected: ERROR - null value in column "book_id" violates not-null constraint -- Test 2: Attempt to insert duplicate primary key INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (1, 'DBMS', 500, 101); INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (1, 'OS', 600, 102); -- Expected: ERROR - duplicate key value violates unique constraint "book_pkey" -- Test 3: Audit query to detect pre-existing violations SELECT book_id, COUNT(*) FROM BOOK GROUP BY book_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1; -- Expected: 0 rows ``` **(a) — Part 2: Referential Integrity (3 marks)** ```sql -- Test 1: Insert child with non-existent parent INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (10, 'Networks', 450, 999); -- Expected: ERROR - insert or update on table "book" violates -- foreign key constraint "book_author_id_fkey" -- Test 2: ON DELETE CASCADE behaviour INSERT INTO AUTHOR VALUES (101, 'Navathe'); INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (1, 'DBMS', 500, 101); INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (2, 'OS', 600, 101); SELECT COUNT(*) FROM BOOK WHERE author_id = 101; -- Expected: 2 (parent and children exist) DELETE FROM AUTHOR WHERE author_id = 101; -- Expected: SUCCEEDS SELECT COUNT(*) FROM BOOK WHERE author_id = 101; -- Expected: 0 (CASCADE deleted children) -- Test 3: Audit query for orphan records SELECT b.book_id FROM BOOK b LEFT JOIN AUTHOR a ON b.author_id = a.author_id WHERE a.author_id IS NULL; -- Expected: 0 rows ``` **(a) — Part 3: Domain Integrity of price (2 marks)** ```sql -- Test 1: Negative price INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (20, 'Invalid', -50, 102); -- Expected: ERROR - violates check constraint "book_price_check" -- Test 2: Zero price (boundary) INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (21, 'Free', 0, 102); -- Expected: ERROR - violates check constraint (price > 0) -- Test 3: Audit price range SELECT MIN(price), MAX(price), AVG(price) FROM BOOK; -- All values must satisfy price > 0 ``` **(b) — Lossless Join & Transitive Dependency Verification (7 marks)** **Step 1: Lossless Join Test (3.5 marks)** For decomposition $\{BOOK, AUTHOR\}$ to be lossless with respect to the join attribute `author_id`, the following must hold: $$|BOOK \bowtie AUTHOR| = |R_{original}|$$ Since the original table R is no longer present, we verify using the **intersection property**: $$BOOK \cap AUTHOR = \{author\_id\}$$ And the constraint that `author_id → AUTHOR` (i.e., `author_id` is the primary key of AUTHOR). Since `author_id` is a key in AUTHOR, by the **Heath's Theorem**, the decomposition is lossless. **Verification Query:** ```sql -- Insert sample data INSERT INTO AUTHOR VALUES (201, 'Silberschatz'); INSERT INTO AUTHOR VALUES (202, 'Korth'); INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (1, 'DBMS Concepts', 750, 201); INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (2, 'OS Principles', 680, 202); INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (3, 'Networks', 550, 201); -- Lossless join verification SELECT COUNT(*) AS joined_rows FROM BOOK NATURAL JOIN AUTHOR; -- Expected: 3 (one row per book, no inflation, no loss) ``` If the join produces **more rows** than the count of BOOK, the decomposition is lossy. If it produces **fewer rows**, some data is being lost. **Step 2: Transitive Dependency Removal Test (3.5 marks)** Original FD: `book_id → author_id → author_name` (transitive, violates 3NF). After decomposition, `author_name` should be **fully dependent on `author_id`** in AUTHOR, and **not present at all** in BOOK. ```sql -- Test 1: Verify author_name is NOT in BOOK (proves decomposition) SELECT column_name FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_name = 'book' AND column_name = 'author_name'; -- Expected: 0 rows -- Test 2: Verify FD author_id -> author_name in AUTHOR SELECT author_id, COUNT(DISTINCT author_name) AS names FROM AUTHOR GROUP BY author_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT author_name) > 1; -- Expected: 0 rows (proves FD holds) -- Test 3: Verify no transitive dependency remains in BOOK -- (i.e., no non-key attribute in BOOK determines another non-key attribute) SELECT author_id, COUNT(DISTINCT title) AS title_count FROM BOOK GROUP BY author_id; -- In BOOK, this should equal the count of books per author (1:M is allowed) -- But within a single book, title and price must be unique to book_id SELECT book_id, COUNT(DISTINCT title) FROM BOOK GROUP BY book_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT title) > 1; -- Expected: 0 rows ``` **Conclusion:** All 3NF and lossless join requirements are satisfied. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Option 2* **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]** For the schema given in Question A, answer the following: **(a)** Write SQL queries to perform **comprehensive schema validation** using `information_schema` views. List the structural checks you would perform, and explain how you would detect: (i) missing PRIMARY KEY declarations, (ii) foreign keys with no matching parent index, (iii) CHECK constraints with contradictory conditions. (7 marks) **(b)** Demonstrate how you would use **INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations** to test the **cascade behavior** of the foreign key. What specific test scenarios would you design to expose bugs in the cascade implementation? (7 marks) --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) — Information Schema Validation Queries (7 marks)** **(i) Missing PRIMARY KEY detection (2.5 marks):** ```sql -- Find all user-defined tables that have NO primary key declared SELECT t.table_name FROM information_schema.tables t LEFT JOIN information_schema.table_constraints tc ON t.table_name = tc.table_name AND tc.constraint_type = 'PRIMARY KEY' AND t.table_schema = tc.table_schema WHERE t.table_schema = 'public' AND t.table_type = 'BASE TABLE' AND tc.constraint_name IS NULL; ``` Any row returned indicates a table missing its primary key. Such tables violate **Entity Integrity** and must be flagged. **(ii) Foreign Keys with no matching parent index (2.5 marks):** ```sql -- Find foreign keys whose parent column lacks an index SELECT tc.table_name AS child_table, kcu.column_name AS child_column, ccu.table_name AS parent_table, ccu.column_name AS parent_column FROM information_schema.table_constraints tc JOIN information_schema.key_column_usage kcu ON tc.constraint_name = kcu.constraint_name JOIN information_schema.constraint_column_usage ccu ON tc.constraint_name = ccu.constraint_name WHERE tc.constraint_type = 'FOREIGN KEY' AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM information_schema.statistics s WHERE s.table_name = ccu.table_name AND s.column_name = ccu.column_name ); ``` **Why this matters:** Unindexed parent columns cause **full table scans** during DELETE/UPDATE on parent, drastically slowing referential integrity checks. Most RDBMS engines do NOT auto-index parent PKs in some cases (e.g., MySQL with composite FKs). **(iii) Contradictory CHECK constraints (2 marks):** ```sql -- Retrieve all CHECK constraints SELECT tc.table_name, tc.constraint_name, cc.check_clause FROM information_schema.table_constraints tc JOIN information_schema.check_constraints cc ON tc.constraint_name = cc.constraint_name WHERE tc.constraint_type = 'CHECK' AND tc.table_schema = 'public'; ``` **Contradiction detection** is logical, not just syntactic. Example of a contradictory pair: ```sql CHECK (age >= 18) CHECK (age <= 17) -- Contradicts the first; no row can ever be inserted! ``` The validator must attempt to insert a sample row satisfying all CHECKs. If no such row exists, the constraint set is contradictory. ```sql -- Test: Try to insert any valid row INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (1, 'Test', 100, 101); -- If this fails due to CHECK violation despite valid data, constraints are contradictory ``` **(b) — Cascade Behavior Testing (7 marks)** The schema specifies `ON DELETE CASCADE` from AUTHOR to BOOK. To thoroughly test this: **Test Scenario 1: Delete parent with single child (1.5 marks)** ```sql INSERT INTO AUTHOR VALUES (301, 'Elmasri'); INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (1, 'Fundamentals of DBMS', 800, 301); SELECT COUNT(*) AS books_before FROM BOOK WHERE author_id = 301; -- Expected: 1 DELETE FROM AUTHOR WHERE author_id = 301; -- Expected: 1 row deleted from AUTHOR, 1 row auto-deleted from BOOK SELECT COUNT(*) AS books_after FROM BOOK WHERE author_id = 301; -- Expected: 0 ``` **Test Scenario 2: Delete parent with multiple children (1.5 marks)** ```sql INSERT INTO AUTHOR VALUES (302, 'Ramakrishnan'); INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (2, 'DBMS Management', 700, 302); INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (3, 'Database Systems', 750, 302); INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (4, 'Data Warehousing', 900, 302); SELECT COUNT(*) FROM BOOK WHERE author_id = 302; -- Expected: 3 DELETE FROM AUTHOR WHERE author_id = 302; -- Expected: 3 rows auto-deleted from BOOK (cascade) SELECT COUNT(*) FROM BOOK WHERE author_id = 302; -- Expected: 0 -- Verify no orphans exist SELECT b.book_id FROM BOOK b LEFT JOIN AUTHOR a ON b.author_id = a.author_id WHERE b.author_id = 302 AND a.author_id IS NULL; -- Expected: 0 rows ``` **Test Scenario 3: Cascading chain across 3 levels (2 marks)** Suppose a third table `CHAPTER` references BOOK. The cascade must propagate correctly. ```sql CREATE TABLE CHAPTER ( chapter_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, book_id INTEGER, title VARCHAR(100), FOREIGN KEY (book_id) REFERENCES BOOK(book_id) ON DELETE CASCADE ); INSERT INTO AUTHOR VALUES (303, 'Date'); INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (5, 'DBMS Deep Dive', 850, 303); INSERT INTO CHAPTER VALUES (1, 5, 'Introduction'); INSERT INTO CHAPTER VALUES (2, 5, 'Normalization'); -- Delete grandparent; expect entire chain collapsed DELETE FROM AUTHOR WHERE author_id = 303; -- Expected: BOOK row 5 deleted, CHAPTER rows 1, 2 deleted (transitive cascade) ``` **Test Scenario 4: UPDATE CASCADE on FK (2 marks)** ```sql -- If parent PK is updated, child FKs should auto-update INSERT INTO AUTHOR VALUES (304, 'Ullman'); INSERT INTO BOOK VALUES (10, 'DB Systems', 600, 304); UPDATE AUTHOR SET author_id = 999 WHERE author_id = 304; SELECT * FROM BOOK WHERE book_id = 10; -- Expected: book_id=10, author_id=999 (auto-updated via ON UPDATE CASCADE) ``` **Bug-Exposing Edge Cases:** | **Test** | **Scenario** | **Expected Behaviour** | |---|---|---| | Self-referencing FK | `AUTHOR.author_id` references `AUTHOR.reviewer_id` | Must detect cycles | | NULL FK on DELETE | If FK is NULL and parent deleted | NULL preserved (no cascade on NULL) | | Multiple paths to parent | Two FKs from same child to same parent | Both must cascade independently | | Circular FKs | A → B, B → A | DELETE must respect order or use DEFERRABLE | --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** - ✅ **Entity Integrity** = `PRIMARY KEY` is unique + not NULL. Test with `INSERT NULL PK` and `INSERT duplicate PK`. Both must fail. - ✅ **Referential Integrity** = Every non-NULL FK must match an existing parent PK. Test with `INSERT invalid FK` (must fail) and `DELETE parent with children` (must respect ON DELETE rule). - ✅ **Domain Integrity** = `CHECK` constraints + data types. Test with boundary values (0, -1, MAX_INT, empty string). - ✅ **User-Defined Integrity** = `UNIQUE`, `DEFAULT`, `TRIGGER`. UNIQUE differs from PK: it allows **one NULL** in standard SQL. - ✅ **Information Schema** is the standard metadata view in KTU syllabus. Always use `information_schema.tables`, `information_schema.columns`, `information_schema.table_constraints` for structural audits. - ✅ **Cascade Options to remember**: - `CASCADE` → propagate delete/update - `SET NULL` → set FK to NULL on parent delete - `SET DEFAULT` → set FK to its default value - `NO ACTION` / `RESTRICT` → block the operation - ✅ **Lossless Join Test**: $|R_1 \bowtie R_2| = |R|$ for the original relation R. Use `NATURAL JOIN` and compare counts. - ✅ **FD Verification Pattern**: `SELECT X, COUNT(DISTINCT Y) FROM T GROUP BY X HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT Y) > 1;` returns 0 rows if $X \rightarrow Y$ holds. - ✅ **KTU Expected Query Style**: Always state (a) the **query**, (b) the **expected result** (PASS/FAIL or row count), and (c) the **interpretation** in 1 line. This 3-part answer format fetches full marks. - ✅ **Common KTU trick questions**: "What will happen if we try to insert a row violating the CHECK constraint?" → Answer: "The DBMS will raise a SQLSTATE error and **reject the entire tuple** (atomicity). No partial insertion occurs." - ✅ **Module 4 Context**: This topic bridges **schema design** (Modules 1–3) with **advanced features** (triggers, views, cursors in Module 5). Validation queries are often combined with trigger questions in 14-mark problems. - ✅ **Real-world parallel**: This is exactly how tools like *SQL Server Data Tools (SSDT)*, *pgTAP*, and *Oracle SQL Developer Data Modeler* perform automated schema validation in production CI/CD pipelines. ---

topics.title: Integration with Applications: Develop simple applications (e.g., using Python) to interact with the database
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT304 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Integration with Applications: Develop simple applications (e.g., using Python) to interact with the database

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Database Application Integration** is the process of establishing a communication channel between a high-level general-purpose programming language (host language) and a Database Management System (DBMS) such that the application can issue **Data Definition Language (DDL)**, **Data Manipulation Language (DML)**, and **Transaction Control Language (TCL)** statements, fetch result sets, handle exceptions, and maintain ACID properties programmatically.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PBITT304), the canonical reference for this integration is the **Python Database API Specification (PEP 249)**, often simply called **DB-API 2.0**. It standardizes the way Python modules providing database connectivity expose their functionality, ensuring that the same client code works across different relational back-ends (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, Oracle) by simply swapping the *driver* (e.g., `mysql-connector-python`, `psycopg2`, `sqlite3`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The student must be able to write a complete, self-contained Python program that:
> 1. Imports a database driver module.
> 2. Establishes a `Connection` object.
> 3. Creates a `Cursor` object.
> 4. Executes parameterized SQL queries (`execute`, `executemany`).
> 5. Fetches results (`fetchone`, `fetchall`, `fetchmany`).
> 6. Commits / rolls back the transaction.
> 7. Closes the cursor and the connection.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Waiter in a Restaurant

Imagine you are sitting in a restaurant (your **Python Application**). You want food from the kitchen (your **Database Server**). You cannot walk into the kitchen because the chef speaks a different language (raw TCP socket protocols). So you call the **Waiter** (the **Database Driver / Adapter**). The waiter:

1. Takes your **order** (your SQL query written in Python).
2. Walks to the kitchen, hands the order to the chef, and waits.
3. The chef prepares the dish (executes the query against the data files).
4. The waiter brings the dish back to your table (the **Result Set**).
5. The waiter also brings the **bill** (metadata: row count, column descriptions, status messages).

In this analogy:
- **You** = Python program
- **Waiter** = DB-API compliant driver (`pymysql`, `sqlite3`, `psycopg2`)
- **Order slip** = SQL statement string
- **Kitchen** = DBMS engine
- **Dish** = Result set (rows of tuples)
- **Bill** = Cursor description and rowcount

If you change restaurants, the kitchen changes (MySQL → PostgreSQL), but the *protocol* with the waiter (DB-API 2.0) stays exactly the same. This **abstraction** is the central engineering virtue of the DB-API specification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Foundational Objects in DB-API 2.0:**
> - **`Connection` Object** — Represents the live session/pipe to the DB. It manages transactions, isolation level, and is the *factory* for cursors.
> - **`Cursor` Object** — A pointer/abstraction over the result set. It is the actual conduit that carries your SQL into the engine and brings rows back.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Lifecycle of a Database Application

Every Python program that talks to a relational database passes through the same five logical phases. Skipping any one of them leads to either a runtime error or a resource leak (an "open connection" that survives the program and eventually exhausts the DB's connection pool).

### Phase 1 — Driver Import & Connection Establishment
The driver is imported, and a `Connection` object is instantiated. At this stage, authentication (username, password, host, port, database name) and connection-pool negotiation occur.

### Phase 2 — Cursor Creation
A cursor is acquired from the connection. In a multi-cursor scenario, each cursor is an *independent* transactional context within the same connection.

### Phase 3 — Query Execution
The SQL statement is sent through the cursor. If the SQL contains user-supplied data, **parameterized queries (`%s` placeholders)** are mandatory to prevent **SQL Injection**, which is the most common vulnerability listed in OWASP Top 10.

### Phase 4 — Result Retrieval / Persistence
- For `SELECT`: call `fetchone()`, `fetchmany(n)`, or `fetchall()`.
- For `INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE`: call `connection.commit()` to persist changes.

### Phase 5 — Cleanup (Resource Release)
Always close the cursor and connection — preferably using a `with` block (context manager) so that `__exit__` handles cleanup even on exceptions.

## 2.2 KTU Reference Sheet — Connection & Cursor API

| Method / Attribute | Belongs To | Purpose & Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| `connect(host, user, passwd, db)` | Module-level | Returns a new `Connection` object. Opens a network/auth session with the RDBMS. |
| `connection.cursor()` | Connection | Returns a new `Cursor` object bound to this connection. |
| `connection.commit()` | Connection | Atomically persists all DML statements issued since the last `commit()` or `rollback()`. |
| `connection.rollback()` | Connection | Undoes all uncommitted work — used to recover from a mid-transaction error. |
| `connection.close()` | Connection | Closes the network socket; after this, any cursor created from it raises `ProgrammingError`. |
| `cursor.execute(sql, params)` | Cursor | Compiles and runs a single SQL statement. Uses `params` to safely bind values. |
| `cursor.executemany(sql, seq)` | Cursor | Bulk execution — used for batch inserts/updates. Highly efficient for $>1000$ rows. |
| `cursor.fetchone()` | Cursor | Returns the next row as a **tuple**, or `None` when exhausted. |
| `cursor.fetchall()` | Cursor | Returns **all remaining rows** as a list of tuples. Memory-heavy for large result sets. |
| `cursor.fetchmany(size)` | Cursor | Returns a list of at most `size` rows — ideal for pagination. |
| `cursor.rowcount` | Cursor (attribute) | Number of rows affected by the last DML/DQL. Read-only **integer**. |
| `cursor.description` | Cursor (attribute) | A sequence of 7-tuples `(name, type_code, display_size, internal_size, precision, scale, null_ok)`. |
| `cursor.close()` | Cursor | Releases server-side resources. |
| `with connection:` | Context manager | Auto-commits on success, auto-rolls-back on exception. **Best practice.** |

## 2.3 Parameterized Query — The SQL Injection Defense

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Security Concept (Frequently tested in KTU):**
> Never construct SQL by string concatenation with user input. The DB-API `%s` placeholder is *not* Python's `%` formatting; it is a **driver-level bind variable** that is sent to the server separately from the SQL text, so user input can never be misinterpreted as SQL syntax.

**Wrong (vulnerable):**

$$
\text{sql} = \text{"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '"} + \text{user\_input} + \text{"'"}
$$

If `user_input = "'; DROP TABLE users; --"`, the entire table is destroyed.

**Correct (safe):**

$$
\text{sql} = \text{"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = %s"}
$$
$$
\text{cursor.execute(sql, (user\_input,))}
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Domain | Integration Pattern |
|---|---|
| **E-Commerce** | Python (Django/Flask) backend ↔ MySQL/PostgreSQL for cart, inventory, and order processing. |
| **Banking & FinTech** | Python scripts execute parameterized batch transfers via `executemany`; ACID compliance is enforced by `commit()`. |
| **Data Science** | `pandas.read_sql(query, connection)` loads a result set directly into a DataFrame for analytics. |
| **IoT & Telemetry** | Python on edge devices buffers sensor readings and flushes them to a time-series DB. |
| **Web APIs (REST)** | Flask/FastAPI route handlers call a connection pool to serve JSON responses from SQL. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical SQLite Example — A Complete Working Program

SQLite is bundled with Python's standard library (module `sqlite3`), so it is the *de-facto* teaching vehicle in KTU lab exams. The following program performs the complete **CRUD** cycle on a `students` table.

### Step 1 — Import the Driver

```python
import sqlite3
```

The standard library ships with `sqlite3` as a DB-API 2.0 compliant module. No `pip install` is required. For other databases, you would write `import mysql.connector` or `import psycopg2`.

### Step 2 — Establish the Connection

```python
conn = sqlite3.connect("university.db")
```

If `university.db` does not exist, SQLite **creates** it on disk. The argument is the *data source name (DSN)*. For server-based DBs, the call would look like `mysql.connector.connect(host="localhost", user="root", passwd="...", database="ktu_exam")`.

### Step 3 — Acquire a Cursor

```python
cur = conn.cursor()
```

The cursor is the workhorse object — every SQL command is dispatched through it.

### Step 4 — Execute DDL (Create Table)

```python
cur.execute("""
    CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS students (
        roll_no   INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
        name      TEXT    NOT NULL,
        cgpa      REAL    CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0 AND 10)
    )
""")
```

`IF NOT EXISTS` makes the script **idempotent** — running it twice does not raise an error.

### Step 5 — Insert Rows with Parameterized Binding

```python
sql = "INSERT INTO students (roll_no, name, cgpa) VALUES (?, ?, ?)"
records = [
    (101, "Anand",  8.7),
    (102, "Bhavna", 9.1),
    (103, "Chitra", 7.8),
]
cur.executemany(sql, records)
conn.commit()
```

> [!NOTE]
> Note the placeholder symbol: SQLite uses `?`, while MySQL uses `%s`, and psycopg2 (PostgreSQL) uses `%s`. This is a *driver convention* — the DB-API specification does not standardize the placeholder, only the *mechanism*.

### Step 6 — Query and Fetch

```python
cur.execute("SELECT roll_no, name, cgpa FROM students WHERE cgpa >= 8.0")
print("Column metadata:", cur.description)
for row in cur.fetchall():
    print(row)
```

The output is a list of **tuples** like `(101, 'Anand', 8.7)`. To get dictionaries instead, instantiate the cursor as `conn.cursor(row_factory=sqlite3.Row)`.

### Step 7 — Update

```python
cur.execute("UPDATE students SET cgpa = ? WHERE roll_no = ?", (8.9, 101))
conn.commit()
print("Rows modified:", cur.rowcount)
```

### Step 8 — Delete

```python
cur.execute("DELETE FROM students WHERE roll_no = ?", (103,))
conn.commit()
```

### Step 9 — Use a Context Manager (Best Practice)

```python
with sqlite3.connect("university.db") as conn:
    cur = conn.cursor()
    cur.execute("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM students")
    print("Total rows:", cur.fetchone()[0])
    # __exit__ will auto-commit (or rollback on exception) AND close the connection
```

### Step 10 — Explicit Cleanup

```python
cur.close()
conn.close()
```

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection by Use Case

| Use Case | Recommended DB-API Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Inserting one row from a web form | `cursor.execute(sql, params)` | Simplest, single-statement, no overhead of preparing a batch. |
| Bulk-loading a CSV (10,000+ rows) | `cursor.executemany(sql, list_of_tuples)` | One parse, one network round-trip, exponential speedup over looping `execute`. |
| Verifying a login credential | `cursor.execute()` + `cursor.fetchone()` | Memory-efficient — we only need 0 or 1 row. |
| Building a paginated admin dashboard | Loop of `cursor.fetchmany(50)` | Streams the result set in chunks; constant memory regardless of total row count. |
| Exporting the whole table to JSON | `cursor.execute()` + `cursor.fetchall()` | Acceptable for small tables; never for tables $>10^6$ rows. |
| Detecting the number of affected rows | `cursor.rowcount` (attribute) | Read **after** `execute`/`executemany`, before the next statement. |

## 3.3 Mathematical Model of Network Round-Trips

Let $N$ be the number of rows to insert and $R$ the network round-trip latency (in seconds). Compare the two strategies:

**Strategy A — Loop of `execute`:**
$$
T_A = N \cdot (R + T_{parse}) \approx N \cdot R \quad \text{when } T_{parse} \ll R
$$

**Strategy B — Single `executemany`:**
$$
T_B = R + N \cdot T_{bind} \approx R \quad \text{when } T_{bind} \ll R
$$

$$
\therefore \frac{T_A}{T_B} \approx N
$$

So a 5,000-row insert via looping is roughly **5,000×** slower than a single `executemany`. This is the single most important performance lesson in database integration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:**
> In the 14-mark question on Python–DB integration, full marks are awarded only when the student uses `with conn:` (context manager) and `executemany` for bulk operations. Marks are deducted for `string +` concatenation and missing `commit()`.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Architecture of a Python–Database Application

The diagram below models the layered request flow from the moment a user interacts with the application until data is persisted or retrieved.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User Interface: CLI or Web Form"] --> B["Python Application Layer: Business Logic"]
    B --> C["DB-API 2.0 Driver: mysql-connector or sqlite3"]
    C --> D["Database Engine: MySQL or SQLite Server"]
    D --> E["Physical Storage: Disk Based Data Files and Index Pages"]
    E --> D
    D --> C
    C --> B
    B --> A
    F["Result Set: List of Tuples"] --> B
    G["Connection Pool: Reusable Connection Objects"] --> C
    B --> G
    H["Error Handler: try-except-finally Block"] --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**

1. The **User Interface** captures input.
2. The **Python Application Layer** holds business rules and orchestrates flow.
3. The **DB-API 2.0 Driver** translates Python calls into the DB's wire protocol.
4. The **Database Engine** parses, optimizes, and executes the SQL.
5. **Physical Storage** holds rows and B-tree index pages.
6. The reverse path returns a **Result Set** to Python.
7. A **Connection Pool** caches open connections to avoid repeated handshakes.
8. An **Error Handler** ensures transactional integrity on failure.

## 4.2 State Diagram of the Cursor Object

The cursor is a stateful object. Understanding its state transitions is a frequent KTU short-answer topic.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Fresh: cursor = conn.cursor()
    Fresh --> Executing: execute or executemany
    Executing --> ResultAvailable: SELECT statement
    Executing --> NoResult: INSERT UPDATE DELETE
    ResultAvailable --> Iterating: fetchone or fetchmany or fetchall
    Iterating --> ResultAvailable: more rows remain
    Iterating --> Exhausted: fetchone returns None
    NoResult --> Fresh: implicit reset on next execute
    Exhausted --> Fresh: implicit reset on next execute
    Fresh --> Closed: cursor.close
    Closed --> [*]
```

**State descriptions:**

- **Fresh** — Cursor created, no SQL sent yet.
- **Executing** — The driver has shipped SQL to the server.
- **ResultAvailable** — Server returned a result set; rows are buffered client-side.
- **Iterating** — Python is reading tuples from the buffer.
- **Exhausted** — All rows consumed; further `fetchone` returns `None`.
- **Closed** — Server resources released; any further method call raises `ProgrammingError`.

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — A Single SELECT Round-Trip

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Python App
    participant Drv as DB-API Driver
    participant DB as Database Server
    App->>Drv: conn = connect(host, user, pwd, db)
    Drv->>DB: TCP handshake and authentication
    DB-->>Drv: OK session established
    Drv-->>App: Connection object
    App->>Drv: cur = conn.cursor()
    Drv-->>App: Cursor object
    App->>Drv: cur.execute(SELECT, params)
    Drv->>DB: Send prepared statement
    DB-->>Drv: Result set (rows)
    Drv-->>App: Buffered tuples
    App->>Drv: rows = cur.fetchall()
    Drv-->>App: list of tuples
    App->>Drv: conn.close()
    Drv->>DB: Send QUIT
    DB-->>Drv: Connection terminated
```
