# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**"List and briefly explain any three real-world domains suitable for a DBMS mini-project, with one example entity and its attributes for each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Domain | Example Entity | Sample Attributes |
|---|---|---|
| Hospital Management | `Patient` | PatientID, Name, DOB, BloodGroup, Phone |
| Library Management | `Book` | BookID, ISBN, Title, Publisher, Year, Price |
| E-Commerce | `Product` | ProductID, Name, Category, Price, StockQty |

Each domain above supports at least 8 tables, normalization, and triggers — meeting KTU expectations. *(3 marks: 1 per domain with entity + 1 attribute list)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]**
**"What are the essential deliverables expected in a KTU DBMS mini-project report?"**

**Model Answer:**

A complete KTU DBMS mini-project report must contain the following deliverables:

1. **Synopsis** — problem statement, scope, objectives *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Software Requirement Specification (SRS)** — functional and non-functional requirements *(0.5 mark)*
3. **ER Diagram and Relational Schema** — conceptual and logical design *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Normalization Proof** — conversion from 1NF to 3NF *(0.5 mark)*
5. **DDL/DML Script File** — table creation and data insertion *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Front-end Source Code + Screenshots** *(0.5 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"You are assigned the mini-project 'Online Book Store Management System'. Identify all major entities, their attributes, and the relationships among them. Draw the ER diagram and convert it into a relational schema."**

**Model Solution:**

**Entities & Attributes:**

1. `Book(BookID, ISBN, Title, Price, StockQty, PublisherID, CategoryID)`
2. `Author(AuthorID, Name, Bio)`
3. `Publisher(PublisherID, Name, Address, Phone)`
4. `Category(CategoryID, CategoryName)`
5. `Customer(CustomerID, Name, Email, Phone, Address)`
6. `Order(OrderID, OrderDate, CustomerID, TotalAmount)`
7. `OrderItem(OrderID, BookID, Quantity, UnitPrice)` — bridge for M:N
8. `Cart(CartID, CustomerID, Date)`
9. `Payment(PaymentID, OrderID, Amount, Mode, Status)`
10. `Review(ReviewID, CustomerID, BookID, Rating, Comment)`

**Relationships:**

| Relationship | Cardinality | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Customer — Order | 1 : M | One customer places many orders |
| Order — Book | M : N | Resolved by `OrderItem` |
| Book — Author | M : N | Resolved by `Book_Author(BookID, AuthorID)` |
| Book — Publisher | M : 1 | Many books from one publisher |
| Book — Category | M : 1 | Many books in one category |
| Order — Payment | 1 : 1 | Each order has one payment |
| Customer — Review | 1 : M | One customer posts many reviews |
| Book — Review | 1 : M | One book has many reviews |

**Relational Schema (with primary keys underlined):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Book}(\underline{\text{BookID}},\ \text{ISBN},\ \text{Title},\ \text{Price},\ \text{StockQty},\ \text{PublisherID},\ \text{CategoryID}) \\
&\text{Author}(\underline{\text{AuthorID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{Bio}) \\
&\text{Book\_Author}(\underline{\text{BookID}},\ \underline{\text{AuthorID}}) \\
&\text{Publisher}(\underline{\text{PublisherID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{Address},\ \text{Phone}) \\
&\text{Category}(\underline{\text{CategoryID}},\ \text{CategoryName}) \\
&\text{Customer}(\underline{\text{CustomerID}},\ \text{Name},\ \text{Email},\ \text{Phone},\ \text{Address}) \\
&\text{Order}(\underline{\text{OrderID}},\ \text{OrderDate},\ \text{CustomerID},\ \text{TotalAmount}) \\
&\text{OrderItem}(\underline{\text{OrderID}},\ \underline{\text{BookID}},\ \text{Quantity},\ \text{UnitPrice}) \\
&\text{Payment}(\underline{\text{PaymentID}},\ \text{OrderID},\ \text{Amount},\ \text{Mode},\ \text{Status}) \\
&\text{Review}(\underline{\text{ReviewID}},\ \text{CustomerID},\ \text{BookID},\ \text{Rating},\ \text{Comment})
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks: 3 marks for entity-attribute identification, 2 marks for relationships with cardinalities, 2 marks for relational schema conversion)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"For the above Online Book Store, write SQL DDL for the `Book` and `OrderItem` tables with all constraints, a trigger to update `StockQty` automatically when a new order item is inserted, and a view showing the top 5 selling books."**

**Model Solution:**

**DDL — Book Table:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Book (
    BookID INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    ISBN VARCHAR(13) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    Title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    Price DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (Price > 0),
    StockQty INT DEFAULT 0 CHECK (StockQty >= 0),
    PublisherID INT NOT NULL,
    CategoryID INT NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (PublisherID) REFERENCES Publisher(PublisherID) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (CategoryID) REFERENCES Category(CategoryID) ON DELETE SET NULL
);
```

**DDL — OrderItem Table:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE OrderItem (
    OrderID INT,
    BookID INT,
    Quantity INT CHECK (Quantity > 0),
    UnitPrice DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (UnitPrice > 0),
    PRIMARY KEY (OrderID, BookID),
    FOREIGN KEY (OrderID) REFERENCES `Order`(OrderID) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (BookID) REFERENCES Book(BookID) ON DELETE RESTRICT
);
```

**Trigger — Auto-decrement stock on order:**

```sql
DELIMITER //
CREATE TRIGGER trg_UpdateStock
AFTER INSERT ON OrderItem
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    UPDATE Book
    SET StockQty = StockQty - NEW.Quantity
    WHERE BookID = NEW.BookID;
END //
DELIMITER ;
```

**View — Top 5 Selling Books:**

```sql
CREATE VIEW vw_Top5Books AS
SELECT b.BookID, b.Title, SUM(oi.Quantity) AS TotalSold
FROM Book b
JOIN OrderItem oi ON b.BookID = oi.BookID
GROUP BY b.BookID, b.Title
ORDER BY TotalSold DESC
LIMIT 5;
```

*(7 marks: 2 marks for DDL with constraints, 2 marks for trigger, 2 marks for view, 1 mark for proper syntax and indentation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Propose a DBMS mini-project for a 'Blood Bank Management System'. List its entities, attributes, primary keys, and explain any three business rules that must be enforced through triggers or constraints."**

**Model Solution:**

**Entities:**

1. `Donor(DonorID, Name, Age, BloodGroup, Phone, LastDonationDate)`
2. `Recipient(RecipientID, Name, Hospital, DoctorRef, RequiredDate)`
3. `BloodUnit(UnitID, DonorID, BloodGroup, CollectionDate, ExpiryDate, Status)`
4. `Request(RequestID, RecipientID, BloodGroup, Quantity, RequestDate)`
5. `Issue(IssueID, UnitID, RecipientID, IssueDate, IssuedBy)`
6. `InventoryCheck(CheckID, CheckDate, UnitID, VerifiedBy)`

**Business Rules (enforced via triggers):**

**Rule 1 — Donor Age Validation:** A donor's age must be between 18 and 65.

```sql
ALTER TABLE Donor
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_age CHECK (Age BETWEEN 18 AND 65);
```

**Rule 2 — Stock Expiry Auto-Update:** Any blood unit with `ExpiryDate < CURDATE()` must be marked `'Expired'`.

```sql
CREATE TRIGGER trg_ExpireBlood
BEFORE INSERT ON BloodUnit
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    IF NEW.ExpiryDate <= NEW.CollectionDate THEN
        SIGNAL SQLSTATE '45000'
        SET MESSAGE_TEXT = 'Expiry must be after collection';
    END IF;
END;
```

**Rule 3 — No Double Issue:** A `BloodUnit` can be issued to at most one recipient.

```sql
CREATE TRIGGER trg_PreventDoubleIssue
BEFORE INSERT ON Issue
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    DECLARE cnt INT;
    SELECT COUNT(*) INTO cnt FROM Issue WHERE UnitID = NEW.UnitID;
    IF cnt > 0 THEN
        SIGNAL SQLSTATE '45000'
        SET MESSAGE_TEXT = 'Blood unit already issued';
    END IF;
END;
```

*(7 marks: 2 marks for entities, 2 marks for attributes/keys, 3 marks for trigger explanations)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"For the Blood Bank project, write SQL for: (i) a stored procedure to fulfill a blood request by allocating the oldest non-expired matching unit, (ii) a view showing current blood-group-wise stock, and (iii) a query to list donors who are eligible to donate today (last donation > 90 days ago)."**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Stored Procedure:**

```sql
DELIMITER //
CREATE PROCEDURE sp_FulfillRequest(IN reqID INT)
BEGIN
    DECLARE reqGroup VARCHAR(5);
    DECLARE reqQty INT;
    DECLARE allocatedUnit INT;

    SELECT BloodGroup, Quantity INTO reqGroup, reqQty
    FROM Request WHERE RequestID = reqID;

    SELECT UnitID INTO allocatedUnit
    FROM BloodUnit
    WHERE BloodGroup = reqGroup
      AND Status = 'Available'
      AND ExpiryDate > CURDATE()
    ORDER BY CollectionDate ASC
    LIMIT 1;

    IF allocatedUnit IS NOT NULL THEN
        INSERT INTO Issue(UnitID, RecipientID, IssueDate, IssuedBy)
        VALUES (allocatedUnit,
                (SELECT RecipientID FROM Request WHERE RequestID = reqID),
                CURDATE(), USER());
        UPDATE BloodUnit SET Status = 'Issued' WHERE UnitID = allocatedUnit;
    END IF;
END //
DELIMITER ;
```

**(ii) View — Stock Summary:**

```sql
CREATE VIEW vw_StockByGroup AS
SELECT BloodGroup, COUNT(*) AS AvailableUnits
FROM BloodUnit
WHERE Status = 'Available' AND ExpiryDate > CURDATE()
GROUP BY BloodGroup;
```

**(iii) Eligible Donors Query:**

```sql
SELECT DonorID, Name, Phone, LastDonationDate
FROM Donor
WHERE DATEDIFF(CURDATE(), LastDonationDate) > 90
   OR LastDonationDate IS NULL;
```

*(7 marks: 3 marks for procedure, 2 marks for view, 2 marks for query)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Choosing an over-simple project (≤ 4 tables):** The examiner will mark it down as "insufficient complexity". Always aim for **8–12 tables**.
> 2. **Skipping the normalization proof:** Listing tables in 3NF *without showing the derivation* costs 2–3 marks. Always show 1NF → 2NF → 3NF explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting foreign-key constraints:** Many students write `PRIMARY KEY` but forget `FOREIGN KEY` with `REFERENCES`. This is the #1 deduction point.
> 4. **No trigger or view:** KTU mandates at least **one trigger** and **one view**. Projects without these lose marks.
> 5. **Single-page front-end:** The front-end must have **login + CRUD + at least one report**. A single form with no validation is not acceptable.
> 6. **No sample data:** Tables must be populated with **realistic test data** (minimum 20 rows in main tables) for demonstration.
> 7. **Inconsistent naming convention:** Mixing `camelCase` and `snake_case` in SQL (`patientId` vs `patient_id`) creates confusion. Stick to **snake_case** throughout.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **KTU DBMS mini-project** is a *full-stack* software artefact combining **front-end + back-end + database**.
- Always select a project with **8–12 normalized tables** — this is the **Standard Tier** accepted by KTU.
- The **three-tier architecture** (Presentation → Application → Data) is *non-negotiable*.
- Every project MUST include: **ER Diagram, Normalization Proof, DDL+DML Scripts, Triggers, Views, Stored Procedures**.
- **Top 5 Project Recommendations for KTU 2024:**
  1. Hospital Management System
  2. University Exam & Result Processing
  3. Online Book Store
  4. E-Commerce Inventory & Order
  5. Blood Bank Management
- **Normalization must reach 3NF** at minimum; BCNF is a bonus for higher marks.
- **Foreign key constraints with `ON DELETE CASCADE / SET NULL`** are mandatory for referential integrity.
- **Sample data must be loaded** into all tables before the demo.
- The **report must follow KTU format**: Title Page → Certificate → Abstract → Acknowledgement → TOC → Chapters (1–5) → References → Appendices.
- **Project evaluation rubric (typical KTU weightage):** Problem Definition (10%), ER & Schema (25%), Normalization (15%), SQL Implementation (25%), Front-end (15%), Viva-Voce (10%).
- **Avoid these common errors:** trivial projects, missing triggers/views, no foreign keys, no sample data, single-page front-end, missing normalization proof.
- The project **directly maps to CO4** of PBITT304 — *"Design and implement a database application for a real-world problem."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pharmacy Database Management System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT304 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 5
> - **Topic:** Pharmacy Database Management System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Pharmacy Database Management System (Pharma-DBMS)** is a domain-specific, integrated relational database system designed to automate, store, manage, and retrieve all transactional, inventory, regulatory, and clinical data related to the operation of a pharmaceutical retail or wholesale outlet. It models entities such as *Medicines, Batches, Manufacturers, Suppliers, Customers, Prescriptions, Bills, Employees, and Purchase Orders*, enforcing integrity, atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability (ACID) guarantees on every operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (CO5 / Bloom Level: Apply / Create):** A Pharmacy DBMS is the canonical *case-study application* of RDBMS design — it tests the student's ability to perform requirements analysis, build an ER model, derive a normalized relational schema, and author analytical SQL (joins, sub-queries, triggers, views, and stored procedures).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Medicine Cabinet"

Imagine a pharmacist running a busy medical shop. He has:

* Thousands of **medicine strips** stacked in trays.
* A **register book** to log customer bills.
* A **calendar** to check which drugs expire next month.
* A **notebook** for doctor's prescriptions.
* A **ledger** for money owed to suppliers.

Doing all this on paper is slow, error-prone, and risky (a single expired drug sold can cause legal trouble). A **Pharmacy DBMS** is like a **smart digital assistant** that:

| Real-World Action | Database Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Storing a new drug box | `INSERT INTO Batch VALUES (...)` |
| Handing over a strip and noting its price | `INSERT INTO BillItem` + `UPDATE Batch SET Quantity = Quantity - 1` |
| Checking expiry on the wall calendar | `SELECT * FROM Batch WHERE ExpiryDate < DATE_ADD(CURDATE(), INTERVAL 30 DAY)` |
| Verifying a doctor's prescription | `SELECT * FROM Prescription WHERE DoctorID = ?` |
| Summarising daily takings | `SELECT SUM(NetAmount) FROM Bill WHERE DATE(Date) = CURDATE()` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters to the KTU examiner:** The examiner does not expect you to know pharmacy law — they expect a clean **ER → Schema → Normalization → SQL** pipeline applied to a real-world domain. Master this pipeline and you master 80% of the Module 5 marks.

## 1.3 Functional Scope of a Pharmacy DBMS

A robust system must handle six core operational pillars:

1. **Inventory & Batch Control** — Track every strip/syrup bottle to its unique batch, manufacturing date, expiry date, and physical location.
2. **Point-of-Sale (POS) Billing** — Generate GST-compliant invoices with discounts, taxes, and payment-mode records.
3. **Prescription Management** — Maintain a digital record of doctor-issued prescriptions for scheduled and OTC drugs.
4. **Procurement (Purchase Orders)** — Manage supplier orders, GRNs (Goods Received Notes), and reorder thresholds.
5. **Regulatory Compliance & Reporting** — Drug licence auditing, Schedule H/X drug tracking, expiry alerts, and stock statements.
6. **HR & Security** — Employee roles (Pharmacist vs. Cashier), salary records, and role-based access control.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Architecture (Why Each Entity Exists)

The pharmacy domain is fundamentally a **batch-traceable inventory** problem. Unlike a grocery shop where "Parle-G Biscuit 1 kg" is one SKU, in a pharmacy the *same medicine* can exist in 10 different batches with different expiry dates. This forces the design to keep a separate **Batch** table.

### 2.1.1 Entity Justification

* **Medicine** — Master data; defines what the drug *is* (composition, manufacturer, category).
* **Batch** — Transactional data; defines a specific *lot* of that medicine with its own expiry and quantity.
* **Supplier** — Who delivered the batch; required for recall and payment reconciliation.
* **Manufacturer** — Who *made* the drug (separate from supplier who distributes it).
* **Category** — Analytic grouping: Analgesic, Antibiotic, Antacid, etc. — powers sales reports.
* **Customer** — A patient or walk-in buyer; needed for loyalty, prescription linkage, and GST invoices.
* **Doctor** — Required for Schedule H/H1 drugs whose sale is *legally contingent* on a valid prescription.
* **Prescription + PrescriptionItem** — A weak entity (existence-dependent on Prescription).
* **Bill + BillItem** — A composite transaction; BillItem is also weak.
* **Employee** — Records who processed the sale (audit trail).
* **PurchaseOrder + PurchaseOrderItem** — Procurement side; mirrors Bill/BillItem structure.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** When asked "Why did you create a separate Batch table?" — answer: *"To satisfy 1NF atomicity and to enable First-Expiry-First-Out (FEFO) dispatch, which is a legal and ethical requirement in pharmaceutical retail."*

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Design Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Rule | KTU Board Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Cardinality (1:1)** | `A \|\|--\|\| B` | Each entity instance maps to exactly one other (e.g., Prescription ↔ Customer visit record) |
| **Cardinality (1:N)** | `A \|\|--o{ B` | One parent, many children (e.g., Category → Medicine) |
| **Cardinality (M:N)** | `A }o--o{ B` | Always resolved via a junction/bridge table (e.g., Medicine ↔ Supplier via Batch) |
| **1NF** | Atomic values, no repeating groups, define PK | Eliminates multi-valued attributes (e.g., multiple phone numbers in one column) |
| **2NF** | 1NF + no partial dependency on a composite PK | Splits junction tables cleanly |
| **3NF** | 2NF + no transitive dependency | Removes derivable columns (e.g., TotalAmount stored when it can be `SUM(Subtotal)`) |
| **BCNF** | 3NF + every determinant is a candidate key | Stronger — needed when 3NF still has anomalies |
| **FEFO Rule** | `ORDER BY ExpiryDate ASC` | Mandatory SQL clause for dispensing |
| **Reorder Level** | `Quantity ≤ ReorderLevel` | Trigger condition for purchase order generation |
| **GST Formula** | `NetAmount = (TotalAmount − Discount) × (1 + TaxRate/100)` | Billing computation |
| **ACID** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | Enforced via `COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK` in transactions |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Hospital Information Systems (HIS):** Pharmacy modules feed the central EHR (Electronic Health Record) — every drug dispensed is logged against the patient's encounter.
* **Cold-Chain Pharma Logistics:** Companies like DHL Life Sciences use this schema to track temperature-sensitive vaccines batch-by-batch.
* **E-Pharmacy Giants (1mg, PharmEasy, Netmeds):** Their backend is essentially a scaled-out Pharmacy DBMS with sharding by pincode and read-replicas for the product catalogue.
* **Government Drug Regulators (CDSCO, FDA):** Use the batch-traceability model for nationwide *drug recall* operations in minutes rather than weeks.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Normalization Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Normalization of a Pharmacy Transaction

Consider a single, un-normalized relation as it might appear in a naive Excel sheet:

**RawTable (Bill_Master)** — `BillNo, BillDate, CustName, CustPhone, DoctorName, MedName, Category, BatchNo, Expiry, Qty, UnitPrice, Total`

This violates **1NF, 2NF, and 3NF** simultaneously. We walk through each Normal Form explicitly.

### 3.1.1 Conversion to 1NF

**Rule:** Every cell must be atomic. The primary key must uniquely identify every row.

**Step 1 — Identify the composite primary key.** Because one bill can have many medicines, the natural key is `(BillNo, BatchNo)`.

**Step 2 — Split repeating groups.** Move each medicine to its own row. Customer and Doctor info are repeated, but that's fine at this stage — we'll address it in 2NF.

The relation is now in 1NF (atomic values, single-valued columns, defined PK).

### 3.1.2 Conversion to 2NF

**Rule:** Every non-key attribute must depend on the *whole* primary key, not just part of it.

**Step 3 — Detect partial dependencies.**
With PK = `(BillNo, BatchNo)`:

* `CustName`, `CustPhone` depend only on `BillNo` (the bill identifies the customer).
* `MedName`, `Category` depend on `BatchNo` (a batch identifies its medicine).
* `UnitPrice` depends on `BatchNo`.
* `Qty` depends on the *combination* (full key).
* `Total` is a derived attribute (will be removed).

**Step 4 — Decompose into 2NF relations.**

$$
R_1 = \text{Bill}(\underline{\text{BillNo}}, \text{BillDate}, \text{CustID}, \text{DoctorID}, \text{EmployeeID})
$$

$$
R_2 = \text{Customer}(\underline{\text{CustID}}, \text{CustName}, \text{CustPhone})
$$

$$
R_3 = \text{Medicine}(\underline{\text{MedicineID}}, \text{MedName}, \text{CategoryID}, \text{UnitPrice})
$$

$$
R_4 = \text{Batch}(\underline{\text{BatchNo}}, \text{MedicineID}, \text{ExpiryDate})
$$

$$
R_5 = \text{BillItem}(\underline{\text{BillNo}}, \underline{\text{BatchNo}}, \text{Qty})
$$

All non-key attributes now fully functionally depend on the whole PK in their respective relations.

### 3.1.3 Conversion to 3NF

**Rule:** Eliminate transitive dependencies (A → B → C).

**Step 6 — Detect transitive dependencies in Medicine.**
`MedicineID` → `CategoryID` → `CategoryName`.
So `CategoryName` is transitively dependent on `MedicineID`.

**Step 7 — Decompose Category out.**

$$
\text{Category}(\underline{\text{CategoryID}}, \text{CategoryName})
$$

$$
\text{Medicine}(\underline{\text{MedicineID}}, \text{MedName}, \text{CategoryID}^{FK}, \text{UnitPrice})
$$

**Step 8 — Remove derived attributes.** The `Total` column in the original bill must *not* be stored — it is a computed value derived from:

$$
\text{Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Qty}_i \times \text{UnitPrice}_i
$$

We compute it at query time using `SUM()` instead of storing it (3NF principle). The final schema is in **Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)** for this scenario, since every determinant is a candidate key.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — SQL Aggregate Functions (Examiner's Favourite)

The following matrix is what the KTU examiner uses to grade SQL aggregation questions:

| Function | Purpose | Pharmacy Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `COUNT(*)` | Number of rows | Total bills generated today |
| `SUM(NetAmount)` | Adds numeric column | Daily revenue |
| `AVG(UnitPrice)` | Arithmetic mean | Average medicine price per category |
| `MIN(ExpiryDate)` | Smallest value | Most-imminent expiry across all batches |
| `MAX(Salary)` | Largest value | Highest-paid pharmacist |
| `GROUP BY` | Bucket rows | Sales grouped by medicine category |
| `HAVING` | Filter after aggregation | Categories with sales > ₹1,00,000 |
| `DATEDIFF(CURDATE(), ExpiryDate)` | Date arithmetic | Days-since-expiry of expired stock |

## 3.3 Worked Example — The FEFO Dispatch Query

**Problem:** *A customer asks for 5 strips of "Dolo 650". The pharmacist must hand over the strips from the batch nearest to expiry (First-Expiry-First-Out).*

**Logical Steps (must be written in the exam):**
1. Identify all batches of MedicineID = 101 with sufficient quantity.
2. Sort them by ExpiryDate ascending.
3. Pick the top row and decrement its quantity.
4. If the top batch is insufficient, combine with the next.

**SQL Implementation:**

```sql
SELECT b.BatchID, b.BatchNumber, b.ExpiryDate, b.Quantity, m.Name
FROM Batch b
JOIN Medicine m ON b.MedicineID = m.MedicineID
WHERE m.Name = 'Dolo 650' AND b.Quantity > 0
ORDER BY b.ExpiryDate ASC
LIMIT 1;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** The examiner awards 2 marks for the `ORDER BY ... ASC` clause. Omitting it (using default order) is a common 2-mark deduction.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Billing Transaction Flow

This diagram maps the **interactions between modules** during a real-world sale, which is the operation KTU questions test most heavily.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer arrives with prescription] --> B{Schedule drug?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Pharmacist verifies Prescription table]
    B -- No --> D[Proceed to billing]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Scan Barcode / Select Medicine]
    E --> F[Query Batch table for stock and expiry]
    F --> G{Batch available and not expired?}
    G -- No --> H[Show out-of-stock alert]
    G -- Yes --> I[FEFO: select batch with nearest ExpiryDate]
    I --> J[Compute Subtotal = Qty x UnitPrice]
    J --> K[Apply discount and GST tax]
    K --> L[INSERT into Bill and BillItem in a TRANSACTION]
    L --> M[UPDATE Batch SET Quantity = Quantity - SoldQty]
    M --> N{Quantity below ReorderLevel?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Generate Purchase Order alert]
    N -- No --> P[Print receipt and COMMIT]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[End of sale]
    H --> Q
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this for the exam:** Steps **L and M** form a single **ACID transaction**. If the bill inserts but the batch update fails, the entire transaction must `ROLLBACK` — otherwise we sell "ghost stock" that doesn't exist. The KTU examiner specifically tests this in the 14-mark questions.

## 4.3 Module Interconnection Map (Logical Tiers)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRESENTATION_TIER [Presentation Tier]
        UI[POS GUI / Web Portal]
    end
    subgraph LOGIC_TIER [Application Tier]
        BILL[Billing Module]
        INV[Inventory Module]
        PRES[Prescription Module]
        REP[Reporting Module]
    end
    subgraph DATA_TIER [Data Tier]
        DB[(Pharmacy RDBMS)]
    end
    UI --> BILL
    UI --> INV
    UI --> PRES
    UI --> REP
    BILL --> DB
    INV --> DB
    PRES --> DB
    REP --> DB
```

This **3-tier architecture** separation is a common KTU short-answer — the data tier is where the Pharmacy DBMS physically lives.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Define a Pharmacy Database Management System. List any four key entities that must be modeled.**

**Model Answer (Examiner Expectation):**
A Pharmacy Database Management System is a computerized relational database system that automates the storage, retrieval, and management of pharmaceutical data including medicines, batches, suppliers, customers, prescriptions, and bills, while ensuring data integrity, security, and regulatory compliance.

Four key entities:
1. **Medicine** — master record of every drug the pharmacy sells.
2. **Batch** — tracks each lot's manufacturing and expiry dates.
3. **Bill / BillItem** — records each sales transaction.
4. **Supplier** — distributor from whom medicines are procured.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**

**Q2. Why is a separate `Batch` table required in a Pharmacy DBMS, even though medicine information is already in the `Medicine` table?**

**Model Answer:**
The same medicine may be present in multiple lots, each with a distinct batch number, manufacturing date, expiry date, purchase price, and current stock level. The `Medicine` table stores *static* product information, whereas the `Batch` table stores *dynamic, transactional* inventory information. Separating them enables First-Expiry-First-Out (FEFO) dispatch, batch-wise stock tracking, and recall of specific lots — all legal requirements in pharmaceutical retail.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting weak entities** — `BillItem` and `PrescriptionItem` are weak entities; they cannot exist without their parent. Always depict them with **double-rectangle** notation and use composite PKs in the schema. Missing this loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Manufacturer with Supplier** — Manufacturer *produces* the drug; Supplier *distributes* it. Many students merge them into one entity. The KTU 2024 paper explicitly tested this distinction.
> 3. **Storing derived columns** — `TotalAmount` in `Bill` should NOT be redundantly stored; compute via `SUM()`. Storing it violates 3NF.
> 4. **Missing `CHECK` constraints** — `ExpiryDate > MfgDate` and `Quantity >= 0` are domain integrity rules. The examiner awards 1 mark for at least one correct `CHECK` constraint.
> 5. **Skipping the FEFO clause** — In any "dispense medicine" query, always use `ORDER BY ExpiryDate ASC LIMIT 1`.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Design-Oriented Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design an Entity-Relationship (ER) diagram for a Pharmacy Database Management System. The system must handle medicines, batches, manufacturers, suppliers, customers, doctors, prescriptions, bills, and employees. State all cardinality constraints clearly.

**Model Solution Outline (What the examiner expects):**
* Draw the ER diagram (see Section 4.1) with at least 10 entities.
* Show the following cardinalities explicitly:
  * `MANUFACTURER 1:N MEDICINE`
  * `CATEGORY 1:N MEDICINE`
  * `MEDICINE 1:N BATCH`
  * `SUPPLIER 1:N BATCH`
  * `CUSTOMER 1:N PRESCRIPTION 1:N PRESCRIPTION_ITEM N:1 MEDICINE`
  * `CUSTOMER 1:N BILL 1:N BILL_ITEM N:1 MEDICINE` and `N:1 BATCH`
  * `DOCTOR 1:N PRESCRIPTION`
  * `EMPLOYEE 1:N BILL`
* Mark `PrescriptionItem` and `BillItem` as **weak entities** with double-bordered rectangles.
* Mark the `BillDate`, `ExpiryDate` as **derived/date attributes** where appropriate.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Convert the above ER diagram into a relational schema. Perform normalization up to **Third Normal Form (3NF)** for the `Bill` and `Prescription` sub-systems. Justify each normalization step.

**Model Solution Outline:**
* List all relations with **Primary Keys (underlined)**, **Foreign Keys (superscript FK)**, and data types.
* Show the un-normalized master table (as in Section 3.1) and walk through 1NF → 2NF → 3NF.
* Justify the removal of `CategoryName` (transitive dependency) and `TotalAmount` (derived attribute).
* Final 3NF schema:

```
Category(CategoryID, CategoryName, Description)
Manufacturer(ManufacturerID, Name, Contact, LicenseNo)
Medicine(MedicineID, Name, BrandName, UnitPrice, DrugType, CategoryID, ManufacturerID)
Supplier(SupplierID, Name, Contact, GSTNo)
Batch(BatchID, BatchNumber, MfgDate, ExpiryDate, Quantity, PurchasePrice, SellingPrice, MedicineID, SupplierID)
Customer(CustomerID, Name, Phone, Address, DOB)
Doctor(DoctorID, Name, Specialization, RegNo, Hospital)
Prescription(PrescriptionID, Date, Notes, DoctorID, CustomerID)
PrescriptionItem(PrescriptionID, MedicineID, Dosage, Duration, Frequency)
Employee(EmployeeID, Name, Role, Salary, Phone)
Bill(BillID, BillDate, TotalAmount, Discount, Tax, NetAmount, PaymentMode, CustomerID, EmployeeID)
BillItem(BillID, MedicineID, BatchID, Quantity, UnitPrice)
PurchaseOrder(OrderID, OrderDate, Status, TotalAmount, SupplierID)
PurchaseOrderItem(OrderID, MedicineID, Quantity, UnitCost)
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Implementation-Oriented Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write the SQL DDL statements to create the tables `Medicine`, `Batch`, `Customer`, `Doctor`, `Prescription`, `PrescriptionItem`, and `Bill` for a Pharmacy DBMS. Include all primary key, foreign key, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, and CHECK constraints.

**Model Solution:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Medicine (
    MedicineID     INT             PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    Name           VARCHAR(100)    NOT NULL,
    BrandName      VARCHAR(100),
    UnitPrice      DECIMAL(10,2)   NOT NULL CHECK (UnitPrice > 0),
    DrugType       VARCHAR(30)     NOT NULL,
    CategoryID     INT             NOT NULL,
    ManufacturerID INT             NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (CategoryID)     REFERENCES Category(CategoryID),
    FOREIGN KEY (ManufacturerID) REFERENCES Manufacturer(ManufacturerID)
);

CREATE TABLE Batch (
    BatchID        INT          PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    BatchNumber    VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    MedicineID     INT          NOT NULL,
    SupplierID     INT          NOT NULL,
    MfgDate        DATE         NOT NULL,
    ExpiryDate     DATE         NOT NULL,
    Quantity       INT          NOT NULL CHECK (Quantity >= 0),
    PurchasePrice  DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
    SellingPrice   DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (MedicineID) REFERENCES Medicine(MedicineID),
    FOREIGN KEY (SupplierID) REFERENCES Supplier(SupplierID),
    CHECK (ExpiryDate > MfgDate)
);

CREATE TABLE Customer (
    CustomerID INT          PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    Name       VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    Phone      VARCHAR(15)  UNIQUE,
    Address    VARCHAR(255),
    DOB        DATE
);

CREATE TABLE Doctor (
    DoctorID      INT          PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    Name          VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    Specialization VARCHAR(80),
    RegNo         VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    Hospital      VARCHAR(100)
);

CREATE TABLE Prescription (
    PrescriptionID INT  PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    DoctorID       INT  NOT NULL,
    CustomerID     INT  NOT NULL,
    Date           DATE NOT NULL DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE),
    Notes          TEXT,
    FOREIGN KEY (DoctorID)   REFERENCES Doctor(DoctorID),
    FOREIGN KEY (CustomerID) REFERENCES Customer(CustomerID)
);

CREATE TABLE PrescriptionItem (
    PrescriptionID INT          NOT NULL,
    MedicineID     INT          NOT NULL,
    Dosage         VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,
    Duration       VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,
    Frequency      VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,
    PRIMARY KEY (PrescriptionID, MedicineID),
    FOREIGN KEY (PrescriptionID) REFERENCES Prescription(PrescriptionID) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (MedicineID)     REFERENCES Medicine(MedicineID)
);

CREATE TABLE Bill (
    BillID        INT           PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    CustomerID    INT,
    EmployeeID    INT           NOT NULL,
    BillDate      DATETIME      NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    TotalAmount   DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
    Discount      DECIMAL(10,2) DEFAULT 0,
    Tax           DECIMAL(10,2) DEFAULT 0,
    NetAmount     DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
    PaymentMode   VARCHAR(20)   NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (CustomerID) REFERENCES Customer(CustomerID),
    FOREIGN KEY (EmployeeID) REFERENCES Employee(EmployeeID)
);
```

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write SQL queries for the following:
**(i)** Find the names of medicines whose batches are expiring within the next 30 days.
**(ii)** Generate a monthly sales report showing total revenue per month.
**(iii)** Find the top 5 medicines by total quantity sold in the last quarter.
**(iv)** Create a trigger that automatically decrements `Batch.Quantity` when a new row is inserted into `BillItem`.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Expiring-soon query:**
```sql
SELECT DISTINCT m.Name, b.BatchNumber, b.ExpiryDate
FROM Medicine m
JOIN Batch b ON m.MedicineID = b.MedicineID
WHERE b.ExpiryDate BETWEEN CURDATE() AND DATE_ADD(CURDATE(), INTERVAL 30 DAY)
  AND b.Quantity > 0
ORDER BY b.ExpiryDate ASC;
```

**(ii) Monthly sales report:**
```sql
SELECT DATE_FORMAT(BillDate, '%Y-%m') AS SalesMonth,
       SUM(NetAmount)                 AS TotalRevenue,
       COUNT(*)                        AS TotalBills
FROM Bill
GROUP BY SalesMonth
ORDER BY SalesMonth DESC;
```

**(iii) Top 5 medicines in the last quarter:**
```sql
SELECT m.Name, SUM(bi.Quantity) AS TotalQtySold
FROM BillItem bi
JOIN Medicine m ON bi.MedicineID = m.MedicineID
JOIN Bill b     ON bi.BillID = b.BillID
WHERE b.BillDate >= DATE_SUB(CURDATE(), INTERVAL 3 MONTH)
GROUP BY m.MedicineID, m.Name
ORDER BY TotalQtySold DESC
LIMIT 5;
```

**(iv) Auto-decrement trigger:**
```sql
DELIMITER //
CREATE TRIGGER trg_DecrementBatch
AFTER INSERT ON BillItem
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    UPDATE Batch
    SET Quantity = Quantity - NEW.Quantity
    WHERE BatchID = NEW.BatchID;
END //
DELIMITER ;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution for Question B(b):** 2 marks per sub-question (i)–(iv) = 8 marks total — slight over-allotment because the trigger typically carries 2.5 marks due to its `DELIMITER` complexity. The examiner rounds down — your goal is to keep each query syntactically perfect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

* **Domain Essence:** A Pharmacy DBMS is fundamentally a *batch-traceable inventory* system; the **Batch** entity is the heart of the design.
* **Two Weak Entities:** `PrescriptionItem` and `BillItem` — always with **composite PKs** and `ON DELETE CASCADE`.
* **Manufacturer ≠ Supplier:** Manufacturer *produces*, Supplier *distributes*. Never merge them.
* **FEFO Rule:** All medicine-dispensing queries MUST include `ORDER BY ExpiryDate ASC LIMIT 1`.
* **Schedule H/H1 Drugs:** Cannot be sold without a valid `Prescription` row — the schema enforces this as a business rule.
* **3NF Discipline:** Never store `TotalAmount` in `Bill` if it is derivable; never store `CategoryName` in `Medicine` (transitive dep).
* **ACID Transaction:** A sale = `INSERT Bill` + `INSERT BillItem` + `UPDATE Batch.Quantity` must be atomic. Use `START TRANSACTION; ... COMMIT;` or a trigger.
* **Domain Constraints (CHECK):** `UnitPrice > 0`, `Quantity >= 0`, `ExpiryDate > MfgDate` — examiners award free marks for these.
* **GST Calculation:** `NetAmount = (TotalAmount − Discount) × (1 + TaxRate/100)` — memorize this for billing questions.
* **Reorder-Level Trigger:** If `Batch.Quantity` falls below `ReorderLevel`, an alert (or auto-PO) must fire.
* **Audit Trail:** The `EmployeeID` foreign key in `Bill` provides accountability for every transaction.
* **Top 5 SQL Patterns to Memorize:** `JOIN` for master-detail, `GROUP BY ... HAVING` for analytics, `DATEDIFF` for expiry, `ORDER BY ... LIMIT` for FEFO, `CREATE TRIGGER` for auto-updates.
* **Normalization Verdict for this case study:** The schema naturally lands in **BCNF**; the examiner is satisfied with **3NF** demonstration.
* **3-Tier Architecture:** Presentation (POS GUI) → Application (PHP/Java logic) → Data (Pharmacy RDBMS) — be ready to label the tiers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Restaurant Database Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT304 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 6
> - **Topic:** Restaurant Database Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Restaurant Database Management System (RDBMS-Restaurant)** is a structured, integrated collection of persistent data describing the operational entities of a food-service enterprise (customers, menu, orders, reservations, staff, inventory, billing) organised according to the relational model, manipulated via the Structured Query Language (SQL), and governed by the ACID transaction properties to ensure consistency under concurrent access and system failure.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, designing a Restaurant Database integrates four sequential competencies:

1. **Conceptual Modelling** – Entity-Relationship (ER) / Enhanced-ER (EER) modelling.
2. **Logical Mapping** – Translation of ER constructs into relational schema with primary, candidate, and foreign keys.
3. **Refinement via Normalisation** – Application of 1NF → 2NF → 3NF → BCNF to eliminate update, insertion, and deletion anomalies.
4. **Physical Implementation & Transaction Control** – DDL/DML scripting, indexing, and ACID-compliant transaction management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 6):** A restaurant case study is the canonical "real-world capstone" used by KTU paper setters to test **schema design, normalisation, complex SQL, and transaction handling** in a single integrated question.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of a restaurant as a **highly dynamic, concurrent battlefield of tiny transactions**:
- A **Waiter** acts like a *session* — opens a "tab" (transaction), issues reads/writes (orders), and commits on payment.
- The **Kitchen Display System** is a *write-intensive buffer* — multiple cooks (transactions) concurrently update `ORDER_ITEM.status`.
- The **Reservation Book** is a *constraint enforcer* — UNIQUE(table_no, date, time_slot) prevents double-booking.
- The **Bill** is a *derived view* — computed by aggregating `ORDER_ITEM` rows joined with `MENU_ITEM.price`.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a head chef insists every ingredient be measured precisely, a relational schema insists every attribute be atomic (1NF). Just as a head chef trains cooks to follow one recipe, normalisation trains designers to follow functional dependencies (FDs).

## 1.3 Primary Entities of a Restaurant Database

| # | Entity | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `CUSTOMER` | Stores diner profiles and contact info |
| 2 | `RESTAURANT_BRANCH` | Multi-outlet chain support |
| 3 | `TABLE_INFO` | Physical tables and seating capacity |
| 4 | `RESERVATION` | Booking records (date, time, party size) |
| 5 | `MENU_CATEGORY` | Starters, Mains, Desserts, Beverages |
| 6 | `MENU_ITEM` | Individual dishes with price |
| 7 | `INGREDIENT` | Raw materials for inventory |
| 8 | `SUPPLIER` | Vendors supplying ingredients |
| 9 | `STAFF` | Chef, Waiter, Manager, Cashier |
| 10 | `ORDER` | Header of a customer order |
| 11 | `ORDER_ITEM` | Line items of an order |
| 12 | `PAYMENT` | Bill settlement record |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard metric used in industry:** Average transaction value (ATV) = Total revenue / Number of orders. Database must support fast aggregation queries on `PAYMENT` joined with `ORDER` for real-time dashboards.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Design Methodology (The "How" and "Why")

### Step 1 — Requirements Gathering
**Why:** Prevents *redundancy* and *missing constraints*.
- Identify business rules:
  - A customer can place **many** orders, but each order belongs to **one** customer.
  - A table can have **at most one** active reservation per time slot.
  - An order must have **at least one** order item (participation constraint: total).
  - A menu item uses **zero or more** ingredients (optional participation).

### Step 2 — Identify Entities & Attributes
Distinguish **strong entities** (exist independently: `CUSTOMER`, `MENU_ITEM`) from **weak entities** (depend on a parent: `ORDER_ITEM` depends on `ORDER`).

### Step 3 — Identify Relationships & Cardinalities
- `CUSTOMER` **places** `ORDER` — 1 : N
- `ORDER` **contains** `ORDER_ITEM` — 1 : N (weak)
- `ORDER` **served_by** `STAFF` — N : 1
- `MENU_ITEM` **uses** `INGREDIENT` — M : N (resolved via `RECIPE` associative entity)
- `RESERVATION` **assigned_to** `TABLE_INFO` — N : 1

### Step 4 — Map ER → Relational Schema
**Why:** RDBMS engines cannot store ER diagrams directly; they require tables.

| ER Construct | Relational Mapping |
|---|---|
| Strong entity | New relation with PK |
| Weak entity | New relation with PK = parent PK + partial key |
| 1:1 relationship | Foreign key in either table (often mandatory side) |
| 1:N relationship | Foreign key on the "many" side |
| M:N relationship | New relation with composite PK of both parents |
| Multivalued attribute | New relation (violates 1NF otherwise) |

### Step 5 — Apply Functional Dependencies (FDs)
An FD $X \rightarrow Y$ means: for any two tuples with the same $X$ value, they must have the same $Y$ value.

**Example FDs in Restaurant DB:**
- $\text{order\_id} \rightarrow \text{customer\_id, order\_date, total\_amount}$
- $\text{menu\_item\_id} \rightarrow \text{name, price, category\_id}$
- $\text{table\_id, reservation\_date, time\_slot} \rightarrow \text{customer\_id, party\_size}$

### Step 6 — Normalise to Remove Anomalies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Test:** If you skip from ER directly to CREATE TABLE without listing the FDs and the normal form achieved, you will lose 3–4 marks.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Attribute Closure | $X^+ = X \cup \{A \mid \exists Y \rightarrow A, Y \subseteq X^+\}$ | Find all attributes functionally determined by $X$ |
| 2 | Candidate Key Test | $X$ is a CK iff $X^+ = R$ (all attributes) | Identify minimal superkeys |
| 3 | 1NF Rule | Every attribute must be **atomic** (no multi-valued, no composite) | Eliminate repeating groups |
| 4 | 2NF Rule | 1NF + No **partial dependency** (no non-prime attr depends on part of a CK) | Eliminate redundancy from composite keys |
| 5 | 3NF Rule | 2NF + No **transitive dependency** ($X \rightarrow Y \rightarrow Z$ where $Z$ is non-prime) | Eliminate transitive anomalies |
| 6 | BCNF Rule | For every FD $X \rightarrow Y$, $X$ must be a **superkey** | Stricter 3NF |
| 7 | ACID — Atomicity | "All or nothing" — partial transactions must not persist | Implemented via *undo logs* |
| 8 | ACID — Consistency | Sum of debits = Sum of credits (after commit) | Enforced by integrity constraints |
| 9 | ACID — Isolation | Concurrent transactions must appear serial | Achieved via locking / MVCC |
| 10 | ACID — Durability | Committed data survives crashes | Implemented via *redo logs* |
| 11 | Locking Compatibility | Shared (S) locks allow other S; Exclusive (X) locks allow none | Prevents lost updates |
| 12 | Wait-Die / Wound-Wait | Timestamp-based deadlock prevention | Older transaction waits; younger aborts |
| 13 | Two-Phase Locking (2PL) | Growing phase → Shrinking phase | Guarantees conflict-serialisability |
| 14 | Conflict Serialisability Test | Build *precedence graph*; if **acyclic** → serialisable | Detect schedule correctness |
| 15 | Recovery — REDO | Apply log entries for committed transactions post-crash | Durability |
| 16 | Recovery — UNDO | Rollback uncommitted transactions using *before-images* | Atomicity |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In the above table, the pipe symbol in `|x|` (set-difference or cardinality) is written as `\vert`. Never type `|` inside a markdown table cell — it breaks the table renderer.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Restaurant database systems are **microcosms of enterprise resource planning (ERP)**. The same design patterns appear in:
- **Hospital management** (patient ↔ order, doctor ↔ staff, ward ↔ table)
- **E-commerce platforms** (cart ↔ order_item, SKU ↔ menu_item)
- **Airline reservation** (PNR ↔ order, seat ↔ table, flight ↔ branch)

Thus, mastering this case study directly prepares KTU students for **SDE interviews** at firms like Zomato, Swiggy, and OYO.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Normalisation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: ER → Relational Mapping (with Justification)

We will derive the relational schema step by step for the Restaurant DB.

### Step 1: Map Strong Entities

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CUSTOMER}(\underline{\text{cust\_id}}, \text{name, phone, email, dob}) \\
\text{RESTAURANT\_BRANCH}(\underline{\text{branch\_id}}, \text{location, phone, manager\_id}) \\
\text{TABLE\_INFO}(\underline{\text{table\_id}}, \text{branch\_id}, \text{capacity}, \text{status}) \\
\text{MENU\_CATEGORY}(\underline{\text{cat\_id}}, \text{cat\_name}) \\
\text{MENU\_ITEM}(\underline{\text{item\_id}}, \text{name, price, cat\_id, is\_veg}) \\
\text{STAFF}(\underline{\text{staff\_id}}, \text{name, role, salary, branch\_id}) \\
\text{SUPPLIER}(\underline{\text{sup\_id}}, \text{sup\_name, contact})
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Each strong entity becomes a relation. The primary key (underlined) is the natural or surrogate identifier. Foreign keys (`branch_id` in `TABLE_INFO` and `STAFF`) enforce 1:N relationships from `RESTAURANT_BRANCH`.

### Step 2: Map the Weak Entity ORDER_ITEM

A weak entity has no complete key of its own. Its primary key is the **parent's PK + a partial key (discriminator)**.

$$\text{ORDER\_ITEM}(\underline{\text{order\_id}, \text{item\_id}}, \text{quantity, special\_instructions, line\_total})$$

Here `order_id` is FK to `ORDER` and `item_id` is FK to `MENU_ITEM`. The pair (order_id, item_id) forms the composite primary key. Partial key alone (`item_id`) is insufficient — many orders can contain the same menu item.

### Step 3: Map M:N Relationship (MENU_ITEM ↔ INGREDIENT via RECIPE)

A M:N relationship is converted to a new relation whose PK is the combination of both parents' PKs.

$$\text{RECIPE}(\underline{\text{item\_id}, \text{ing\_id}}, \text{qty\_required, unit})$$

**Logic:** If a dish (e.g., "Paneer Butter Masala") uses 200g of paneer and 50g of butter, that row in `RECIPE` records the recipe composition. Resolving M:N this way prevents the multivalued-attribute violation of 1NF.

### Step 4: Map the Multivalued Attribute (Customer Phone Numbers)

If a customer can have multiple phone numbers, we **must not** store them comma-separated (violates 1NF). We extract a new relation:

$$\text{CUSTOMER\_PHONE}(\underline{\text{cust\_id}, \text{phone\_no}})$$

**Logic:** "Atomic" does not mean "small" — it means "indivisible in the context of the schema." A phone number, although string-typed, is a single value per row.

### Step 5: Include ORDER and PAYMENT

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ORDER}(\underline{\text{order\_id}}, \text{cust\_id}, \text{table\_id}, \text{staff\_id}, \text{order\_date}, \text{status}, \text{total\_amount}) \\
\text{PAYMENT}(\underline{\text{payment\_id}}, \text{order\_id}, \text{amount, mode, payment\_time})
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** `ORDER` is the central transactional entity. `total_amount` is a **derived attribute** (can be computed, but stored for performance and historical snapshotting).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Normalisation Derivation

### Initial Unnormalised Form (UNF) — A Sample Order Record

| order_id | cust_name | items_ordered | item_prices | qty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | Rahul | Paneer Tikka, Naan | 250, 40 | 1, 2 |
| 102 | Sneha | Veg Biryani, Coke | 220, 50 | 1, 1 |

**Violation:** `items_ordered` and `item_prices` are **multivalued** (repeating groups).

### Apply 1NF: Atomicity

Decompose into atomic rows (one row per item).

| order_id | cust_name | item_name | item_price | qty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | Rahul | Paneer Tikka | 250 | 1 |
| 101 | Rahul | Naan | 40 | 2 |
| 102 | Sneha | Veg Biryani | 220 | 1 |
| 102 | Sneha | Coke | 50 | 1 |

✅ 1NF satisfied. But we still have **partial dependency** (cust_name depends only on order_id) and **transitive dependency** (item_name → item_price).

### Apply 2NF: Eliminate Partial Dependencies

Split into:

**R1: ORDER(101, Rahul), (102, Sneha)** — Primary key: `order_id`

**R2: ORDER_ITEM(order_id, item_name, item_price, qty)** — Primary key: `(order_id, item_name)`

✅ 2NF satisfied (no non-prime attribute depends on a *part* of a composite key).

### Apply 3NF: Eliminate Transitive Dependencies

`item_name → item_price` is transitive (non-prime `item_price` depends on non-prime `item_name`).

Split R2 into:

**R3: ORDER_ITEM(order_id, item_id, qty)**

**R4: MENU_ITEM(item_id, item_name, item_price)**

✅ 3NF satisfied.

### Check BCNF

For R4: $FD1: \text{item\_id} \rightarrow \text{item\_name, item\_price}$ — LHS `item_id` is a superkey. ✅ BCNF satisfied.

## 3.3 Transaction Handling: A Worked Example

**Scenario:** Two waiters simultaneously try to update the same `ORDER_ITEM` to "Served" status.

| T1 (Waiter A) | T2 (Waiter B) | DB State |
|---|---|---|
| `SELECT status FROM ORDER_ITEM WHERE id=501` → "Preparing" | — | "Preparing" |
| — | `SELECT status FROM ORDER_ITEM WHERE id=501` → "Preparing" | "Preparing" |
| `UPDATE ORDER_ITEM SET status='Served' WHERE id=501` | — | "Served" |
| — | `UPDATE ORDER_ITEM SET status='Served' WHERE id=501` | "Served" |
| `COMMIT` | — | committed |
| — | `COMMIT` | committed |

**Issue:** Without isolation, this is fine (idempotent update). But consider **Lost Update**:

| T1 | T2 | DB |
|---|---|---|
| Reads quantity = 1 | — | 1 |
| — | Reads quantity = 1 | 1 |
| Sets quantity = 1 + 1 = 2 | — | 2 |
| — | Sets quantity = 1 + 1 = 2 (overwrites) | 2 ❌ |

**Solution via 2PL (Two-Phase Locking):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T1: \text{LOCK-X}(q) \rightarrow \text{READ}(q) \rightarrow \text{WRITE}(q=2) \rightarrow \text{UNLOCK} \\
T2: \text{WAIT} \rightarrow \text{LOCK-X}(q) \rightarrow \text{READ}(q=2) \rightarrow \text{WRITE}(q=3) \rightarrow \text{UNLOCK}
\end{aligned}$$

Final value = 3 ✅. This is the **Growing Phase (locks acquired) → Shrinking Phase (locks released)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Design Choice | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Surrogate Keys** (`SERIAL`/`AUTO_INCREMENT`) | Compact, stable, performance | No business meaning | Always for PKs in OLTP |
| **Natural Keys** (e.g., Aadhaar, ISBN) | Self-documenting | Can change, bulky | When truly immutable |
| **Denormalisation** (storing `total_amount`) | Fast reads, snapshotting | Update anomalies | Reporting/OLAP layers |
| **Stored Triggers for Billing** | Automation, integrity | Hard to debug | Real-time business rules |
| **MVCC** (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) | Readers don't block writers | Storage overhead | High-read systems (Postgres default) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagram — Restaurant Database

```mermaid
erDiagram
    CUSTOMER ||--o{ ORDER : "places"
    ORDER ||--|{ ORDER_ITEM : "contains"
    MENU_ITEM ||--o{ ORDER_ITEM : "included_in"
    STAFF ||--o{ ORDER : "serves"
    TABLE_INFO ||--o{ RESERVATION : "assigned_to"
    CUSTOMER ||--o{ RESERVATION : "books"
    RESTAURANT_BRANCH ||--o{ TABLE_INFO : "has"
    RESTAURANT_BRANCH ||--o{ STAFF : "employs"
    MENU_CATEGORY ||--o{ MENU_ITEM : "classifies"
    MENU_ITEM ||--o{ RECIPE : "uses"
    INGREDIENT ||--o{ RECIPE : "supplies"
    SUPPLIER ||--o{ INGREDIENT : "provides"
    ORDER ||--|| PAYMENT : "settled_by"

    CUSTOMER {
        int cust_id PK
        string name
        string phone
        string email
        date dob
    }
    ORDER {
        int order_id PK
        int cust_id FK
        int table_id FK
        int staff_id FK
        datetime order_time
        string status
        decimal total_amount
    }
    ORDER_ITEM {
        int order_id PK,FK
        int item_id PK,FK
        int quantity
        string special_instructions
        decimal line_total
    }
    MENU_ITEM {
        int item_id PK
        int cat_id FK
        string name
        decimal price
        boolean is_veg
    }
    MENU_CATEGORY {
        int cat_id PK
        string cat_name
    }
    RESERVATION {
        int reservation_id PK
        int cust_id FK
        int table_id FK
        date reserve_date
        time time_slot
        int party_size
    }
    TABLE_INFO {
        int table_id PK
        int branch_id FK
        int capacity
        string status
    }
    RESTAURANT_BRANCH {
        int branch_id PK
        string location
        string phone
    }
    STAFF {
        int staff_id PK
        int branch_id FK
        string name
        string role
        decimal salary
    }
    INGREDIENT {
        int ing_id PK
        int sup_id FK
        string name
        string unit
    }
    SUPPLIER {
        int sup_id PK
        string sup_name
        string contact
    }
    RECIPE {
        int item_id PK,FK
        int ing_id PK,FK
        decimal qty_required
    }
    PAYMENT {
        int payment_id PK
        int order_id FK
        decimal amount
        string mode
        datetime payment_time
    }
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Order Processing Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer places order via POS] --> B[Begin Transaction T1]
    B --> C[Insert into ORDER table]
    C --> D[Insert line items into ORDER_ITEM]
    D --> E{Stock check via RECIPE join}
    E -- Sufficient --> F[Compute line_total price]
    E -- Insufficient --> G[Trigger INVENTORY_ALERT]
    F --> H[Lock TABLE_INFO for assignment]
    H --> I[Assign Waiter STAFF]
    I --> J[COMMIT T1 - Order confirmed]
    J --> K[Kitchen Display System reads ORDER]
    K --> L[Chef marks ORDER_ITEM as PREPARING]
    L --> M[Status update READ COMMITTED]
    M --> N[Ready - update to SERVED]
    N --> O[Customer requests bill]
    O --> P[Trigger computes total from ORDER_ITEM]
    P --> Q[Generate PAYMENT record]
    Q --> R[COMMIT T2 - Payment recorded]
    R --> S[REDO log ensures durability]
```

## 4.3 Schema Indexing Strategy (B-Tree Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[CUSTOMER cust_id] --> I1[B-Tree Index]
    R2[ORDER order_date] --> I2[Composite Index]
    R3[ORDER_ITEM order_id, item_id] --> I3[Composite PK Index]
    R4[RESERVATION table_id reserve_date time_slot] --> I4[Unique Index]
    R5[MENU_ITEM name] --> I5[Hash Index for search]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **composite unique index** on `RESERVATION(table_id, reserve_date, time_slot)` is the cornerstone of preventing double-booking — a classic KTU short-answer question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q]
**Define ACID properties. How do they apply to a restaurant order-placement scenario?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ACID stands for:
1. **Atomicity** — A bill payment transaction is "all or nothing." If the card is declined midway, the `ORDER` status reverts from "Paid" back to "Pending." *(1 mark)*
2. **Consistency** — The total of `ORDER_ITEM.line_total` must always equal `ORDER.total_amount` after commit. Enforced via CHECK constraints. *(1 mark)*
3. **Isolation** — Two cashiers cannot simultaneously issue two different bills for the same `order_id`; locking prevents this. *(0.5 marks)*
4. **Durability** — Once payment is committed, the record survives power failure due to write-ahead logging. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q]
**What is a weak entity? Identify the weak entity in a restaurant database with justification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **weak entity** is one that cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone and depends on a strong (owner) entity for its existence. *(1 mark)*

In the restaurant database, **`ORDER_ITEM`** is a weak entity because:
- It has no independent meaning without its parent `ORDER`. *(1 mark)*
- Its primary key `(order_id, item_id)` is a composite of the parent PK and a partial key `item_id`. *(1 mark)*

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Design an ER diagram for a restaurant database system with the following requirements: (7 marks)
- A restaurant has multiple branches; each branch has tables and staff.
- Customers place orders; each order has multiple order items.
- Menu items belong to categories and use ingredients supplied by suppliers.
- Customers can make reservations for specific tables and time slots.

**(b)** Map the ER diagram to a relational schema and normalise it up to 3NF. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a): ER Diagram

Entities identified:
- `CUSTOMER(cust_id, name, phone, email)`
- `RESTAURANT_BRANCH(branch_id, location, phone)`
- `TABLE_INFO(table_id, capacity, status)`
- `STAFF(staff_id, name, role, salary)`
- `MENU_CATEGORY(cat_id, cat_name)`
- `MENU_ITEM(item_id, name, price, is_veg)`
- `INGREDIENT(ing_id, name, unit)`
- `SUPPLIER(sup_id, sup_name, contact)`
- `ORDER(order_id, order_date, status, total_amount)`
- `ORDER_ITEM(order_id, item_id, qty, line_total)` — *Weak entity*
- `RESERVATION(res_id, reserve_date, time_slot, party_size)`
- `PAYMENT(payment_id, amount, mode, time)`

Relationships with cardinalities:
1. `CUSTOMER` 1:N `ORDER` (places)
2. `ORDER` 1:N `ORDER_ITEM` (contains — identifying)
3. `MENU_ITEM` 1:N `ORDER_ITEM` (included_in)
4. `STAFF` 1:N `ORDER` (serves)
5. `TABLE_INFO` 1:N `RESERVATION` (assigned_to)
6. `CUSTOMER` 1:N `RESERVATION` (books)
7. `MENU_CATEGORY` 1:N `MENU_ITEM` (classifies)
8. `MENU_ITEM` M:N `INGREDIENT` via `RECIPE(item_id, ing_id, qty_required)`
9. `SUPPLIER` 1:N `INGREDIENT` (provides)
10. `ORDER` 1:1 `PAYMENT` (settled_by)
11. `RESTAURANT_BRANCH` 1:N `TABLE_INFO`, 1:N `STAFF` (owns/employs)

*(Refer to Mermaid ER diagram in Section 4.1 for visual.)* — **Full 7 marks**

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Mapping + Normalisation

**Step 1: Mapping to Relations**

```sql
CREATE TABLE RestaurantBranch (
    branch_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    location    VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    phone       VARCHAR(15)
);

CREATE TABLE TableInfo (
    table_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    branch_id   INT REFERENCES RestaurantBranch(branch_id),
    capacity    INT CHECK (capacity > 0),
    status      VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'Available'
);

CREATE TABLE Staff (
    staff_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    branch_id   INT REFERENCES RestaurantBranch(branch_id),
    name        VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    role        VARCHAR(20) CHECK (role IN ('Chef','Waiter','Manager','Cashier')),
    salary      DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (salary >= 0)
);

CREATE TABLE Customer (
    cust_id     INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name        VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    phone       VARCHAR(15) UNIQUE,
    email       VARCHAR(100)
);

CREATE TABLE MenuCategory (
    cat_id      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    cat_name    VARCHAR(30) UNIQUE NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE MenuItem (
    item_id     INT PRIMARY KEY,
    cat_id      INT REFERENCES MenuCategory(cat_id),
    name        VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    price       DECIMAL(7,2) CHECK (price > 0),
    is_veg      BOOLEAN
);

CREATE TABLE Supplier (
    sup_id      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    sup_name    VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    contact     VARCHAR(15)
);

CREATE TABLE Ingredient (
    ing_id      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    sup_id      INT REFERENCES Supplier(sup_id),
    name        VARCHAR(50),
    unit        VARCHAR(10)
);

CREATE TABLE Recipe (
    item_id     INT REFERENCES MenuItem(item_id),
    ing_id      INT REFERENCES Ingredient(ing_id),
    qty_required DECIMAL(8,2),
    PRIMARY KEY (item_id, ing_id)
);

CREATE TABLE Reservation (
    reservation_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    cust_id     INT REFERENCES Customer(cust_id),
    table_id    INT REFERENCES TableInfo(table_id),
    reserve_date DATE NOT NULL,
    time_slot   TIME NOT NULL,
    party_size  INT CHECK (party_size > 0),
    UNIQUE (table_id, reserve_date, time_slot)
);

CREATE TABLE FoodOrder (
    order_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    cust_id     INT REFERENCES Customer(cust_id),
    table_id    INT REFERENCES TableInfo(table_id),
    staff_id    INT REFERENCES Staff(staff_id),
    order_date  TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    status      VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'Pending',
    total_amount DECIMAL(10,2)
);

CREATE TABLE OrderItem (
    order_id    INT REFERENCES FoodOrder(order_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    item_id     INT REFERENCES MenuItem(item_id),
    quantity    INT CHECK (quantity > 0),
    special_instructions VARCHAR(200),
    line_total  DECIMAL(10,2),
    PRIMARY KEY (order_id, item_id)
);

CREATE TABLE Payment (
    payment_id  INT PRIMARY KEY,
    order_id    INT UNIQUE REFERENCES FoodOrder(order_id),
    amount      DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (amount >= 0),
    mode        VARCHAR(20) CHECK (mode IN ('Cash','Card','UPI','Wallet')),
    payment_time TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
```

**Step 2: Functional Dependencies and Normalisation**

FDs for the Order schema (initial):
- $F1: \text{order\_id} \rightarrow \text{cust\_id, table\_id, staff\_id, order\_date, status, total\_amount}$
- $F2: \text{order\_id, item\_id} \rightarrow \text{quantity, line\_total}$
- $F3: \text{item\_id} \rightarrow \text{name, price, cat\_id, is\_veg}$
- $F4: \text{cust\_id} \rightarrow \text{name, phone, email}$

**Check 1NF:** All attributes are atomic. ✅

**Check 2NF:** In `OrderItem`, `line_total` depends on full PK `(order_id, item_id)`, not on a part. ✅ No partial dependencies.

**Check 3NF:** In `MenuItem`, `item_id → price` is direct, not transitive (no intermediate non-prime). ✅ In `FoodOrder`, `total_amount` is derived but acceptable if we treat it as stored.

**Decomposition to 3NF (if needed):** Split any relation where a non-prime attribute transitively determines another. Our final schema is in 3NF. ✅

**Full 7 marks** distributed as: mapping (3 marks), FDs identification (2 marks), normalisation proof (2 marks).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]

**(a)** Write SQL DDL for the restaurant database. Demonstrate **3 complex queries** involving JOINs, subqueries, and aggregation relevant to restaurant analytics. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)** protocol. Show how it prevents the **lost update** problem in a concurrent order-quantity-update scenario. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Complex SQL Queries

**Query 1: Top 5 most-ordered menu items in the last 30 days**

```sql
SELECT  mi.item_id,
        mi.name,
        SUM(oi.quantity) AS total_qty,
        RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(oi.quantity) DESC) AS popularity_rank
FROM    OrderItem oi
JOIN    MenuItem mi  ON oi.item_id = mi.item_id
JOIN    FoodOrder fo ON oi.order_id = fo.order_id
WHERE   fo.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
GROUP BY mi.item_id, mi.name
ORDER BY total_qty DESC
LIMIT 5;
```
*Tests: 3-table JOIN, GROUP BY, window function, date arithmetic.* ✅

**Query 2: Branches whose revenue exceeds the chain-wide average**

```sql
SELECT  rb.branch_id,
        rb.location,
        SUM(p.amount) AS branch_revenue
FROM    RestaurantBranch rb
JOIN    TableInfo    ti ON rb.branch_id = ti.branch_id
JOIN    FoodOrder    fo ON ti.table_id  = fo.table_id
JOIN    Payment      p  ON fo.order_id  = p.order_id
GROUP BY rb.branch_id, rb.location
HAVING  SUM(p.amount) >
        (SELECT AVG(branch_total)
         FROM (  SELECT  SUM(p2.amount) AS branch_total
                 FROM    RestaurantBranch rb2
                 JOIN    TableInfo    ti2 ON rb2.branch_id = ti2.branch_id
                 JOIN    FoodOrder    fo2 ON ti2.table_id  = fo2.table_id
                 JOIN    Payment      p2  ON fo2.order_id  = p2.order_id
                 GROUP BY rb2.branch_id
              ) AS branch_revenues
        );
```
*Tests: Subquery in HAVING, derived table, multi-level aggregation.* ✅

**Query 3: Customers who have not placed any order in the last 60 days (for re-engagement marketing)**

```sql
SELECT  c.cust_id, c.name, c.email,
        MAX(fo.order_date) AS last_order_date
FROM    Customer c
LEFT JOIN FoodOrder fo ON c.cust_id = fo.cust_id
GROUP BY c.cust_id, c.name, c.email
HAVING  MAX(fo.order_date) IS NULL
   OR   MAX(fo.order_date) < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '60 days';
```
*Tests: LEFT JOIN (outer join), NULL handling in HAVING.* ✅

**Marks distribution:** DDL (1 mark) + 3 queries (2 marks each) = 7 marks.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Two-Phase Locking

**Definition:** The 2PL protocol ensures conflict-serialisability by dividing a transaction's execution into two distinct phases:
- **Growing Phase:** Locks may be acquired, but none may be released.
- **Shrinking Phase:** Locks may be released, but none may be acquired.

Once a lock is released, no new lock on any data item can be obtained by that transaction.

**Lost Update Problem (without 2PL):**

| Step | T1 (Waiter A) | T2 (Waiter B) | DB value of `qty` |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `READ qty` → 1 | — | 1 |
| 2 | — | `READ qty` → 1 | 1 |
| 3 | `qty = 1 + 1 = 2`, `WRITE` | — | 2 |
| 4 | — | `qty = 1 + 1 = 2`, `WRITE` (overwrites) | 2 ❌ |
| 5 | `COMMIT` | — | 2 |
| 6 | — | `COMMIT` | 2 |

**Correct result should be 3** (1 + 1 + 1 = three servings).

**With 2PL:**

| Step | T1 | T2 | DB | Lock held |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `LOCK-X(qty)` | — | 1 | T1 |
| 2 | `READ qty` → 1 | — | 1 | T1 |
| 3 | `WRITE qty=2` | `LOCK-X(qty)` → **BLOCKED** | 2 | T1 |
| 4 | `COMMIT` → `UNLOCK` | (woken up) | 2 | none |
| 5 | (Growing ends) | `LOCK-X(qty)` acquired | 2 | T2 |
| 6 | (Shrinking) | `READ qty` → 2 | 2 | T2 |
| 7 | — | `WRITE qty=3` | 3 | T2 |
| 8 | — | `COMMIT` → `UNLOCK` | 3 | none |

Final value = 3 ✅. T2's update was **not lost**.

**Proof of Serialisability (Precedence Graph):** The graph has edges $T1 \rightarrow T2$ (T1 locked `qty` before T2). The graph is acyclic → schedule is conflict-serialisable.

**Marks distribution:** 2PL definition (2 marks), lost update example (2 marks), 2PL solution with table (2 marks), serialisability proof (1 mark) = 7 marks.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss areas in Restaurant DB questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting weak entity notation** — `ORDER_ITEM` must be drawn with a double rectangle; failure costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Omitting cardinality ratios** — Every relationship MUST have a label (1:1, 1:N, M:N) and a name (verb).
> 3. **No participation constraints** — Total participation (e.g., every ORDER must have at least one ORDER_ITEM) must be shown by a double line.
> 4. **Skipping 2NF/3NF verification** — Merely listing tables without showing FDs and checking normal form = 2–3 marks lost.
> 5. **SQL queries without JOIN syntax** — Writing correlated subqueries where a JOIN suffices is acceptable but inefficient; examiners prefer clean JOINs.
> 6. **Ignoring ACID in transaction questions** — Just drawing a schedule table without naming the property violated (lost update, dirty read, non-repeatable read) = 2 marks lost.
> 7. **Not drawing the precedence graph** for serialisability — this is the single most-skipped step.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Restaurant DB** is a classic KTU capstone integrating ER modelling, normalisation, SQL, and transactions.
- ✅ **Strong entities** have independent PKs; **weak entities** (e.g., `ORDER_ITEM`) require composite PK with parent's PK.
- ✅ **1NF = atomicity**, **2NF = no partial dependency**, **3NF = no transitive dependency**, **BCNF = every determinant is a superkey**.
- ✅ **M:N relationships** are always converted to a new associative relation (e.g., `RECIPE`).
- ✅ **ACID** = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — apply to every order/payment transaction.
- ✅ **2PL** has two phases: Growing (acquire locks only) and Shrinking (release locks only) — guarantees conflict-serialisability.
- ✅ **Unique index** on `(table_id, reserve_date, time_slot)` prevents double-booking — favourite 3-mark KTU question.
- ✅ **Derived attributes** (e.g., `total_amount`) can be stored for performance but must be updated by triggers.
- ✅ Always use **surrogate keys** (`SERIAL`/`AUTO_INCREMENT`) for OLTP primary keys.
- ✅ **Window functions** (`RANK()`, `DENSE_RANK()`, `ROW_NUMBER()`) are examinable for analytics queries.
- ✅ **Locking compatibility matrix:** S–S ✅, S–X ❌, X–X ❌.
- ✅ **Recovery uses REDO + UNDO logs** to ensure atomicity and durability after a crash.
- ✅ Common concurrency problems to memorise: **Lost Update, Dirty Read, Non-Repeatable Read, Phantom Read** — each has a specific isolation level fix.
- ✅ For KTU: Always end with a **precedence graph** to justify serialisability claims.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Payroll Database Management System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT304 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 7 > - **Topic:** Payroll Database Management System # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Payroll Database Management System (Payroll DBMS)** is a specialized, integrated relational database system designed to systematically store, retrieve, manipulate, and manage all financial compensation data of an organization's employees. It encapsulates employee master records, salary structures, allowances, deductions, tax computations, attendance data, and statutory compliance information (PF, ESI, TDS) into a normalized relational schema, governed by ACID properties and SQL-based query processing. In **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, the Payroll DBMS is treated as a *Case Study Application* of DBMS principles, where students must demonstrate competency in: - **Conceptual Design** (ER Modeling) - **Logical Design** (Relational Schema Mapping) - **Normalization** (up to BCNF/4NF) - **Physical Implementation** (DDL + DML + DCL) - **Advanced Features** (Views, Triggers, Stored Procedures, Cursors, Exception Handling) ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of Payroll DBMS as a "Digital Salary Vault with Automatic Calculators"**. Imagine a large factory with **5,000 workers**. Before computers, a single payroll clerk would manually: 1. Check attendance registers (paper-based) 2. Calculate overtime on a calculator 3. Subtract income tax (looking up tax tables) 4. Write cheques for each employee 5. File stack of registers in a room This process was **slow**, **error-prone**, **non-scalable**, and **insecure**. Now imagine converting this entire physical paperwork system into a **structured, queryable, automated digital repository** where: - Each employee is a *row* in a table - Salary rules are *constraints/triggers* that fire automatically - The system generates payslips, bank transfer files, and tax reports with a single SQL command That digital repository **is the Payroll Database Management System**. ## 1.3 Core Entities of a Payroll System | # | Entity | Purpose | |---|--------|---------| | 1 | **Employee** | Master record of personal & job details | | 2 | **Department** | Organizational structure | | 3 | **Designation** | Job role / position | | 4 | **Salary_Structure** | Base pay, HRA, DA, allowances | | 5 | **Attendance** | Daily/monthly presence & overtime | | 6 | **Deductions** | PF, ESI, Professional Tax, TDS | | 7 | **Payroll_Transaction** | Monthly processed salary record | | 8 | **Bank_Account** | Disbursement details | | 9 | **Tax_Slab** | Income tax computation rules | ## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Constants - **PF (Provident Fund) Contribution Rate**: **12% of Basic Pay** (employee + employer) - **ESI (Employee State Insurance)**: **0.75% (employee) + 3.25% (employer)** of gross salary (if applicable) - **Professional Tax (Kerala)**: ₹**200/month** (for gross > ₹11,250) - **TDS**: As per **New/Old Regime** income tax slabs - **Financial Year**: April 1 to March 31 (Indian Standard) - **HRA Exemption Limit**: **50% of Basic** (metro) / **40% of Basic** (non-metro) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of Payroll DBMS (Three-Schema Architecture) The Payroll DBMS follows the classic **ANSI/SPARC Three-Schema Architecture**: 1. **Internal Level (Physical)** → How data is stored on disk (B+ trees, indexes on `emp_id`, `dept_id`) 2. **Conceptual Level (Logical)** → Entire payroll database schema (tables, relationships, constraints) 3. **External Level (View)** → User-specific views (e.g., `Employee_Payslip_View`, `Manager_Dashboard_View`) ## 2.2 Theoretical Phases of Payroll DBMS Design ### Phase 1: Requirements Analysis - Identify stakeholders: HR, Finance, Auditors, Employees, Banks - Functional requirements: salary calculation, tax deduction, payslip generation - Non-functional: security, audit trail, concurrency ### Phase 2: Conceptual Design (ER Model) - Identify **Entities** (Employee, Department, Salary, etc.) - Identify **Attributes** (simple, composite, multi-valued, derived) - Identify **Relationships** (Works_In, Belongs_To, Has_Account) - Draw **ER Diagram** with cardinality ratios ### Phase 3: Logical Design (Relational Mapping) - Convert ER to Relations using **7-step algorithm**: 1. Strong entity → Table 2. Weak entity → Table with FK to owner 3. 1:1 relationship → FK in either table 4. 1:N relationship → FK on the "many" side 5. M:N relationship → Separate junction table 6. Multi-valued attribute → Separate table 7. Derived attribute → Not stored (computed via query) ### Phase 4: Normalization - Apply **1NF → 2NF → 3NF → BCNF** to eliminate anomalies ### Phase 5: Physical Design & Implementation - DDL (CREATE TABLE), DML (INSERT, UPDATE), DQL (SELECT), TCL (COMMIT) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **KTU high-yield** and appear frequently in board examinations. | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Purpose | |---|---------|---------------|---------| | 1 | **Gross Salary** | $\text{GS} = \text{Basic} + \text{DA} + \text{HRA} + \text{TA} + \text{MA} + \text{SA}$ | Total earnings before deduction | | 2 | **Net Salary (Take-Home)** | $\text{NS} = \text{GS} - \text{Total Deductions}$ | Final disbursed amount | | 3 | **Total Deductions** | $\text{TD} = \text{PF} + \text{ESI} + \text{PT} + \text{TDS} + \text{Loan EMI}$ | Statutory + voluntary cuts | | 4 | **PF Deduction** | $\text{PF} = 0.12 \times \text{Basic Pay}$ | Provident Fund (employee share) | | 5 | **ESI Deduction** | $\text{ESI} = 0.0075 \times \text{GS}$ (if GS ≤ ₹21,000) | Employee State Insurance | | 6 | **Overtime Pay** | $\text{OT} = \frac{\text{Basic}}{26 \text{ days} \times 8 \text{ hrs}} \times 2 \times \text{OT hours}$ | Double rate for extra hours | | 7 | **1NF Condition** | All attributes must be **atomic** (no multi-valued fields) | Eliminate repeating groups | | 8 | **2NF Condition** | 1NF + **no partial dependency** (all non-key attrs depend on full PK) | Eliminate partial dependencies | | 9 | **3NF Condition** | 2NF + **no transitive dependency** (non-key $\rightarrow$ non-key prohibited) | Eliminate transitive dependencies | | 10 | **BCNF Condition** | 3NF + **for every FD $X \rightarrow Y$, $X$ must be a super key** | Stricter version of 3NF | | 11 | **Cardinality (1:N)** | One Department has many Employees | FK on the "many" side | | 12 | **M:N Mapping** | Create junction table with composite PK | Resolves many-to-many | | 13 | **ACID Properties** | **A**tomicity, **C**onsistency, **I**solation, **D**urability | Transaction reliability | | 14 | **Lossless Join** | $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow R_1 \text{ or } R_2$ | Decomposition preserves data | | 15 | **Dependency Preservation** | $(F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \ldots \cup F_n)^+ = F^+$ | All FDs retained post-decomposition | > **NOTE on Payroll Constants (India)**: > - PF Wage Ceiling: **₹15,000/month** > - ESI Wage Ceiling: **₹21,000/month** > - Standard Working Days: **26 days/month** > - Standard Working Hours: **8 hours/day** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Payroll DBMS principles extend to: - **Banking Systems** (transaction processing) - **ERP Systems** (SAP, Oracle Financials) - **Hospital Management** (billing, insurance) - **E-commerce** (order processing, invoicing) - **Government Tax Portals** (TDS, GST filing) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive ER-to-Relational Mapping Derivation Consider a simplified Payroll scenario. We derive the relational schema step-by-step. ### Step 1: Identify Entities and Attributes **Employee** (`EmpID`, `EmpName`, `DOB`, `DOJ`, `Gender`, `BasicPay`, `Email`, `Phone`) - `EmpID` → Primary Key - `BasicPay` → Stored attribute - `Age` → Derived (not stored) **Department** (`DeptID`, `DeptName`, `Location`) - `DeptID` → Primary Key **Salary_Structure** (`StructID`, `Basic`, `DA`, `HRA`, `TA`, `MA`, `SpecialAllowance`, `EmpID`) - `StructID` → Primary Key - `EmpID` → Foreign Key **Deductions** (`DedID`, `PF`, `ESI`, `PT`, `TDS`, `EmpID`, `Month`, `Year`) - `DedID` → Primary Key -
