Composite key candidate: (`EmpID`, `Month`, `Year`) **Payroll_Master** (`PayrollID`, `EmpID`, `Month`, `Year`, `GrossSal`, `TotalDed`, `NetSal`, `PayDate`) - `PayrollID` → Primary Key ### Step 2: Identify Relationships | Relationship | Between | Cardinality | Action | |--------------|---------|-------------|--------| | **Works_In** | Employee – Department | N:1 | Add `DeptID` (FK) to Employee | | **Has_Structure** | Employee – Salary_Structure | 1:1 | Share PK or add FK | | **Subjected_To** | Employee – Deductions | 1:N | FK `EmpID` in Deductions | | **Generates** | Employee – Payroll_Master | 1:N | FK `EmpID` in Payroll_Master | ### Step 3: Derive the Relational Schema (with Constraints) | Table Name | Attributes | Primary Key | Foreign Keys | Constraints | |------------|-----------|-------------|--------------|-------------| | **Department** | `DeptID`, `DeptName`, `Location` | `DeptID` | None | `DeptID` INT NOT NULL UNIQUE | | **Employee** | `EmpID`, `EmpName`, `DOB`, `DOJ`, `Gender`, `BasicPay`, `Email`, `Phone`, `DeptID` | `EmpID` | `DeptID` → Department | `BasicPay` ≥ 0; `Email` UNIQUE | | **Salary_Structure** | `StructID`, `Basic`, `DA`, `HRA`, `TA`, `MA`, `SpecialAllowance`, `EmpID` | `StructID` | `EmpID` → Employee | All numeric > 0 | | **Deductions** | `DedID`, `PF`, `ESI`, `PT`, `TDS`, `LoanEMI`, `EmpID`, `Month`, `Year` | `DedID` | `EmpID` → Employee | UNIQUE(`EmpID`, `Month`, `Year`) | | **Payroll_Master** | `PayrollID`, `EmpID`, `Month`, `Year`, `GrossSal`, `TotalDed`, `NetSal`, `PayDate` | `PayrollID` | `EmpID` → Employee | CHECK (`NetSal` ≥ 0) | ## 3.2 Normalization Derivation (Step-by-Step) ### Starting Relation (Un-normalized — 0NF) $$\text{PAYROLL\_UNF} = \{ \text{EmpID}, \text{EmpName}, \text{DeptName}, \text{BasicPay}, (\text{PF}, \text{ESI}, \text{PT}, \text{TDS}, \text{Month}, \text{Year}) \}$$ > **Problem:** The deductions + month/year form a **repeating group** (multi-valued attribute). ### Apply 1NF: Atomicity Remove the multi-valued group by creating a separate table: $$\text{PAYROLL\_1NF} = \{ \underline{\text{EmpID}, \text{Month}, \text{Year}}, \text{EmpName}, \text{DeptName}, \text{BasicPay}, \text{PF}, \text{ESI}, \text{PT}, \text{TDS} \}$$ - Primary Key: Composite (`EmpID`, `Month`, `Year`) - All attributes atomic ✓ ### Apply 2NF: Remove Partial Dependencies **Partial Dependency Identified:** - `EmpName`, `DeptName`, `BasicPay` depend *only* on `EmpID` (part of PK), not on full (`EmpID`, `Month`, `Year`) **Decompose:** $$\text{EMP\_2NF} = \{ \underline{\text{EmpID}}, \text{EmpName}, \text{DeptName}, \text{BasicPay} \}$$ $$\text{SALARY\_2NF} = \{ \underline{\text{EmpID}, \text{Month}, \text{Year}}, \text{PF}, \text{ESI}, \text{PT}, \text{TDS} \}$$ ### Apply 3NF: Remove Transitive Dependencies **Transitive Dependency in EMP_2NF:** - `EmpID` → `DeptID` → `DeptName, Location` - (`DeptName` depends on `DeptID`, not directly on `EmpID`) **Decompose:** $$\text{EMPLOYEE\_3NF} = \{ \underline{\text{EmpID}}, \text{EmpName}, \text{DeptID}, \text{BasicPay} \}$$ $$\text{DEPARTMENT\_3NF} = \{ \underline{\text{DeptID}}, \text{DeptName}, \text{Location} \}$$ $$\text{SALARY\_3NF} = \{ \underline{\text{EmpID}, \text{Month}, \text{Year}}, \text{PF}, \text{ESI}, \text{PT}, \text{TDS} \}$$ ### BCNF Verification For every non-trivial FD $X \rightarrow Y$, check if $X$ is a super key: - `EmpID` → all Emp attrs ✓ (super key) - `DeptID` → all Dept attrs ✓ (super key) - (`EmpID`, `Month`, `Year`) → all Salary attrs ✓ (super key) **Final BCNF Schema: 3NF = BCNF in this case ✓** ## 3.3 Gross Salary Calculation Derivation Given a monthly payroll record, compute Net Salary: $$\text{Gross Salary (GS)} = \text{Basic} + \text{DA} + \text{HRA} + \text{TA} + \text{MA} + \text{SA} + \text{OT}$$ $$\text{Total Deductions (TD)} = \text{PF} + \text{ESI} + \text{PT} + \text{TDS} + \text{Loan EMI}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Net Salary} = \text{GS} - \text{TD}}$$ **Numerical Worked Example:** | Component | Value (₹) | |-----------|-----------| | Basic Pay | 30,000 | | DA (10% of Basic) | 3,000 | | HRA (40% of Basic) | 12,000 | | TA (Transport Allowance) | 1,600 | | MA (Medical Allowance) | 1,250 | | Overtime (10 hrs @ ₹144/hr) | 1,440 | | **Gross Salary** | **49,290** | | Deduction | Value (₹) | |-----------|-----------| | PF (12% of Basic) | 3,600 | | ESI (0.75% of GS) | 369.68 | | Professional Tax | 200 | | TDS (assumed) | 1,500 | | Loan EMI | 2,000 | | **Total Deductions** | **7,669.68** | $$\text{Net Salary} = 49{,}290 - 7{,}669.68 = 41{,}620.32$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Payroll Queries | Query Type | Purpose | SQL Construct | |------------|---------|---------------| | **Aggregate Queries** | Total payroll, department-wise salary sum | `SUM()`, `GROUP BY`, `HAVING` | | **Join Queries** | Combine employee with department & salary | `INNER JOIN`, `LEFT JOIN` | | **Nested Queries** | Employees earning above department average | Subquery with `>` | | **View Creation** | Hide sensitive columns (TDS, Bank A/C) | `CREATE VIEW` | | **Triggers** | Auto-update Net Salary on PF/PT change | `AFTER INSERT/UPDATE` | | **Cursors** | Process payroll row-by-row in PL/SQL | `DECLARE CURSOR` | | **Exception Handling** | Handle division by zero / missing data | `EXCEPTION WHEN` | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ER Diagram for Payroll DBMS ```mermaid erDiagram DEPARTMENT ||--o{ EMPLOYEE : "Works_In" EMPLOYEE ||--|| SALARY_STRUCTURE : "Has" EMPLOYEE ||--o{ DEDUCTIONS : "Subjected_To" EMPLOYEE ||--o{ PAYROLL_MASTER : "Generates" EMPLOYEE ||--|| BANK_ACCOUNT : "Receives_In" EMPLOYEE ||--o{ ATTENDANCE : "Records" EMPLOYEE ||--o{ LEAVE_RECORD : "Avails" SALARY_SLAB ||--o{ EMPLOYEE : "Applicable_To" DEPARTMENT { int DeptID PK string DeptName string Location string HOD_Name } EMPLOYEE { int EmpID PK string EmpName date DOB date DOJ string Gender decimal BasicPay string Email UK string Phone int DeptID FK int DesignationID FK } SALARY_STRUCTURE { int StructID PK decimal Basic decimal DA decimal HRA decimal TA decimal MA decimal SpecialAllowance int EmpID FK } DEDUCTIONS { int DedID PK decimal PF decimal ESI decimal PT decimal TDS decimal LoanEMI int EmpID FK int Month int Year } PAYROLL_MASTER { int PayrollID PK int EmpID FK int Month int Year decimal GrossSal decimal TotalDed decimal NetSal date PayDate string Status } BANK_ACCOUNT { int AccID PK int EmpID FK string BankName string AccountNo UK string IFSC } ATTENDANCE { int AttID PK int EmpID FK date AttDate int HoursWorked int OTHours string Status } LEAVE_RECORD { int LeaveID PK int EmpID FK date FromDate date ToDate string LeaveType int DaysCount string ApprovalStatus } SALARY_SLAB { int SlabID PK decimal MinAmount decimal MaxAmount decimal TaxPercent } ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Employee Master Data
HR Input] --> B[Payroll DBMS
Central Repository] C[Attendance & Leave
Biometric/Manual] --> B D[Salary Structure
Finance Team] --> B E[Tax Slabs
Income Tax Dept] --> B B --> F[Payroll Processing Engine
Triggers and Stored Procs] F --> G{Gross Salary
Calculation} G --> H[Deduction Engine
PF ESI PT TDS] H --> I[Net Salary
Computation] I --> J[Payslip Generation
View Report] I --> K[Bank Transfer File
NEFT RTGS] I --> L[Tax Reports
Form 16 TDS] I --> M[Statutory Reports
PF ESI Challans] J --> N[Employee Self-Service Portal] K --> O[Bank Server] L --> P[Income Tax Portal] M --> Q[EPFO ESIC Portal] style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2 style C fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2 style D fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2 style E fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2 style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00 style F fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00 style G fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2 style H fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2 style I fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2 style J fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C style K fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C style L fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C style M fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C ``` ## 4.3 Normalization Flow (0NF → BCNF) ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[0NF: Un-normalized Form
Repeating groups present] -->|Atomicity| A1[1NF: First Normal Form
All attributes atomic] A1 -->|Remove partial dependencies| A2[2NF: Second Normal Form
Full functional dependency on PK] A2 -->|Remove transitive dependencies| A3[3NF: Third Normal Form
No transitive dependencies] A3 -->|Stricter check: X must be super key| A4[BCNF: Boyce-Codd NF
Lossless + Dependency Preserving] A4 -->|Further refinement| A5[4NF: Multi-valued dependencies] A4 -->|Further refinement| A6[5NF: Join dependencies] style A0 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828 style A1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 style A2 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825 style A3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32 style A4 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C style A5 fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B style A6 fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#4527A0 ``` ## 4.4 Schema Architecture (Relational Tables) ```mermaid flowchart LR DEPT[DEPARTMENT
DeptID PK
DeptName
Location] EMP[EMPLOYEE
EmpID PK
DeptID FK
BasicPay] SS[SALARY_STRUCTURE
StructID PK
EmpID FK] DED[DEDUCTIONS
DedID PK
EmpID FK
Month Year] PM[PAYROLL_MASTER
PayrollID PK
EmpID FK
NetSal] BA[BANK_ACCOUNT
AccID PK
EmpID FK] DEPT -->|1:N| EMP EMP -->|1:1| SS EMP -->|1:N| DED EMP -->|1:N| PM EMP -->|1:1| BA style DEPT fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A style EMP fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C style SS fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E style DED fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00 style PM fill:#B39DDB,stroke:#4527A0 style BA fill:#80DEEA,stroke:#00838F ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define Payroll Database Management System. List any four entities involved in a typical payroll system.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Payroll Database Management System** is a relational database system designed to automate and manage employee compensation processes including salary computation, deductions, tax calculations, and disbursement. It stores employee master data, salary structures, attendance, deductions, and monthly payroll transactions in an integrated, normalized schema ensuring data integrity, security, and ACID-compliant transaction processing. **Four Key Entities:** 1. **Employee** — stores personal & job details (EmpID, Name, DOJ, BasicPay) 2. **Department** — organizational unit (DeptID, DeptName, Location) 3. **Salary_Structure** — earnings components (Basic, DA, HRA, TA, MA, SA) 4. **Deductions** — statutory & voluntary cuts (PF, ESI, PT, TDS, Loan EMI) > **Examiner Note:** Award 1.5 marks for definition; 1.5 marks (0.375 × 4) for entities. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Differentiate between 2NF and 3NF with a payroll database example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | 2NF | 3NF | |--------|-----|-----| | **Definition** | 1NF + no partial dependency | 2NF + no transitive dependency | | **Handles** | Partial dependency on composite PK | Non-key → non-key dependency | | **Payroll Example** | Remove `EmpName` from `Salary(EmpID, Month, Year, EmpName, Basic)` since `EmpName` depends only on `EmpID` (partial) | Remove `DeptName` from `Employee(EmpID, ..., DeptID, DeptName)` since `DeptID → DeptName` is transitive via `DeptID` | > **Examiner Note:** Award 1.5 marks for clear definitions; 1.5 marks for the payroll example. --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 7] **Q. (a)** Design an ER diagram for a Payroll Database Management System of an IT company. Clearly identify entities, attributes, relationships, and cardinality. (7 marks) **(b)** Convert the ER diagram into a normalized relational schema (up to 3NF) and write the DDL statements. (7 marks) #### Model Solution: ### Part (a) — ER Diagram Design (7 marks) **Entities Identified:** 1. **Employee** (`EmpID` PK, `EmpName`, `DOB`, `DOJ`, `Gender`, `Email`, `Phone`, `BasicPay`) 2. **Department** (`DeptID` PK, `DeptName`, `Location`) 3. **Project** (`ProjID` PK, `ProjName`, `StartDate`, `EndDate`, `Budget`) 4. **Salary_Structure** (`StructID` PK, `Basic`, `DA`, `HRA`, `TA`, `SpecialAllowance`) 5. **Deductions** (`DedID` PK, `PF`, `ESI`, `PT`, `TDS`, `Month`, `Year`) 6. **Payroll_Master** (`PayrollID` PK, `GrossSal`, `TotalDed`, `NetSal`, `PayDate`) **Relationships:** | Relationship | Entities | Cardinality | |--------------|----------|-------------| | Works_In | Employee – Department | N:1 | | Assigned_To | Employee – Project | M:N | | Has_Structure | Employee – Salary_Structure | 1:1 | | Subjected_To | Employee – Deductions | 1:N | | Generates | Employee – Payroll_Master | 1:N | **ER Diagram (Mermaid):** ```mermaid erDiagram DEPARTMENT ||--o{ EMPLOYEE : "Works_In" EMPLOYEE }o--o{ PROJECT : "Assigned_To" EMPLOYEE ||--|| SALARY_STRUCTURE : "Has" EMPLOYEE ||--o{ DEDUCTIONS : "Subjected_To" EMPLOYEE ||--o{ PAYROLL_MASTER : "Generates" DEPARTMENT { int DeptID PK string DeptName string Location } EMPLOYEE { int EmpID PK string EmpName date DOB date DOJ decimal BasicPay int DeptID FK } PROJECT { int ProjID PK string ProjName decimal Budget } SALARY_STRUCTURE { int StructID PK decimal Basic decimal DA decimal HRA } DEDUCTIONS { int DedID PK decimal PF decimal ESI int EmpID FK } PAYROLL_MASTER { int PayrollID PK decimal NetSal int EmpID FK } ``` **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - Entities with attributes: 3 marks - Relationships with cardinality: 2 marks - Neat ER diagram: 2 marks --- ### Part (b) — Normalized Schema + DDL (7 marks) **Step 1: Initial Un-normalized Form** Contains all attributes in one relation → repeating groups present **Step 2: Apply 1NF** → All attributes atomic; create separate rows for each monthly record **Step 3: Apply 2NF** → Remove `EmpName`, `DeptName` from salary record (partial dependency) **Step 4: Apply 3NF** → Separate `Department` table; remove transitive dependency **Final Normalized Schema (3NF):** 1. **Department**(`DeptID` PK, `DeptName`, `Location`) 2. **Employee**(`EmpID` PK, `EmpName`, `DOB`, `DOJ`, `BasicPay`, `DeptID` FK) 3. **Project**(`ProjID` PK, `ProjName`, `Budget`) 4. **Emp_Project**(`EmpID` FK, `ProjID` FK, PRIMARY KEY(`EmpID`,`ProjID`)) — Junction table 5. **Salary_Structure**(`StructID` PK, `Basic`, `DA`, `HRA`, `TA`, `SA`, `EmpID` FK) 6. **Deductions**(`DedID` PK, `PF`, `ESI`, `PT`, `TDS`, `Month`, `Year`, `EmpID` FK) 7. **Payroll_Master**(`PayrollID` PK, `GrossSal`, `TotalDed`, `NetSal`, `PayDate`, `EmpID` FK, `Month`, `Year`) **DDL Statements:** ```sql CREATE TABLE Department ( DeptID INT PRIMARY KEY, DeptName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE, Location VARCHAR(50) ); CREATE TABLE Employee ( EmpID INT PRIMARY KEY, EmpName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, DOB DATE, DOJ DATE, BasicPay DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (BasicPay > 0), DeptID INT, FOREIGN KEY (DeptID) REFERENCES Department(DeptID) ON DELETE SET NULL ON UPDATE CASCADE ); CREATE TABLE Project ( ProjID INT PRIMARY KEY, ProjName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, Budget DECIMAL(12,2) CHECK (Budget >= 0) ); CREATE TABLE Emp_Project ( EmpID INT, ProjID INT, PRIMARY KEY (EmpID, ProjID), FOREIGN KEY (EmpID) REFERENCES Employee(EmpID), FOREIGN KEY (ProjID) REFERENCES Project(ProjID) ); CREATE TABLE Salary_Structure ( StructID INT PRIMARY KEY, Basic DECIMAL(10,2), DA DECIMAL(10,2), HRA DECIMAL(10,2), TA DECIMAL(10,2), SA DECIMAL(10,2), EmpID INT UNIQUE, FOREIGN KEY (EmpID) REFERENCES Employee(EmpID) ); CREATE TABLE Deductions ( DedID INT PRIMARY KEY, PF DECIMAL(8,2), ESI DECIMAL(8,2), PT DECIMAL(8,2), TDS DECIMAL(8,2), Month INT CHECK (Month BETWEEN 1 AND 12), Year INT CHECK (Year BETWEEN 2000 AND 2100), EmpID INT, FOREIGN KEY (EmpID) REFERENCES Employee(EmpID) ); CREATE TABLE Payroll_Master ( PayrollID INT PRIMARY KEY, GrossSal DECIMAL(12,2), TotalDed DECIMAL(10,2), NetSal DECIMAL(12,2) CHECK (NetSal >= 0), PayDate DATE, Month INT, Year INT, EmpID INT, FOREIGN KEY (EmpID) REFERENCES Employee(EmpID) ); ``` **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - Normalization step-by-step explanation: 3 marks - Final schema with PK/FK identification: 2 marks - DDL statements with constraints: 2 marks --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 7] **Q. (a)** Consider the following relation for a payroll database: `PAYROLL(EmpID, EmpName, DeptID, DeptName, BasicPay, DA, HRA, PF, ESI, Month, Year)` with FDs: - F1: `EmpID → EmpName, DeptID, DeptName, BasicPay, DA, HRA, PF, ESI` - F2: `DeptID → DeptName` - F3: `{EmpID, Month, Year} → BasicPay, DA, HRA, PF, ESI` Normalize it up to **BCNF** with proper justification. (7 marks) **(b)** Write SQL queries to: 1. Find the total payroll disbursed for each department in the year 2024. 2. Display the names of employees whose net salary is greater than the average net salary of their department. 3. Create a view `HIGH_EARNERS` showing employees with `NetSal > 50,000`. (7 marks) #### Model Solution: ### Part (a) — Normalization to BCNF (7 marks) **Initial Relation (1NF):** `R1 = PAYROLL(EmpID, EmpName, DeptID, DeptName, BasicPay, DA, HRA, PF, ESI, Month, Year)` PK: `{EmpID, Month, Year}` **Identify Functional Dependencies:** | FD | Type | Violation | |----|------|-----------| | F1: `EmpID → EmpName, DeptID, DeptName, BasicPay, DA, HRA, PF, ESI` | Partial Dependency | `EmpID` is part of composite PK but determines other attrs | | F2: `DeptID → DeptName` | Transitive Dependency | `EmpID → DeptID → DeptName` | | F3: `{EmpID, Month, Year} → ...` | Full Dependency (on PK) | OK | **Step 1: Resolve Partial Dependency (2NF)** Decompose based on F1: - **R2 (Employee)** = `{EmpID, EmpName, DeptID, DeptName, BasicPay, DA, HRA, PF, ESI}` — PK: `EmpID` - **R3 (MonthlyPay)** = `{EmpID, Month, Year}` — PK: `{EmpID, Month, Year}` **Step 2: Resolve Transitive Dependency (3NF)** Decompose R2 to remove `DeptID → DeptName`: - **R4 (Employee)** = `{EmpID, EmpName, DeptID, BasicPay, DA, HRA, PF, ESI}` — PK: `EmpID` - **R5 (Department)** = `{DeptID, DeptName}` — PK: `DeptID` **Step 3: BCNF Verification** For every FD $X \rightarrow Y$, $X$ must be a super key. | FD | Is $X$ a super key? | BCNF? | |----|---------------------|-------| | `EmpID → ...` (R4) | Yes, `EmpID` is PK | ✓ | | `DeptID → DeptName` (R5) | Yes, `DeptID` is PK | ✓ | | `{EmpID, Month, Year}` → ... (R3) | Yes, composite PK | ✓ | **All relations are in BCNF ✓** **Final BCNF Schema:** $$\text{Department} = \{ \underline{\text{DeptID}}, \text{DeptName} \}$$ $$\text{Employee} = \{ \underline{\text{EmpID}}, \text{EmpName}, \text{DeptID}, \text{BasicPay}, \text{DA}, \text{HRA}, \text{PF}, \text{ESI} \}$$ $$\text{MonthlyPay} = \{ \underline{\text{EmpID}, \text{Month}, \text{Year}} \}$$ **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - FD identification with types: 2 marks - 2NF decomposition: 2 marks - 3NF decomposition: 1.5 marks - BCNF verification: 1.5 marks --- ### Part (b) — SQL Queries (7 marks) **Query 1: Total payroll per department in 2024 (2 marks)** ```sql SELECT d.DeptName, SUM(pm.NetSal) AS TotalPayroll FROM Department d JOIN Employee e ON d.DeptID = e.DeptID JOIN Payroll_Master pm ON e.EmpID = pm.EmpID WHERE pm.Year = 2024 GROUP BY d.DeptName HAVING SUM(pm.NetSal) > 0 ORDER BY TotalPayroll DESC; ``` **Query 2: Employees earning above department average (3 marks)** ```sql SELECT e.EmpName, e.DeptID, pm.NetSal FROM Employee e JOIN Payroll_Master pm ON e.EmpID = pm.EmpID WHERE pm.NetSal > ( SELECT AVG(pm2.NetSal) FROM Employee e2 JOIN Payroll_Master pm2 ON e2.EmpID = pm2.EmpID WHERE e2.DeptID = e.DeptID ) ORDER BY e.DeptID, pm.NetSal DESC; ``` **Query 3: Create view HIGH_EARNERS (2 marks)** ```sql CREATE VIEW HIGH_EARNERS AS SELECT e.EmpID, e.EmpName, d.DeptName, pm.NetSal FROM Employee e JOIN Department d ON e.DeptID = d.DeptID JOIN Payroll_Master pm ON e.EmpID = pm.EmpID WHERE pm.NetSal > 50000 WITH CHECK OPTION; ``` **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - Query 1 with correct JOINs: 2 marks - Query 2 with correlated subquery: 3 marks - Query 3 with proper view syntax: 2 marks --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Missing Cardinality Symbols** — Many students forget to put `1`, `N`, `M` on relationship lines. This loses **1.5 marks** instantly. > 2. **Wrong PK in M:N Junction Tables** — Use **composite PK** `(EmpID, ProjID)`, not a single surrogate key. Single key loses 1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting to Add `CHECK (NetSal >= 0)`** — Business rules must be enforced. Missing constraints = **0.5 mark** deduction per query. > 4. **Confusing 3NF with BCNF** — In 3NF, $X \rightarrow Y$ is allowed if $Y$ is a *prime attribute*. In **BCNF**, NO exception. Write this clearly. > 5. **Not Using `WITH CHECK OPTION` in Views** — Views that allow INSERT/UPDATE must enforce their WHERE clause; missing this loses 0.5 mark. > 6. **Confusing PF Wage Ceiling (₹15,000) with ESI Ceiling (₹21,000)** — Examiners love asking this; wrong values = full marks cut. > 7. **Forgetting Foreign Key Constraints** — Every `FK` reference must be declared explicitly in DDL; implicit references lose marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** ### **Core Concepts (Module 7 Focus)** - [ ] Payroll DBMS is a **case study application** of DBMS — focus on design, not finance - [ ] **9 Key Entities**: Employee, Department, Salary_Structure, Deductions, Payroll_Master, Attendance, Leave, Bank_Account, Tax_Slab - [ ] **Gross Salary** = Sum of all earning components (Basic + DA + HRA + TA + MA + SA + OT) - [ ] **Net Salary** = Gross Salary − Total Deductions - [ ] **PF** = 12% of Basic; **ESI** = 0.75% of Gross (if eligible) - [ ] Standard working days = **26 days/month**; hours = **8 hours/day** ### **ER & Relational Design** - [ ] 1:N relationship → FK on the "many" side - [ ] M:N relationship → New junction table with composite PK - [ ] 1:1 relationship → FK in either table - [ ] Multi-valued attribute → Separate table - [ ] Derived attribute → Computed, NOT stored ### **Normalization Path (Must Memorize Order)** - [ ] **1NF** → Atomicity (no multi-valued) - [ ] **2NF** → No partial dependency (on composite PK) - [ ] **3NF** → No transitive dependency (non-key → non-key) - [ ] **BCNF** → For every FD, LHS must be super key ### **SQL Must-Know Queries** - [ ] Aggregate functions: `SUM()`, `AVG()`, `MAX()`, `MIN()`, `COUNT()` - [ ] `GROUP BY` + `HAVING` (filter after grouping) - [ ] `INNER JOIN`, `LEFT OUTER JOIN`, `RIGHT OUTER JOIN`, `FULL OUTER JOIN` - [ ] Correlated subqueries (use outer query column in inner WHERE) - [ ] `CREATE VIEW ... WITH CHECK OPTION` - [ ] `CREATE TRIGGER ... AFTER INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` - [ ] `DECLARE CURSOR` for row-by-row processing - [ ] `EXCEPTION WHEN OTHERS THEN ...` for error handling ### **ACID Properties (Frequently Asked)** - [ ] **Atomicity** — All or nothing - [ ] **Consistency** — Valid state → Valid state - [ ] **Isolation** — Concurrent transactions don't interfere - [ ] **Durability** — Committed data survives crashes ### **KTU Exam Pattern (Module 7)** - [ ] Part A (3 marks) → Definition + entity listing (very common) - [ ] Part B (14 marks) → Choice between (a) ER/Design + (b) Normalization/SQL - [ ] Always write **both** 1NF/2NF/3NF step with **justification** (not just final table) - [ ] Always include **cardinality** in ER diagram - [ ] Always add **constraints** (PK, FK, CHECK, UNIQUE) in DDL > **Final Tip:** In KTU exams, the **Payroll DBMS question is a "safe bet"** — it tests all 5 modules together. Master ER + Normalization + SQL + DDL + Triggers and you can score 14/14. Practice at least 3 past-year questions before the exam.

topics.title: Insurance Products Database Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT304 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 8
> - **Topic:** Insurance Products Database Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Insurance Products Database Management

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

**Insurance Products Database Management** refers to the systematic design, development, implementation, and administration of a relational (or object-relational) database system specifically engineered to store, retrieve, manipulate, and secure the voluminous data associated with the insurance industry. It encapsulates the modelling of heterogeneous insurance products (Life, Health, Motor, Property, Travel), the policy lifecycle (proposal → underwriting → issuance → renewal → maturity/claim), premium calculations, claims adjudication, agent-commission tracking, and regulatory compliance reporting.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it sits at the intersection of **Domain-Specific Database Application Engineering** and **Enterprise Information Systems Design**, drawing heavily from Module 8's focus on advanced database applications and contemporary industry implementations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 8):** This topic integrates ER modelling (Module 2), Relational Schema & Normalization (Module 3), SQL (Module 4), Transaction & Concurrency Control (Module 5), and contemporary database paradigms (Modules 6–7) into a single real-world industry case study.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an insurance company as a **massive digital filing cabinet** that handles millions of paper policies, receipts, medical reports, accident photographs, and legal documents every single day. Without a database, every clerk would rummage through endless stacks of files to find a single customer's policy number — slow, error-prone, and chaotic.

Now replace the cabinet with a **well-indexed library system**: each customer gets a unique card, every policy is logged with an ISBN-like identifier, and a librarian (the DBMS) instantly retrieves the right file using a structured query.

| Insurance Domain Object | Library/Index Analogy | Database Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Policyholder | Library Member Card | `CUSTOMER` entity (Primary Key: `cust_id`) |
| Insurance Policy | Borrowed Book | `POLICY` entity (Primary Key: `policy_id`) |
| Premium Receipt | Due-date Slip | `PREMIUM_PAYMENT` table |
| Claim Form | Damage Report | `CLAIM` table |
| Insurance Agent | Librarian | `AGENT` table |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Insurance is fundamentally a **data-intensive business**. The accuracy of a single claim payout can hinge on retrieving 20+ years of historical premium data correctly. A robust DBMS is therefore not optional — it is the *operational backbone* of the entire industry.

## 1.3 Key Entities & Metrics in Insurance DBMS

- **Premium** = `Sum Assured × Premium Rate × Risk Factor` (units: currency/year)
- **Sum Assured (SA)**: Maximum payout — a **monetary cap** defined per policy.
- **Risk Factor (RF)**: A **multiplier ∈ [0.5, 5.0]** based on actuarial tables.
- **Surrender Value**: Portion of premiums refundable on early termination.
- **Grace Period**: Typically **15 days** (life) or **30 days** (general insurance) post-due-date.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Constants used in this Module:**
> - IRDAI (Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India) compliance retention: **10 years minimum** for policy records.
> - Standard claim settlement TAT (Turn-Around-Time): **30 days** for non-investigative claims.
> - Standard commission rate for agents: **15%** of first-year premium (life insurance benchmark).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components of an Insurance Database

An Insurance Products Database Management System (IPDBMS) comprises **eight logical sub-systems**, each mapped to a set of relational tables.

1. **Customer Master Subsystem** — Personal, KYC, and demographic data.
2. **Product Catalog Subsystem** — Defines available insurance products.
3. **Policy Issuance Subsystem** — Manages policy creation, modification, and status.
4. **Premium & Billing Subsystem** — Tracks payment schedules and receipts.
5. **Claims Processing Subsystem** — Adjudicates and disburses claims.
6. **Agent & Commission Subsystem** — Manages distribution channel.
7. **Underwriting & Risk Subsystem** — Stores actuarial and risk evaluation data.
8. **Audit & Compliance Subsystem** — Regulatory, financial, and security logs.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

- **Step 1 (Customer Onboarding):** Customer submits KYC (Know Your Customer) documents. A `CUSTOMER` row is inserted with a system-generated `cust_id`.
- **Step 2 (Product Selection):** Agent selects a `PRODUCT` from the catalog. The product's premium rate, term, and coverage details are fetched.
- **Step 3 (Risk Evaluation):** Underwriter assesses the proposal; data stored in `RISK_ASSESSMENT` table.
- **Step 4 (Policy Generation):** A `POLICY` row is created with `policy_status = 'ACTIVE'` only after successful underwriting approval.
- **Step 5 (Premium Collection):** Periodic `PREMIUM_PAYMENT` rows are inserted; `last_paid_date` and `next_due_date` are updated.
- **Step 6 (Claim Filing):** On an insured event, a `CLAIM` row is created linked to the parent `POLICY`.
- **Step 7 (Claim Settlement):** Status transitions `FILED → UNDER_REVIEW → APPROVED/REJECTED → DISBURSED`.
- **Step 8 (Renewal/Maturity):** Status updated, policy extended or terminated, archival triggered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these steps matter:** Each transition must satisfy **ACID** properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability). For example, a partial premium payment that updates the policy status without updating the receipt would violate Consistency — a common KTU exam question.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables / Units | Real-World Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Annual Premium | $P = \frac{SA \times R \times RF}{1000}$ | $SA$ = Sum Assured (₹); $R$ = Base Rate per ₹1000 SA; $RF$ = Risk Factor (dimensionless) | Motor & Term Insurance premium |
| 2 | Surrender Value (Life) | $SV = \sum P_{paid} \times SV_{factor}$ | $P_{paid}$ = Total premiums paid; $SV_{factor}$ ∈ [0.30, 0.85] | Early policy termination payout |
| 3 | Claim Ratio (Loss Ratio) | $LR = \frac{\text{Total Claims Settled}}{\text{Total Premium Collected}} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** | Insurer profitability metric |
| 4 | Solvency Margin | $SM = \frac{\text{Available Solvency Margin}}{\text{Required Solvency Margin}} \times 100$ | **Must be ≥ 150%** (IRDAI norm) | Regulatory compliance |
| 5 | Persistency Ratio | $PR_{13} = \frac{\text{Policies renewed at month 13}}{\text{Policies issued 13 months ago}} \times 100$ | Industry benchmark: **> 85%** | Customer retention tracking |
| 6 | Commission Earned | $C = P_{first} \times 0.15 + P_{renewal} \times 0.075$ | Standard agent rates | Agent payroll |
| 7 | Normalization Target (IPDBMS) | $3NF \le \text{Schema} \le BCNF$ | For OLTP claims/policy data | Reduces redundancy |
| 8 | Transaction Throughput | $TPS = \frac{N_{tx}}{t}$ | $N_{tx}$ = Transactions; $t$ = time (seconds) | DBMS performance metric |
| 9 | B-Tree Index Depth | $d = \lceil \log_{m}(N) \rceil$ | $m$ = order; $N$ = records | Index design |
| 10 | Hash-Based Search Cost | $O(1)$ average | $O(n)$ worst case | Fast claim lookup |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** In Markdown tables, never write absolute values like $|x|$ inside table cells — the vertical pipe `|` breaks the table parser. Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$.

## 2.3 Why Insurance Demands a Robust DBMS — The "Why & How"

- **Why?**: Insurance processes **millions of concurrent transactions** during renewal seasons and catastrophic events (e.g., floods, pandemics).
- **How?**: A well-normalized schema (typically **3NF**), strong referential integrity via Foreign Keys, **indexes** on `policy_id`, `cust_id`, `claim_id`, and **stored procedures** for premium recalculation address these demands.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Insurance databases power everything from LIC's 300-million+ policy records to Ola's dynamic ride-insurance claims. Every digital insurance experience you encounter — from buying a policy on PhonePe to filing a motor claim on Acko — is backed by a sophisticated DBMS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Schema Design for an Insurance Database

We begin by deriving a normalized relational schema for an IPDBMS using **bottom-up ER-to-Relational mapping**.

### 3.1.1 ER-to-Relational Mapping Walkthrough

**Given the ER Model** (entities: CUSTOMER, POLICY, PRODUCT, AGENT, CLAIM, PAYMENT, BENEFICIARY):

**Mapping Rule 1 — Strong Entities:** Each strong entity becomes a table with its simple attributes as columns and the key attribute as the primary key.

- `CUSTOMER(cust_id PK, fname, lname, dob, gender, address, phone, email, pan_no, aadhaar_no, kyc_status)`
- `POLICY(policy_id PK, policy_no, issue_date, maturity_date, sum_assured, status, cust_id FK, product_id FK, agent_id FK)`
- `PRODUCT(product_id PK, product_name, product_type, base_rate, term_years, min_age, max_age)`
- `AGENT(agent_id PK, agent_name, branch_id, joining_date, commission_rate)`
- `CLAIM(claim_id PK, policy_id FK, claim_date, claim_amount, status, surveyor_id, settlement_date)`
- `PAYMENT(payment_id PK, policy_id FK, due_date, paid_date, amount, mode, receipt_no)`
- `BENEFICIARY(benef_id PK, policy_id FK, name, relation, share_pct)`

**Mapping Rule 2 — Weak Entities:** Become tables with the owner's PK as a composite FK + partial key.

- `NOMINEE(nominee_id PK, policy_id FK, name, relation, dob)` where `policy_id` is FK → POLICY.

**Mapping Rule 3 — M:N Relationships:** New junction tables.

- `POLICY_PRODUCT_RIDER(rider_id PK, policy_id FK, rider_type, additional_premium, coverage_amt)`

**Mapping Rule 4 — 1:N Relationships:** Foreign key on the "many" side.

- `POLICY` already carries `agent_id FK` and `cust_id FK`.

### 3.1.2 Normalization to 3NF — Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify Functional Dependencies:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FD}_1 &: \text{cust\_id} \rightarrow \text{fname, lname, dob, pan\_no} \\
\text{FD}_2 &: \text{policy\_id} \rightarrow \text{cust\_id, product\_id, sum\_assured, status} \\
\text{FD}_3 &: \text{product\_id} \rightarrow \text{product\_name, base\_rate, term\_years} \\
\text{FD}_4 &: \text{payment\_id} \rightarrow \text{policy\_id, amount, due\_date, paid\_date} \\
\text{FD}_5 &: \text{claim\_id} \rightarrow \text{policy\_id, claim\_amount, status}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Check for 1NF Violations:** Original table may have multi-valued attributes (e.g., `beneficiaries` containing multiple names). Split into separate rows.

**Step 3 — Check for 2NF Violations:** All non-key attributes must depend on the *whole* PK, not part of it. Given single-attribute PKs throughout, no partial dependencies exist.

**Step 4 — Check for 3NF Violations:** Look for transitive dependencies.

$$
\text{policy\_id} \rightarrow \text{product\_id} \rightarrow \text{product\_name}
$$

This is a transitive dependency. **Resolution:** Move `product_name, base_rate` to a separate `PRODUCT` table — already done in our schema. ✓

**Final Normal Form Achieved: 3NF (BCNF-compatible)** for all tables.

### 3.1.3 Premium Calculation — Worked Derivation

Given a Motor Insurance policy with:
- Sum Assured $SA = ₹5{,}00{,}000$
- Base rate $R = ₹25$ per ₹1,000 SA per annum
- Risk factor $RF = 1.4$ (urban driver, age 28)

Compute the annual premium $P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{SA \times R \times RF}{1000} \\
P &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000 \times 25 \times 1.4}{1000} \\
P &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000 \times 35}{1000} \\
P &= \frac{1{,}75{,}00{,}000}{1000} \\
P &= ₹17{,}500 \text{ per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation:**
- Line 1 → Substitute the IPDBMS premium formula.
- Line 2 → Plug in the numerical values ($SA$, $R$, $RF$).
- Line 3 → Combine $R \times RF = 25 \times 1.4 = 35$ (effective per-₹1000 rate).
- Line 4 → Multiply $5{,}00{,}000 \times 35 = 1.75 \times 10^7$.
- Line 5 → Divide by 1000 to convert from per-₹1000 to absolute premium.

### 3.1.4 Loss Ratio (Claim Ratio) Derivation

An insurer collected **₹12 crore** in premiums and settled **₹8.4 crore** in claims in FY 2023-24.

$$
\begin{aligned}
LR &= \frac{\text{Total Claims Settled}}{\text{Total Premium Collected}} \times 100 \\
LR &= \frac{8{,}40{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
LR &= 0.70 \times 100 \\
LR &= 70\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 70% combined loss ratio is **healthy** (industry ideal: 60–75%). Above 85% signals underwriting losses.

### 3.1.5 Solvency Margin Verification

Given:
- Available Solvency Margin (ASM) = ₹450 crore
- Required Solvency Margin (RSM) = ₹250 crore

$$
\begin{aligned}
SM &= \frac{ASM}{RSM} \times 100 \\
SM &= \frac{450}{250} \times 100 \\
SM &= 1.8 \times 100 \\
SM &= 180\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** Since $SM = 180\% \geq 150\%$ (IRDAI minimum), the insurer is **regulatorily compliant**. ✓

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Query Optimization in IPDBMS

| Operation | Frequency | Index Required | Cost (B-Tree) | Cost (Hash) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fetch policy by `policy_no` | Very High | B-Tree on `policy_no` | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| List all claims for a `cust_id` | High | B-Tree composite `(cust_id, status)` | $O(\log n + k)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Insert premium payment | Very High | B-Tree on `payment_id` | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Compute monthly loss ratio | Medium | None (full scan with SUM) | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Update claim status | High | B-Tree on `claim_id` | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Entity-Relationship Diagram (Insurance Domain)

```mermaid
erDiagram
    CUSTOMER ||--o{ POLICY : "owns"
    POLICY ||--|{ PAYMENT : "has"
    POLICY ||--o{ CLAIM : "raises"
    PRODUCT ||--o{ POLICY : "defines"
    AGENT ||--o{ POLICY : "sells"
    POLICY ||--o{ BENEFICIARY : "names"
    CLAIM ||--o| SURVEYOR : "assigned_to"
    POLICY ||--o{ RIDER : "includes"

    CUSTOMER {
        int cust_id PK
        string fname
        string lname
        date dob
        string pan_no
        string kyc_status
    }
    POLICY {
        int policy_id PK
        string policy_no UK
        date issue_date
        decimal sum_assured
        string status
        int cust_id FK
        int product_id FK
        int agent_id FK
    }
    PRODUCT {
        int product_id PK
        string product_name
        string product_type
        decimal base_rate
        int term_years
    }
    AGENT {
        int agent_id PK
        string agent_name
        string branch_id
    }
    PAYMENT {
        int payment_id PK
        int policy_id FK

        date paid_date
        decimal amount
    }
    CLAIM {
        int claim_id PK
        int policy_id FK

    }

        int benef_id PK

    }

        int rider_id PK

        decimal additional_premium
    }

        int surveyor_id PK
        string name
        string region
    }
```

## 4.2 Policy Lifecycle State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PROPOSAL : Customer submits form
    PROPOSAL --> UNDERWRITING : KYC verified
    UNDERWRITING --> ACTIVE : Approved
    UNDERWRITING --> REJECTED : Risk too high
    REJECTED --> [*]
    ACTIVE --> LAPSE : Premium overdue beyond grace
    ACTIVE --> LAPSED : Non-payment
    ACTIVE --> CLAIM_FILED : Insured event
    LAPSED --> REVIVED : Revival within period
    LAPSE --> ACTIVE : Payment received
    CLAIM_FILED --> UNDER_REVIEW : Documents received
    UNDER_REVIEW --> APPROVED : Surveyor confirms
    UNDER_REVIEW --> REJECTED : Fraud detected
    APPROVED --> DISBURSED : Amount paid
    ACTIVE --> MATURED : Term complete
    ACTIVE --> SURRENDERED : Early termination
    DISBURSED --> [*]
    MATURED --> [*]
    SURRENDERED --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Frontend["Frontend Layer - Customer and Agent Portal"]
        UI_CUST["Customer Web and Mobile UI"]
        UI_AGENT["Agent Dashboard"]
        UI_CLAIM["Claim Filing Portal"]
    end

    subgraph Application["Application Layer - Business Logic"]
        AP_ONBOARD["Onboarding Service"]
        AP_UNDER["Underwriting Engine"]
        AP_PREMIUM["Premium Calculator"]
        AP_CLAIM["Claims Adjudicator"]
        AP_RENEW["Renewal Scheduler"]
        AP_REPORT["Regulatory Reporter"]
    end

    subgraph DataLayer["Data Layer - IPDBMS"]
        DB_CUST[("CUSTOMER Master")]
        DB_POL[("POLICY Master")]
        DB_CLAIM[("CLAIM Master")]
        DB_PAY[("PAYMENT Ledger")]
        DB_PROD[("PRODUCT Catalog")]
        DB_LOG[("Audit and Compliance Log")]
    end

    subgraph External["External Systems"]
        EX_KYC["KYC and PAN Verification API"]
        EX_BANK["Banking and Payment Gateway"]
        EX_IRDAI["IRDAI Regulatory Portal"]
    end

    UI_CUST --> AP_ONBOARD
    UI_AGENT --> AP_UNDER
    UI_CLAIM --> AP_CLAIM

    AP_ONBOARD --> DB_CUST
    AP_ONBOARD --> EX_KYC
    AP_UNDER --> DB_POL
    AP_UNDER --> DB_PROD
    AP_PREMIUM --> DB_PAY
    AP_CLAIM --> DB_CLAIM
    AP_CLAIM --> DB_POL
    AP_RENEW --> DB_PAY
    AP_RENEW --> DB_POL
    AP_REPORT --> DB_LOG
    AP_REPORT --> EX_IRDAI

    AP_PREMIUM --> EX_BANK
    AP_CLAIM --> EX_BANK

    DB_CUST -.-> DB_LOG
    DB_POL -.-> DB_LOG
    DB_CLAIM -.-> DB_LOG
    DB_PAY -.-> DB_LOG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The frontend layer never accesses the database directly. All requests pass through the application layer, which enforces business rules, ACID transactions, and security policies — a classic **3-tier architecture** used by every major insurer.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Sum Assured" and "Risk Factor" in the context of an insurance product database. How are they used in premium computation? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Sum Assured (SA):** The maximum monetary amount payable by the insurer to the policyholder or beneficiary upon occurrence of the insured event. It is stored as a column in the `POLICY` table (data type: DECIMAL).
- **Risk Factor (RF):** A dimensionless multiplier ∈ [0.5, 5.0] derived from actuarial tables, representing the probability and severity of risk for the insured entity (e.g., age, occupation, health, vehicle type).
- **Usage in Premium:** Both are combined via the formula $P = \dfrac{SA \times R \times RF}{1000}$, where $R$ is the base rate per ₹1,000 SA. Higher $SA$ and higher $RF$ both increase the premium proportionally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway for valuation:** Define both terms and show the formula. A clear formula carries 1 mark.

---

### Question 2: What is a "Loss Ratio" in insurance database reporting? Write its formula and state the industry benchmark. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Loss Ratio (LR):** The ratio of total claims settled by an insurer to the total premium collected over a period, expressed as a percentage. It is a key profitability metric stored in the `REPORTS` table.
- **Formula:** $\displaystyle LR = \dfrac{\text{Total Claims Settled}}{\text{Total Premium Collected}} \times 100$
- **Benchmark:** A healthy insurer maintains a **combined loss ratio between 60% and 75%**. Above 85% indicates underwriting losses; below 50% may suggest overpricing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**A (a)** Design an ER diagram for an Insurance Products Database covering entities: CUSTOMER, POLICY, PRODUCT, AGENT, CLAIM, and PAYMENT. Clearly state all attributes, primary keys, and relationships (with cardinality). **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The ER diagram (already drawn in Section 4.1 above) contains the following:

| Entity | Primary Key | Notable Attributes | Relationship |
|---|---|---|---|
| CUSTOMER | `cust_id` | `pan_no, kyc_status` | 1:N with POLICY |
| POLICY | `policy_id` | `sum_assured, status` | N:1 with CUSTOMER, PRODUCT, AGENT |
| PRODUCT | `product_id` | `base_rate, term_years` | 1:N with POLICY |
| AGENT | `agent_id` | `branch_id` | 1:N with POLICY |
| CLAIM | `claim_id` | `claim_amount, status` | N:1 with POLICY |
| PAYMENT | `payment_id` | `due_date, paid_date` | N:1 with POLICY |

**Cardinalities:**
- `CUSTOMER (1) : POLICY (N)` — One customer can hold multiple policies.
- `PRODUCT (1) : POLICY (N)` — One product type can issue many policies.
- `AGENT (1) : POLICY (N)` — One agent sells many policies.
- `POLICY (1) : CLAIM (N)` — A policy may generate multiple claims.
- `POLICY (1) : PAYMENT (N)` — A policy has many premium installments.

**Award marks:** ER Diagram = 4 marks; Cardinality & PK notation = 2 marks; Attribute completeness = 1 mark.

---

**A (b)** Consider a Motor Insurance policy with Sum Assured = ₹3,00,000, Base Rate = ₹30 per ₹1,000 SA, and Risk Factor = 1.25. Calculate the annual premium. Also, compute the Loss Ratio if total claims settled = ₹4,50,00,000 and total premium collected = ₹6,00,00,000. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Annual Premium Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{SA \times R \times RF}{1000} \\
P &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000 \times 30 \times 1.25}{1000} \\
P &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000 \times 37.5}{1000} \\
P &= \frac{1{,}12{,}50{,}000}{1000} \\
P &= ₹11{,}250 \text{ per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Loss Ratio Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LR &= \frac{\text{Total Claims Settled}}{\text{Total Premium Collected}} \times 100 \\
LR &= \frac{4{,}50{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
LR &= 0.75 \times 100 \\
LR &= 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The annual premium is **₹11,250**, and the Loss Ratio is **75%**, which lies within the healthy industry range of **60%–75%**.

**Award marks:** Formula = 1 mark; Premium computation = 3 marks; LR formula = 1 mark; LR computation = 2 marks.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**B (a)** Explain the major entities and their attributes in an Insurance Products Database Management System. Discuss the role of normalization in ensuring data integrity for insurance data. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Major Entities:**

1. **CUSTOMER** — `cust_id, fname, lname, dob, gender, address, phone, email, pan_no, aadhaar_no, kyc_status`. Stores personal and KYC information.
2. **POLICY** — `policy_id, policy_no, issue_date, maturity_date, sum_assured, premium_amt, status, cust_id FK, product_id FK, agent_id FK`. Central transactional entity.
3. **PRODUCT** — `product_id, product_name, product_type (LIFE/HEALTH/MOTOR/PROPERTY), base_rate, term_years`. Catalog of offerings.
4. **AGENT** — `agent_id, name, branch_id, joining_date, commission_rate`. Distribution channel.
5. **CLAIM** — `claim_id, policy_id FK, claim_date, claim_amount, status, surveyor_id, settlement_date`.
6. **PAYMENT** — `payment_id, policy_id FK, due_date, paid_date, amount, mode`.
7. **BENEFICIARY** — `benef_id, policy_id FK, name, relation, share_pct`.

**Role of Normalization:**

- **1NF:** Eliminates multi-valued attributes (e.g., multiple beneficiaries stored in one row).
- **2NF:** Removes partial dependencies on composite keys.
- **3NF:** Eliminates transitive dependencies (e.g., `policy → product → product_name` → split into separate tables).
- **BCNF (optional):** Stronger form ensuring every determinant is a candidate key — used in highly sensitive claim tables.

**Why it matters for insurance:**
- A non-normalized schema could cause **update anomalies** (e.g., changing `base_rate` of a product must be done in thousands of policy rows).
- Reduces **storage redundancy** and improves **transaction throughput**.
- Ensures **referential integrity** (FKs) — a deleted POLICY must cascade or restrict CLAIM rows.

**Award marks:** Entity listing with attributes = 4 marks; Normalization discussion = 3 marks.

---

**B (b)** Write SQL DDL statements to create the POLICY, CUSTOMER, and CLAIM tables with appropriate primary and foreign key constraints. Also write a query to find the top 5 customers by total premium paid in the year 2024. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**SQL DDL Statements:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE CUSTOMER (
    cust_id      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    fname        VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    lname        VARCHAR(50),
    dob          DATE,
    pan_no       CHAR(10) UNIQUE,
    kyc_status   VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'PENDING'
);

CREATE TABLE POLICY (
    policy_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    policy_no    VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    issue_date   DATE NOT NULL,
    maturity_date DATE,
    sum_assured  DECIMAL(12,2) CHECK (sum_assured > 0),
    status       VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'ACTIVE',
    cust_id      INT,
    product_id   INT,
    agent_id     INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (cust_id)    REFERENCES CUSTOMER(cust_id) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    FOREIGN KEY (product_id) REFERENCES PRODUCT(product_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (agent_id)   REFERENCES AGENT(agent_id)
);

CREATE TABLE CLAIM (
    claim_id        INT PRIMARY KEY,
    policy_id       INT NOT NULL,
    claim_date      DATE NOT NULL,
    claim_amount    DECIMAL(12,2) CHECK (claim_amount > 0),
    status          VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'FILED',
    settlement_date DATE,
    FOREIGN KEY (policy_id) REFERENCES POLICY(policy_id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);
```

**Query — Top 5 Customers by Total Premium Paid in 2024:**

```sql
SELECT  c.cust_id,
        c.fname,
        c.lname,
        SUM(p.amount) AS total_premium_2024
FROM    CUSTOMER c
JOIN    POLICY   po ON c.cust_id = po.cust_id
JOIN    PAYMENT  p  ON po.policy_id = p.policy_id
WHERE   YEAR(p.paid_date) = 2024
        AND p.paid_date IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY c.cust_id, c.fname, c.lname
ORDER BY total_premium_2024 DESC
LIMIT 5;
```

**Award marks:** DDL with PK = 2 marks; DDL with FK + CHECK = 2 marks; SQL query correctness = 3 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Formula writing:** Always write the formula first, *then* substitute values. Students who directly write the final number lose 1 mark.
> 2. **FK direction:** Remember that FKs are placed on the "many" side of a 1:N relationship. Putting `cust_id` inside CUSTOMER is a classic error.
> 3. **Aggregation:** When using `GROUP BY`, every non-aggregated column in SELECT must appear in `GROUP BY` — omitting this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Unit consistency:** Always specify currency (₹) and time unit (per annum) to avoid ambiguity.
> 5. **3NF proof:** Just stating "schema is in 3NF" without showing FDs and checking transitive dependencies earns **zero marks**. Show the work.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Insurance database = OLTP-heavy system** demanding strong ACID compliance, BCNF/3NF normalization, and B-Tree indexing on policy/claim keys.
- ⭐ **Eight core sub-systems:** Customer, Product, Policy, Premium, Claim, Agent, Underwriting, Audit.
- ⭐ **Premium Formula:** $\displaystyle P = \frac{SA \times R \times RF}{1000}$ — must be memorized.
- ⭐ **Loss Ratio Formula:** $\displaystyle LR = \frac{\text{Claims Settled}}{\text{Premium Collected}} \times 100$ — healthy range 60–75%.
- ⭐ **Solvency Margin ≥ 150%** is the **IRDAI minimum** for regulatory compliance.
- ⭐ **Persistency Ratio** at month 13 should be **> 85%** for life insurers.
- ⭐ **Standard agent commission:** **15%** first year, **7.5%** renewal.
- ⭐ **Foreign keys** must cascade or restrict to preserve referential integrity.
- ⭐ **3-tier architecture** (Frontend → Application → Database) is the industry standard — DB never directly exposed.
- ⭐ **Index wisely:** B-Tree for range queries (`policy_no`, `paid_date`); Hash for exact-match lookups (`cust_id`).
- ⭐ **State transitions** of a policy: PROPOSAL → UNDERWRITING → ACTIVE → (CLAIM or MATURITY or SURRENDER).
- ⭐ **Audit logs** are mandatory — IRDAI requires **minimum 10-year retention** of policy records.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hospital Management System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT304 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 9
> - **Topic:** Hospital Management System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hospital Management System (HMS)** is a **comprehensive, integrated, relational information system** designed to manage the **administrative, financial, and clinical operations** of a healthcare organization. From a **DBMS perspective**, it is a database-backed software application that uses a **relational schema** to store, retrieve, manipulate, and secure data about patients, doctors, appointments, treatments, billing, and inventory.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, the Hospital Management System is studied as a **case-driven application of database design**, illustrating the full life-cycle of database engineering:

1. **Requirement Analysis** (identifying user views)
2. **Conceptual Design** (ER / EER modeling)
3. **Logical Design** (Mapping ER to Relational Schema)
4. **Schema Refinement** (Normalization up to BCNF / 4NF)
5. **Physical Implementation** (SQL DDL, DML, DCL, TCL)
6. **Query Processing & Transaction Management**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Hospital" Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine a hospital as a **giant filing cabinet** where patient files, doctor schedules, bills, and test reports are all scattered across different rooms. A *Hospital Management System* is like replacing this cabinet with a **single, intelligent, computerized index** — one where the receptionist, the doctor, the lab technician, and the billing desk all see **the same up-to-date information** instantly, without paper, without duplication, and without confusion.

Think of HMS as **three interlocking wheels**:
- **Wheel 1 — The Reception Wheel**: Registers patients, books appointments, allocates rooms.
- **Wheel 2 — The Treatment Wheel**: Doctors record diagnoses, prescribe medicines, order lab tests.
- **Wheel 3 — The Billing Wheel**: Generates invoices, tracks payments, manages insurance claims.

The **DBMS is the axle** that holds all three wheels together — every transaction on one wheel is immediately visible on the others.

## 1.3 Key Components / Modules of HMS

| Module | Functional Responsibility | Primary Data Entities |
|---|---|---|
| **Patient Management** | Registration, demographics, history | `PATIENT`, `MEDICAL_HISTORY` |
| **Doctor Management** | Profiles, specialization, schedules | `DOCTOR`, `DEPARTMENT` |
| **Appointment Scheduling** | Booking, slot allocation, cancellation | `APPOINTMENT` |
| **OPD/IPD Management** | Outpatient & Inpatient tracking | `VISIT`, `ADMISSION`, `ROOM` |
| **Pharmacy & Inventory** | Drug stock, expiry, vendor | `MEDICINE`, `STOCK` |
| **Laboratory** | Test requests, results | `LAB_TEST`, `TEST_RESULT` |
| **Billing & Finance** | Invoices, payments, insurance | `BILL`, `PAYMENT` |
| **Staff Management** | Nurses, technicians, admin | `STAFF`, `NURSE` |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard metrics used in HMS databases are: **patient admission rate**, **average length of stay (ALOS)**, **bed occupancy ratio**, and **revenue per patient** — all derived via SQL aggregate queries.

## 1.4 Why HMS is a Critical DBMS Case Study

- **High data volume**: A mid-size hospital generates **1–10 GB/day** of structured and semi-structured data.
- **Strict ACID requirements**: Medical records demand **Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability**.
- **Complex relationships**: One patient may have many doctors, treatments, bills, and lab tests — a classic **many-to-many** scenario.
- **Security & Compliance**: HIPAA / DISHA-style regulations require strict access control (DCL — `GRANT` / `REVOKE`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of Database Design for HMS

The design of the Hospital Management System follows the canonical **6-phase methodology** prescribed by Elmasri & Navathe and adopted by KTU:

### Phase 1 — Requirements Gathering
Identify user groups: **Receptionist, Doctor, Nurse, Lab Technician, Pharmacist, Billing Officer, Administrator**.

For each user, derive **functional requirements**:
- Receptionist → "Register a new patient and assign a unique Patient ID."
- Doctor → "View patient's medical history and add a new prescription."
- Billing → "Generate a consolidated bill for a patient's visit."

### Phase 2 — Conceptual Design (ER Model)
Identify **Entities**, **Attributes**, and **Relationships**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU examinations, students are expected to draw the **ER diagram** and label **cardinality ratios** (1:1, 1:N, M:N) and **participation constraints** (total / partial).

### Phase 3 — Logical Design (Mapping to Relations)
Apply the **ER-to-Relational Mapping Algorithm** (7-step algorithm from Elmasri):
1. Map each **strong entity** → 1 relation.
2. Map each **weak entity** → 1 relation + foreign key to owner.
3. Map each **1:1 relationship** → merge with either side OR create separate relation.
4. Map each **1:N relationship** → add foreign key on the "N" side.
5. Map each **M:N relationship** → create a new relation with two foreign keys + relationship attributes.
6. Map each **multivalued attribute** → separate relation.
7. Map each **n-ary relationship** → separate relation with n foreign keys.

### Phase 4 — Schema Refinement (Normalization)
Apply **1NF → 2NF → 3NF → BCNF** to eliminate anomalies.

### Phase 5 — Physical Design
Choose **indexing strategy** (B+ tree on primary key, hash on frequently searched fields), **file organization**, and **clustering**.

### Phase 6 — Security & Implementation
Define **user roles**, apply **GRANT/REVOKE**, define **views** for restricted access.

## 2.2 Entity Identification for HMS

| Entity | Type | Identifier (PK) | Key Attributes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `PATIENT` | Strong | `patient_id` | name, DOB, gender, blood_group, phone, address |
| `DOCTOR` | Strong | `doctor_id` | name, specialization, salary, dept_id (FK) |
| `DEPARTMENT` | Strong | `dept_id` | dept_name, head_doctor_id (FK) |
| `APPOINTMENT` | Strong | `appointment_id` | patient_id (FK), doctor_id (FK), date, time, status |
| `ROOM` | Strong | `room_id` | room_type, charges, status |
| `ADMISSION` | Strong | `admission_id` | patient_id (FK), room_id (FK), admit_date, discharge_date |
| `MEDICINE` | Strong | `medicine_id` | name, manufacturer, price, stock |
| `PRESCRIPTION` | Strong (associative) | `prescription_id` | patient_id, doctor_id, medicine_id, dosage, duration |
| `LAB_TEST` | Strong | `test_id` | test_name, cost, result, patient_id (FK) |
| `BILL` | Strong | `bill_id` | patient_id (FK), total_amount, date, status |
| `PAYMENT` | Weak | `payment_id` | bill_id (FK), amount, mode, date |
| `NURSE` | Strong | `nurse_id` | name, shift, ward_id (FK) |
| `MEDICAL_RECORD` | Weak | `record_id` | patient_id (FK), diagnosis, treatment_date |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Rule Sheet

| Rule / Concept | Statement | Application in HMS |
|---|---|---|
| **1NF Rule** | Every cell must hold an **atomic (indivisible)** value. | Split "phone_numbers" cell into separate rows. |
| **2NF Rule** | Must be in 1NF + **no partial dependency** (every non-key attr depends on full PK). | In `PRESCRIPTION(pres_id, med_id, patient_id, dosage, med_name)`, `med_name` depends only on `med_id` → split. |
| **3NF Rule** | Must be in 2NF + **no transitive dependency**. | If `DOCTOR(doctor_id, dept_id, dept_name)` — `dept_name` transitively depends on `doctor_id` via `dept_id` → split. |
| **BCNF Rule** | For every FD $X \rightarrow Y$, $X$ must be a **superkey**. | Resolve overlapping candidate keys. |
| **Cardinality 1:1** | Merge with either entity table or create separate relation. | `ADMISSION` ↔ `ROOM` (one patient per room at a time). |
| **Cardinality 1:N** | Place FK on the **"many" side**. | `DEPARTMENT` (1) → `DOCTOR` (N). |
| **Cardinality M:N** | Create a **new associative relation** with 2 FKs. | `DOCTOR` ↔ `PATIENT` (via `APPOINTMENT`). |
| **Referential Integrity** | Every FK value must match a PK in the parent table OR be NULL. | `appointment.doctor_id` must exist in `DOCTOR`. |
| **ACID Property** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. | Bill generation: payment + inventory update must be atomic. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter in engineering:** Hospital databases are **mission-critical**. A violation of referential integrity could mean a patient is prescribed a medicine from a non-existent manufacturer, or billed for a treatment never rendered. Normalization guarantees **data integrity**; transactions guarantee **data consistency**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare Informatics**: HMS is the prototype for **Electronic Health Records (EHR)** systems like Epic, Cerner, and Allscripts.
- **Data Warehousing**: Historical HMS data feeds **OLAP cubes** for disease-pattern analysis and resource planning.
- **IoT & AI Integration**: Modern HMS ingests real-time vitals from medical IoT devices into time-series tables.
- **Audit & Compliance**: KTU emphasizes that DCL commands (`GRANT`, `REVOKE`) enforce **role-based access** to comply with healthcare data protection laws.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 ER-to-Relational Mapping for HMS (Exhaustive 7-Step Derivation)

We derive the relational schema from the conceptual ER model of HMS.

### Step 1: Mapping Strong Entities

Each strong entity becomes an independent relation with its primary key.

$$
\text{PATIENT}(\underline{\text{patient\_id}},\ \text{name},\ \text{DOB},\ \text{gender},\ \text{blood\_group},\ \text{phone},\ \text{address})
$$

$$
\text{DOCTOR}(\underline{\text{doctor\_id}},\ \text{name},\ \text{specialization},\ \text{salary},\ \text{dept\_id}^*)
$$

$$
\text{DEPARTMENT}(\underline{\text{dept\_id}},\ \text{dept\_name},\ \text{head\_doctor\_id}^*)
$$

**Logic:** Underlined attributes are **primary keys**. The `*` superscript marks **foreign keys** to be added in later steps.

### Step 2: Mapping Weak Entities

Weak entities depend on a **owner/identifying entity** and have a **partial key**.

$$
\text{PAYMENT}(\underline{\text{payment\_id}},\ \text{amount},\ \text{mode},\ \text{date},\ \text{bill\_id}^*)
$$

$$
\text{MEDICAL\_RECORD}(\underline{\text{record\_id}},\ \text{patient\_id}^*,\ \text{diagnosis},\ \text{treatment\_date})
$$

**Logic:** The primary key of a weak entity is the **owner's PK + the partial key**, e.g., `MEDICAL_RECORD.PK = (patient_id, record_id)`.

### Step 3: Mapping the 1:1 Relationship — `ADMISSION` ↔ `ROOM`

We choose to **merge** the foreign key into the `ADMISSION` relation (optional side).

$$
\text{ADMISSION}(\underline{\text{admission\_id}},\ \text{patient\_id}^*,\ \text{room\_id}^*,\ \text{admit\_date},\ \text{discharge\_date})
$$

**Logic:** Since each admission occupies **one room** and each room (at a moment) holds **one patient**, this is 1:1. The FK goes to the side with **total participation** (here, ADMISSION is total).

### Step 4: Mapping the 1:N Relationship — `DEPARTMENT` (1) → `DOCTOR` (N)

The foreign key is placed on the **"many" side** — i.e., the `DOCTOR` table.

$$
\text{DOCTOR}(\underline{\text{doctor\_id}},\ \text{name},\ \text{specialization},\ \text{salary},\ \text{dept\_id}^*)
$$

**Logic:** Each doctor belongs to exactly one department, but a department has many doctors. The FK `dept_id` on the `DOCTOR` side preserves this rule.

### Step 5: Mapping the M:N Relationship — `DOCTOR` ↔ `PATIENT` via `APPOINTMENT`

A new associative relation is created.

$$
\text{APPOINTMENT}(\underline{\text{appointment\_id}},\ \text{patient\_id}^*,\ \text{doctor\_id}^*,\ \text{date},\ \text{time},\ \text{status})
$$

**Logic:** One patient can see many doctors; one doctor can see many patients. The M:N cardinality mandates a **junction table** with **two foreign keys** and any **relationship attributes** (date, time, status).

### Step 6: Mapping the M:N Relationship with Attribute — `PRESCRIPTION`

A doctor prescribes multiple medicines; a medicine can be prescribed by multiple doctors.

$$
\text{PRESCRIPTION}(\underline{\text{prescription\_id}},\ \text{patient\_id}^*,\ \text{doctor\_id}^*,\ \text{medicine\_id}^*,\ \text{dosage},\ \text{duration})
$$

**Logic:** `dosage` and `duration` are attributes of the **ternary relationship** among Doctor, Patient, and Medicine, so they belong to the new relation.

### Step 7: Mapping Multivalued Attributes — `PATIENT_PHONE`

If a patient has multiple phone numbers, create a separate relation.

$$
\text{PATIENT\_PHONE}(\underline{\text{patient\_id}^*,\ \text{phone\_number}})
$$

**Logic:** The composite PK is `(patient_id, phone_number)` because the same patient cannot have the **same** phone number listed twice.

## 3.2 Normalization Walk-Through (Analytical Matrix)

Consider an **un-normalized** HMS relation for prescriptions:

$$
\text{PRES\_UNF} = (\text{pres\_id},\ \text{patient\_id},\ \text{patient\_name},\ \text{doctor\_id},\ \text{doctor\_name},\ \text{med\_id},\ \text{med\_name},\ \text{dosage})
$$

### Step A — Convert to 1NF

All cells must be atomic. Suppose a prescription can have multiple medicines — we **flatten** them:

| pres_id | patient_id | med_id | dosage |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | PT1 | M1 | 1-0-1 |
| P1 | PT1 | M2 | 0-1-0 |

**Logic:** 1NF eliminates repeating groups by ensuring each row represents one fact.

### Step B — Convert to 2NF

In 1NF, the PK is `(pres_id, med_id)`. Check for **partial dependencies**:
- `patient_name` depends only on `patient_id` (part of PK only if patient_id is in PK — but here it is not; it depends on patient_id alone).
- `med_name` depends only on `med_id`.

These are **partial dependencies** → split into:
- `PRESCRIPTION(pres_id, patient_id, med_id, dosage)`
- `PATIENT(patient_id, patient_name)`
- `MEDICINE(med_id, med_name)`

### Step C — Convert to 3NF

In `DOCTOR(doctor_id, dept_id, dept_name)`:
- `dept_name` depends on `dept_id`, not directly on `doctor_id` → **transitive dependency**.

Split into:
- `DOCTOR(doctor_id, name, dept_id)`
- `DEPARTMENT(dept_id, dept_name)`

### Step D — Convert to BCNF

Suppose a relation `ROOM_ALLOCATION(room_id, patient_id, date, charge)` with FD `room_id, date → patient_id` and `patient_id, date → room_id`. Both `room_id, date` and `patient_id, date` are **candidate keys**, but if `charge` depends only on `room_id`, then BCNF is violated because `room_id` is not a superkey of the whole relation.

Split to satisfy BCNF:
- `ROOM_CHARGE(room_id, charge)`
- `ROOM_ALLOCATION(room_id, patient_id, date)`

## 3.3 Sample SQL DDL for HMS (Step-by-Step)

Below is the **complete SQL Data Definition Language** script for the HMS database, mapped directly from the ER design.

```sql
-- Step 1: Create DEPT table (no FKs)
CREATE TABLE DEPARTMENT (
    dept_id      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_name    VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    head_doctor_id INT
);

-- Step 2: Create DOCTOR table (FK to DEPARTMENT)
CREATE TABLE DOCTOR (
    doctor_id      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name           VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    specialization VARCHAR(50),
    salary         DECIMAL(10,2),
    dept_id        INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES DEPARTMENT(dept_id)
        ON DELETE SET NULL
        ON UPDATE CASCADE
);

-- Step 3: Create PATIENT table
CREATE TABLE PATIENT (
    patient_id  INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name        VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    DOB         DATE,
    gender      CHAR(1) CHECK (gender IN ('M','F','O')),
    blood_group VARCHAR(5),
    phone       VARCHAR(15),
    address     VARCHAR(200)
);

-- Step 4: Create APPOINTMENT (M:N associative)
CREATE TABLE APPOINTMENT (
    appointment_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    patient_id     INT NOT NULL,
    doctor_id      INT NOT NULL,
    appt_date      DATE NOT NULL,
    appt_time      TIME,
    status         VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'Scheduled',
    FOREIGN KEY (patient_id) REFERENCES PATIENT(patient_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (doctor_id)  REFERENCES DOCTOR(doctor_id)
);

-- Step 5: Create ROOM and ADMISSION
CREATE TABLE ROOM (
    room_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    room_type VARCHAR(20),
    charges   DECIMAL(10,2),
    status    VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'Available'
);

CREATE TABLE ADMISSION (
    admission_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    patient_id     INT NOT NULL,
    room_id        INT,
    admit_date     DATE NOT NULL,
    discharge_date DATE,
    FOREIGN KEY (patient_id) REFERENCES PATIENT(patient_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (room_id)    REFERENCES ROOM(room_id)
);

-- Step 6: Create MEDICINE and PRESCRIPTION
CREATE TABLE MEDICINE (
    med_id       INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name         VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    manufacturer VARCHAR(100),
    price        DECIMAL(10,2),
    stock        INT DEFAULT 0
);

CREATE TABLE PRESCRIPTION (
    prescription_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    patient_id      INT NOT NULL,
    doctor_id       INT NOT NULL,
    med_id          INT NOT NULL,
    dosage          VARCHAR(50),
    duration        VARCHAR(50),
    FOREIGN KEY (patient_id) REFERENCES PATIENT(patient_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (doctor_id)  REFERENCES DOCTOR(doctor_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (med_id)     REFERENCES MEDICINE(med_id)
);

-- Step 7: Create BILL and PAYMENT (weak)
CREATE TABLE BILL (
    bill_id      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    patient_id   INT NOT NULL,
    total_amount DECIMAL(12,2),
    bill_date    DATE,
    status       VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'Unpaid',
    FOREIGN KEY (patient_id) REFERENCES PATIENT(patient_id)
);

CREATE TABLE PAYMENT (
    payment_id INT,
    bill_id    INT NOT NULL,
    amount     DECIMAL(12,2),
    mode       VARCHAR(30),
    pay_date   DATE,
    PRIMARY KEY (payment_id, bill_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (bill_id) REFERENCES BILL(bill_id)
);
```

## 3.4 Common Analytical Queries (For KTU Exam Practice)

**Query 1 — List all patients admitted in the last 30 days with their assigned doctors.**

$$
\pi_{\text{patient.name, doctor.name, admission.admit\_date}}\left(\text{PATIENT} \bowtie_{\text{PATIENT.patient\_id = ADMISSION.patient\_id}} \text{ADMISSION} \bowtie \text{APPOINTMENT} \bowtie \text{DOCTOR}\right)
$$

**Query 2 — Find the total revenue generated by each department.**

$$
\text{SELECT}\ d.\text{dept\_name},\ \text{SUM}(b.\text{total\_amount})\ \text{AS revenue}\ \text{FROM}\ \text{DEPARTMENT}\ d\ \text{JOIN}\ \text{DOCTOR}\ \text{do}\ \text{ON}\ d.\text{dept\_id = do.dept\_id}\ \text{JOIN}\ \text{APPOINTMENT}\ a\ \text{ON}\ \text{do.doctor\_id = a.doctor\_id}\ \text{JOIN}\ \text{BILL}\ b\ \text{ON}\ a.\text{patient\_id = b.patient\_id}\ \text{GROUP BY}\ d.\text{dept\_name};
$$

**Logic:** This uses a **multi-way JOIN** followed by `GROUP BY` and `SUM()` — a classic OLAP-style aggregation over the HMS star schema.

**Reading the diagram:**
- `||--o{` denotes **one-to-many** (mandatory one, optional many).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Patient Module] --> S1[(HMS Central Database)]
    A2[Doctor Module] --> S1
    A3[Appointment Module] --> S1
    A4[Admission Module] --> S1
    A5[Pharmacy Module] --> S1
    A6[Lab Module] --> S1
    A7[Billing Module] --> S1
    A8[Staff Module] --> S1

    S1 --> O1[Generate Reports]
    S1 --> O2[Trigger Alerts]
    S1 --> O3[Enforce Access Control]
    S1 --> O4[Maintain Audit Logs]

    O1 --> R1[Management Dashboards]
    O2 --> R2[Critical Care Notifications]
    O3 --> R3[Role Based Views]
    O4 --> R4[Compliance Reports]
```

**Logic of the flow:**
- The **left column** is the *input* (operational modules feeding data).
- The **central cylinder** represents the **relational DBMS engine**.
- The **right column** is the *output* (reporting, alerts, security, audits).
- Every arrow crossing the central cylinder is a **transaction** (commit / rollback).

## 4.3 Schema Layered View (3-Schema Architecture Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Internal_Level
        IL[Physical Storage: B+ Tree Index on patient_id, Hash on bill_id]
    end

    subgraph Conceptual_Level
        CL[Logical Schema: 13 Relations with FK Constraints]
    end

    subgraph External_Level
        E1[Doctor View: Sees only assigned patients]
        E2[Billing View: Sees only bill and payment tables]
        E3[Receptionist View: Sees only patient and appointment tables]
    end

    IL --> CL
    CL --> E1
    CL --> E2
    CL --> E3
```

**Logic:** This reflects the **ANSI/SPARC 3-schema architecture**. Each user group interacts with a **view** defined via SQL `CREATE VIEW`, ensuring *data abstraction* and *security*.
