### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between strong entity and weak entity with an HMS example.**

**Model Answer (Structured as a Table):**

| Aspect | Strong Entity | Weak Entity |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Has its own primary key. | Cannot exist without an owner entity. |
| **Identifier** | Self-sufficient PK. | PK = owner PK + partial key. |
| **HMS Example** | `DOCTOR(doctor_id)` | `MEDICAL_RECORD(record_id, patient_id)` |
| **Representation** | Single rectangle. | Double rectangle. |
| **Relationship** | Identifying or non-identifying. | Always **identifying** (double diamond). |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design an ER diagram for a Hospital Management System with entities `PATIENT`, `DOCTOR`, `APPOINTMENT`, `DEPARTMENT`, `MEDICINE`, and `PRESCRIPTION`. Specify all attributes, primary keys, relationships, and cardinality ratios.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Map the above ER diagram into a relational schema and normalize it up to **3NF**.

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Entities and Attributes:**

1. `PATIENT` (`patient_id` PK, `name`, `DOB`, `gender`, `blood_group`, `phone`, `address`)
2. `DOCTOR` (`doctor_id` PK, `name`, `specialization`, `salary`, `dept_id` FK)
3. `DEPARTMENT` (`dept_id` PK, `dept_name`, `head_doctor_id` FK)
4. `APPOINTMENT` (`appointment_id` PK, `patient_id` FK, `doctor_id` FK, `date`, `time`, `status`)
5. `MEDICINE` (`med_id` PK, `name`, `manufacturer`, `price`, `stock`)
6. `PRESCRIPTION` (`prescription_id` PK, `patient_id` FK, `doctor_id` FK, `med_id` FK, `dosage`, `duration`)

**Relationships and Cardinality:**

| Relationship | Entities Involved | Cardinality | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `belongs_to` | DOCTOR — DEPARTMENT | N:1 | Each doctor works in one department. |
| `books` | PATIENT — APPOINTMENT | 1:N | A patient can book many appointments. |
| `schedules` | DOCTOR — APPOINTMENT | 1:N | A doctor handles many appointments. |
| `receives` | PATIENT — PRESCRIPTION | 1:N | A patient gets many prescriptions. |
| `writes` | DOCTOR — PRESCRIPTION | 1:N | A doctor writes many prescriptions. |
| `listed_in` | MEDICINE — PRESCRIPTION | M:N | A prescription lists many medicines. |
| `head_of` | DOCTOR — DEPARTMENT | 1:1 | One doctor heads a department. |

**ER Diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
erDiagram
    DEPARTMENT ||--o{ DOCTOR : "belongs_to"
    DOCTOR ||--o{ APPOINTMENT : "schedules"
    PATIENT ||--o{ APPOINTMENT : "books"
    PATIENT ||--o{ PRESCRIPTION : "receives"
    DOCTOR ||--o{ PRESCRIPTION : "writes"
    MEDICINE ||--o{ PRESCRIPTION : "listed_in"
    DOCTOR ||--|| DEPARTMENT : "head_of"
```

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks for listing all 6 entities with PKs.
- 2 marks for identifying relationships.
- 1 mark for cardinality ratios.
- 1 mark for participation constraints.
- 1 mark for diagram neatness and labeled attributes.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Initial Relations from ER Mapping:**

$$
\text{DEPARTMENT}(\underline{\text{dept\_id}},\ \text{dept\_name},\ \text{head\_doctor\_id}^*)
$$

$$
\text{DOCTOR}(\underline{\text{doctor\_id}},\ \text{name},\ \text{specialization},\ \text{salary},\ \text{dept\_id}^*)
$$

$$
\text{PATIENT}(\underline{\text{patient\_id}},\ \text{name},\ \text{DOB},\ \text{gender},\ \text{blood\_group},\ \text{phone},\ \text{address})
$$

$$
\text{APPOINTMENT}(\underline{\text{appointment\_id}},\ \text{patient\_id}^*,\ \text{doctor\_id}^*,\ \text{date},\ \text{time},\ \text{status})
$$

$$
\text{MEDICINE}(\underline{\text{med\_id}},\ \text{name},\ \text{manufacturer},\ \text{price},\ \text{stock})
$$

$$
\text{PRESCRIPTION}(\underline{\text{prescription\_id}},\ \text{patient\_id}^*,\ \text{doctor\_id}^*,\ \text{med\_id}^*,\ \text{dosage},\ \text{duration})
$$

**Step 2 — 1NF Check:** All attributes are atomic. ✓

**Step 3 — 2NF Check:** In `PRESCRIPTION`, the PK is `prescription_id` (single attribute), so no partial dependency exists. ✓

**Step 4 — 3NF Check:** In `DOCTOR`, `dept_id` is a FK to DEPARTMENT. The relation has no non-key attribute transitively dependent on the PK. ✓ However, we should split `DEPARTMENT` to remove transitive dependency of `dept_name` via `dept_id` — already done since `dept_id` IS the PK of DEPARTMENT. So the schema is in **3NF**.

**Final 3NF Schema:** As listed in Step 1. All foreign keys declared with `REFERENCES` clause.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks for relational mapping (1 mark per 3 relations).
- 2 marks for 1NF/2NF identification.
- 2 marks for 3NF transformation.
- 1 mark for FK constraint specification.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **functional dependency** and identify all FDs in the relation:

$$
R = (\text{patient\_id},\ \text{patient\_name},\ \text{doctor\_id},\ \text{doctor\_name},\ \text{dept\_id},\ \text{dept\_name})
$$

Find the **candidate key** and normalize up to **BCNF**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write SQL `CREATE TABLE` statements for `PATIENT`, `DOCTOR`, and `APPOINTMENT` with all necessary constraints (PK, FK, CHECK, NOT NULL).

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Identify Functional Dependencies:**

$$
\text{patient\_id} \rightarrow \text{patient\_name}
$$

$$
\text{doctor\_id} \rightarrow \text{doctor\_name},\ \text{dept\_id}
$$

$$
\text{dept\_id} \rightarrow \text{dept\_name}
$$

**Step 2 — Find Candidate Key:**

Compute the closure of `patient_id, doctor_id`:

$$
\{\text{patient\_id, doctor\_id}\}^{+} = \{\text{patient\_id, patient\_name, doctor\_id, doctor\_name, dept\_id, dept\_name}\}
$$

All attributes are derived, so the **candidate key** is `(patient_id, doctor_id)`.

**Step 3 — Identify Highest Normal Form Violations:**

- `doctor_name` depends only on `doctor_id` → **partial dependency** (2NF violation).
- `dept_name` depends transitively on `(patient_id, doctor_id)` via `doctor_id → dept_id → dept_name` → **3NF violation**.

**Step 4 — Normalize to 3NF (and BCNF):**

**Decomposition:**

$$
R_1 = (\underline{\text{patient\_id}},\ \text{patient\_name})
$$

$$
R_2 = (\underline{\text{doctor\_id}},\ \text{doctor\_name},\ \text{dept\_id}^*)
$$

$$
R_3 = (\underline{\text{dept\_id}},\ \text{dept\_name})
$$

$$
R_4 = (\underline{\text{patient\_id, doctor\_id}})
$$

**BCNF Verification:**
- In $R_1$: `patient_id → patient_name`, and `patient_id` is the PK (superkey). ✓
- In $R_2$: `doctor_id` is the PK. ✓
- In $R_3$: `dept_id` is the PK. ✓
- In $R_4$: Composite PK, no non-key attribute. ✓

Hence the decomposed schema is in **BCNF**.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 1 mark for FD identification.
- 1 mark for candidate key computation.
- 2 marks for 2NF/3NF violation detection.
- 2 marks for correct decomposition.
- 1 mark for BCNF verification.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**SQL DDL Statements:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE PATIENT (
    patient_id  INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name        VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    DOB         DATE NOT NULL,
    gender      CHAR(1) CHECK (gender IN ('M','F','O')),
    blood_group VARCHAR(5) CHECK (blood_group IN ('A+','A-','B+','B-','AB+','AB-','O+','O-')),
    phone       VARCHAR(15) UNIQUE,
    address     VARCHAR(200)
);

CREATE TABLE DOCTOR (
    doctor_id      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name           VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    specialization VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    salary         DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (salary > 0),
    dept_id        INT NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES DEPARTMENT(dept_id)
        ON DELETE CASCADE
);

CREATE TABLE APPOINTMENT (
    appointment_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    patient_id     INT NOT NULL,
    doctor_id      INT NOT NULL,
    appt_date      DATE NOT NULL CHECK (appt_date >= CURRENT_DATE),
    appt_time      TIME NOT NULL,
    status         VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'Scheduled'
                   CHECK (status IN ('Scheduled','Completed','Cancelled')),
    FOREIGN KEY (patient_id) REFERENCES PATIENT(patient_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (doctor_id)  REFERENCES DOCTOR(doctor_id)
);
```

**Explanation of Constraints Used:**

- `PRIMARY KEY` → Uniquely identifies each row (entity integrity).
- `FOREIGN KEY ... REFERENCES` → Enforces referential integrity.
- `CHECK (gender IN ...)` → Domain integrity (only valid values).
- `NOT NULL` → Required fields cannot be empty.
- `UNIQUE` → No duplicate phone numbers allowed.
- `DEFAULT` → Auto-fills status if not provided.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 1 mark per correct `CREATE TABLE` (3 marks total).
- 1 mark for PK constraint.
- 1 mark for FK constraint.
- 1 mark for CHECK + NOT NULL.
- 1 mark for proper data types and lengths.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mark FKs with asterisks** in the relational schema — examiners deduct 1 mark per missing FK notation.
> 2. **Confusing 2NF with 3NF**: 2NF is about *partial* dependency; 3NF is about *transitive* dependency. Mixing these is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Drawing the ER diagram with cardinalities in the wrong direction**: e.g., writing `1:N` when it should be `N:1`. Always state which entity is on the "1" side.
> 4. **Forgetting ON DELETE / ON UPDATE clauses** in FK definitions — KTU 2024 specifically tests referential actions.
> 5. **Using `VARCHAR(50)` for monetary fields** — should always be `DECIMAL(10,2)` to avoid floating-point errors in billing.
> 6. **Not specifying candidate key properly** — a relation may have **multiple** candidate keys; list **all** of them and pick the **primary** one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Summary for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ⭐ **HMS = Database-centric application** with 8+ major modules (Patient, Doctor, Appointment, Admission, Pharmacy, Lab, Billing, Staff).
- ⭐ **ER modeling** is the **first** step — identify **6–13 entities** with PKs, then relationships with **cardinality ratios**.
- ⭐ **M:N relationships** → always create a **junction table** with 2 FKs and relationship attributes.
- ⭐ **1:1 relationships** → merge with either side (typically the side with **total participation**).
- ⭐ **1:N relationships** → place FK on the **"many" side**.
- ⭐ **Weak entities** → double rectangle; PK = owner PK + partial key.
- ⭐ **Multivalued attributes** → separate relation with composite PK.
- ⭐ **Normalization sequence**: 1NF (atomic) → 2NF (no partial dep) → 3NF (no transitive dep) → BCNF (every determinant is a superkey).
- ⭐ **ACID properties** are non-negotiable for HMS — billing and prescription updates must be **atomic**.
- ⭐ **Referential actions**: `CASCADE`, `SET NULL`, `RESTRICT`, `NO ACTION` — choose based on business rule.
- ⭐ **Use `DECIMAL`** for money, **never** `FLOAT` or `DOUBLE`.
- ⭐ **SQL CHECK constraints** enforce domain integrity (e.g., gender ∈ {M, F, O}).
- ⭐ **VIEWS** provide user-level security (Doctor View vs. Billing View vs. Receptionist View).
- ⭐ **3-Schema Architecture** (Internal / Conceptual / External) is the KTU-recommended approach for HMS design.
- ⭐ **Indexes** (B+ tree on PK, hash on frequently searched columns) optimize HMS query performance.
- ⭐ **Roles and Privileges** via `GRANT` and `REVOKE` enforce healthcare data protection (HIPAA/DISHA style).
- ⭐ **Common HMS queries for exam practice**:
  - List patients admitted in the last N days.
  - Find total revenue per department.
  - Find doctors with the most appointments.
  - List medicines with stock below threshold.
  - Generate consolidated bill (JOIN BILL + APPOINTMENT + PRESCRIPTION + LAB_TEST).
- ⭐ **Star Schema** for HMS Data Warehouse: `FACT_BILL(patient_id, doctor_id, date, amount)` + dimension tables `DIM_PATIENT`, `DIM_DOCTOR`, `DIM_DATE`.
- ⭐ **Transaction example**: Bill generation must atomically update `BILL` and decrement `MEDICINE.stock` — if either fails, **ROLLBACK**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU:** **"E-R-M-N"** = **E**ntity → **R**elationship → **M**apping → **N**ormalization. Master these four steps in order, and you can solve any HMS question in the KTU 2024 ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bank Transactions Management System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT304 - DATABASE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 10
> - **Topic:** Bank Transactions Management System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Bank Transactions Management System (BTMS)** is a centralized, relational database–backed software application that models, stores, manipulates, and retrieves all financial activities (deposits, withdrawals, transfers, loan disbursements, interest calculations) of a banking institution while enforcing the **ACID properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability)** mandated by the relational model.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme DBMS syllabus, Module 10 is dedicated to **Real-World Database Application Design** — translating an actual organizational process (here, banking) into a fully normalized relational schema, implementing it using **SQL (DDL, DML, DCL, TCL)**, and embedding business rules through **Triggers, Views, and Stored Procedures**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 10 of PBITT304 emphasizes **practical relational database design** and the use of **active database features (triggers)** to enforce integrity constraints that cannot be expressed via simple `CHECK` clauses.

### Core Entities of a Bank Database

| Entity | Purpose | Key Attributes |
|---|---|---|
| **Branch** | Physical bank location | `branch_id`, `branch_name`, `city` |
| **Customer** | Account holder details | `cust_id`, `name`, `address`, `phone` |
| **Account** | Bank account record | `acc_no`, `cust_id`, `branch_id`, `balance`, `acc_type` |
| **Transaction** | Log of every debit/credit | `trans_id`, `acc_no`, `amount`, `trans_type`, `trans_date` |
| **Loan** | Loan sanctioned to customer | `loan_id`, `cust_id`, `amount`, `interest_rate` |
| **Employee** | Bank staff | `emp_id`, `name`, `branch_id`, `salary` |

> [!NOTE]
> **ACID** is the cornerstone of any banking database — without it, two simultaneous withdrawals could drain an account beyond zero, or a transfer could "vanish" between accounts on a system crash.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **massive digital ledger book** that the entire bank shares in real-time. When you withdraw ₹5,000 from an ATM:

1. The teller/ATM is like a *messenger* who finds the right page (your account row) in the giant book.
2. The messenger writes `-5000` on your page and simultaneously writes `+5000` on the bank's cash ledger page.
3. Crucially, **both writes happen together, or neither does** — otherwise the books become inconsistent (this is *Atomicity*).
4. If two messengers try to update the same page simultaneously, they must *take turns* (this is *Isolation* via locking).

The **DBMS** is the *strict librarian* who enforces these rules so the ledger never lies.

### Physical/Design Metrics Used in Banking Systems

- **Currency Precision**: Stored as `DECIMAL(15,2)` — never `FLOAT` (to avoid binary rounding errors on money).
- **Daily Transaction Limit**: A typical business rule is **₹50,000** for non-privileged accounts.
- **Minimum Balance Requirement (MBR)**: Commonly **₹1,000** for savings accounts.
- **Audit Retention Period**: Transaction logs are retained for **7 years** (RBI mandate).
</br>

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always store monetary values using exact numeric types (`DECIMAL`/`NUMERIC`) — **never** use floating-point (`FLOAT`, `REAL`, `DOUBLE`) in financial systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Relational Schema of BTMS

A banking database must satisfy multiple **Functional Dependencies** to be in **3NF (Third Normal Form)**, eliminating update, insertion, and deletion anomalies.

The canonical KTU-style schema for a Bank Transactions Management System is as follows:

**BRANCH**(<u>branch_id</u>, branch_name, city)

**CUSTOMER**(<u>cust_id</u>, cust_name, address, phone, email)

**ACCOUNT**(<u>acc_no</u>, cust_id*, branch_id*, acc_type, balance, open_date)

**TRANSACTION**(<u>trans_id</u>, acc_no*, amount, trans_type, trans_date, remarks)

**LOAN**(<u>loan_id</u>, cust_id*, amount, interest_rate, start_date)

**EMPLOYEE**(<u>emp_id</u>, emp_name, branch_id*, salary, designation)

\* denotes a **foreign key** reference.

### High-Yield Formula Sheet (DBMS-Theory Mapping)

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Real-World Banking Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Atomicity** | Transaction = `BEGIN ... COMMIT` / `ROLLBACK` | Ensures debit + credit occur as one indivisible unit |
| **Balance Update Rule** | `New_Balance = Old_Balance ± Amount` | Maintains ledger correctness |
| **Minimum Balance Check** | `IF New_Balance < MBR THEN DENY` | Prevents accounts going into unhealthy state |
| **Transfer Equation** | `A.balance -= X` AND `B.balance += X` | Cross-account money movement |
| **Interest Calculation (Simple)** | `I = (P × R × T) / 100` | Annual interest on savings |
| **Interest Calculation (Compound)** | `A = P × (1 + R/n)^(n×t)` | Loan EMI accumulation |
| **1NF Rule** | Every cell must be *atomic* (no multi-valued fields) | Avoids `phone1, phone2, phone3` in one column |
| **2NF Rule** | No *partial dependency* on composite PK | Splits transaction-customer mixed tables |
| **3NF Rule** | No *transitive dependency* between non-key attrs | `city` should not depend on `branch_name` |
| **ACID Mnemonic** | **A**tomicity, **C**onsistency, **I**solation, **D**urability | Foundation of all reliable DB transactions |
| **Lock Granularity** | `Row-level` > `Page-level` > `Table-level` | Fine-grained locking = higher concurrency |
| **Codd's 12 Rules** | 12 conditions for a truly relational DBMS | Theoretical benchmark (rarely fully met) |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **KTU board exam**, the single most tested theoretical fact on banking databases is the mapping of **ACID properties to transaction commands** (`BEGIN`, `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT`).

### Why Each Normal Form Matters in Banking

- **1NF**: A customer's multiple phone numbers must not be stored in a single column like `'9876543210, 9123456789'`. Use a separate `CUSTOMER_PHONE` table.
- **2NF**: If `TRANSACTION` had `(trans_id, acc_no, cust_name)` as composite PK, then `cust_name` would *partially* depend only on `acc_no`. Move `cust_name` to `CUSTOMER` table.
- **3NF**: In `EMPLOYEE(emp_id, branch_id, branch_name, salary)`, `branch_name` transitively depends on `emp_id` via `branch_id`. Move `branch_name` to `BRANCH` table.
- **BCNF**: A stricter 3NF — every determinant must be a candidate key.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently award 7-mark questions on "Normalize this banking schema up to BCNF." Master the **anomaly → dependency → decomposition** reasoning chain.

### Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Online Banking Portals**: Every login session executes dozens of `SELECT`, `UPDATE`, and `INSERT` queries against this schema.
- **ATM Networks**: Real-time `SELECT FOR UPDATE` queries lock the account row during withdrawal.
- **Mobile Banking Apps**: Use the same schema over REST APIs with read replicas.
- **Core Banking Systems (CBS)**: Indian banks like SBI use proprietary RDBMS (Oracle/DB2) hosting exactly this kind of schema on **mainframes**.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Relational Schema Construction (Entity-Relationship Mapping)

**Step 1**: Identify entities from the banking problem statement.
- Strong entities: Branch, Customer, Account, Loan, Employee
- Weak entity: Transaction (depends on Account for existence)

**Step 2**: Identify relationships.
- `Customer` 1 — N `Account` (one customer can have multiple accounts)
- `Branch` 1 — N `Account`
- `Account` 1 — N `Transaction`
- `Customer` 1 — N `Loan`

**Step 3**: Convert ER to relational tables.
- For 1:N relationships, place the **foreign key on the "many" side**.
- For weak entities, include the owner's PK as part of the weak entity's PK (or as a FK with `ON DELETE CASCADE`).

**Step 4**: Apply primary keys.

$$\text{ACCOUNT}_{PK} = \{acc\_no\}$$

$$\text{TRANSACTION}_{PK} = \{trans\_id\}$$

$$\text{TRANSACTION}_{FK} = \{acc\_no \rightarrow ACCOUNT.acc\_no\}$$

**Step 5**: Apply referential integrity constraints.

$$\forall t \in \text{TRANSACTION}, \quad \exists a \in \text{ACCOUNT} \mid t.acc\_no = a.acc\_no$$

**Step 6**: Identify candidate keys and choose primary keys based on minimality and stability.

### Derivation 2 — ACID Property Verification for a Fund Transfer

Consider a transfer of ₹10,000 from Account A → Account B.

**Initial State:**
$$A.balance = 50000, \quad B.balance = 30000$$

**Transaction T:**
```
T1: UPDATE ACCOUNT SET balance = balance - 10000 WHERE acc_no = 'A';
T2: UPDATE ACCOUNT SET balance = balance + 10000 WHERE acc_no = 'B';
COMMIT;
```

**Atomicity check**: If T1 succeeds but T2 fails (e.g., power outage), the system must `ROLLBACK` T1.

**Consistency check**: Total money in system before and after must remain equal.

$$\text{Before: } A + B = 50000 + 30000 = 80000$$

$$\text{After: } A + B = 40000 + 40000 = 80000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Isolation check**: If another transaction reads A's balance between T1 and T2, it must see the *old* value (read-committed) or be blocked.

**Durability check**: Once `COMMIT` is acknowledged, the new balances survive any subsequent system crash (via write-ahead logging).

### Derivation 3 — Simple vs Compound Interest on a Loan

A customer takes a **Personal Loan** of ₹5,00,000 at **10.5% per annum** for **3 years**.

**Simple Interest Formula:**

$$I = \frac{P \times R \times T}{100}$$

Substituting $P = 500000$, $R = 10.5$, $T = 3$:

$$I = \frac{500000 \times 10.5 \times 3}{100}$$

$$I = \frac{15750000}{100} = 157500$$

Total amount payable:

$$A = P + I = 500000 + 157500 = 657500$$

**Compound Interest Formula** (compounded annually, $n = 1$):

$$A = P \times \left(1 + \frac{R}{n}\right)^{n \times t}$$

Substituting:

$$A = 500000 \times \left(1 + \frac{10.5}{100}\right)^{3}$$

$$A = 500000 \times (1.105)^{3}$$

$$A = 500000 \times 1.349232625$$

$$A = 674616.31$$

Compound interest is therefore:

$$I_{compound} = A - P = 674616.31 - 500000 = 174616.31$$

**EMI Calculation** (Equal Monthly Instalment):

$$\text{EMI} = \frac{P \times r \times (1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$$

where $P = 500000$, annual $R = 10.5\%$, monthly $r = 10.5 / 12 / 100 = 0.00875$, $n = 36$ months.

Step 1: Compute $(1+r)^n$:

$$(1.00875)^{36} \approx 1.3693$$

Step 2: Numerator:

$$500000 \times 0.00875 \times 1.3693 = 500000 \times 0.011981 = 5990.55$$

Step 3: Denominator:

$$1.3693 - 1 = 0.3693$$

Step 4: Final EMI:

$$\text{EMI} = \frac{5990.55}{0.3693} \approx 16220.12$$

### Derivation 4 — Normalization Walkthrough (Up to BCNF)

**Original Un-normalized Table `LOAN_DETAILS`:**

| loan_id | cust_name | cust_phone | branch_name | amount | interest |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | Anil | 9876543210 | Kochi | 500000 | 10.5 |

**Functional Dependencies identified:**

$$FD_1: loan\_id \rightarrow cust\_name, cust\_phone, branch\_name, amount, interest$$

$$FD_2: cust\_name \rightarrow cust\_phone$$

$$FD_3: branch\_name \rightarrow \text{(city info — assumed)}$$

**1NF**: Already atomic (each cell holds a single value). ✓

**2NF**: The PK `loan_id` is not composite, so no partial dependency exists. ✓

**3NF**: `cust_phone` transitively depends on `loan_id` via `cust_name`. This violates 3NF.

**Decomposition (3NF):**

- `LOAN(loan_id, cust_name, branch_name, amount, interest)`
- `CUSTOMER(cust_name, cust_phone)`
- `BRANCH(branch_name, city)`

**BCNF Check**: In `LOAN`, the determinant is `loan_id` (a candidate key) ✓. In `CUSTOMER`, determinant is `cust_name` (a candidate key) ✓. In `BRANCH`, determinant is `branch_name` (a candidate key) ✓.

Hence the schema is now in **BCNF**.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Entity-Relationship Diagram (Bank Schema)

```mermaid
erDiagram
    BRANCH ||--o{ ACCOUNT : "has"
    BRANCH ||--o{ EMPLOYEE : "employs"
    CUSTOMER ||--o{ ACCOUNT : "owns"
    CUSTOMER ||--o{ LOAN : "borrows"
    ACCOUNT ||--o{ TRANSACTION : "records"

    BRANCH {
        int branch_id PK
        string branch_name
        string city
    }
    CUSTOMER {
        int cust_id PK
        string cust_name
        string address
        string phone
    }
    ACCOUNT {
        int acc_no PK
        int cust_id FK
        int branch_id FK
        string acc_type
        decimal balance
        date open_date
    }
    TRANSACTION {
        int trans_id PK
        int acc_no FK
        decimal amount
        string trans_type
        datetime trans_date
        string remarks
    }
    LOAN {
        int loan_id PK
        int cust_id FK
        decimal amount
        float interest_rate
        date start_date
    }
    EMPLOYEE {
        int emp_id PK
        string emp_name
        int branch_id FK
        decimal salary
        string designation
    }
```

### Diagram 2 — Fund Transfer Transaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User initiates transfer of Rs 10000 from A to B"] --> B["BEGIN TRANSACTION"]
    B --> C["Lock Account A row SELECT FOR UPDATE"]
    C --> D{"A balance >= 10000?"}
    D -- No --> E["ROLLBACK and show insufficient funds error"]
    D -- Yes --> F["UPDATE Account A balance minus 10000"]
    F --> G["UPDATE Account B balance plus 10000"]
    G --> H{"Both updates succeeded?"}
    H -- No --> I["ROLLBACK entire transaction"]
    H -- Yes --> J["INSERT into TRANSACTION log"]
    J --> K["COMMIT transaction"]
    K --> L["Release locks and notify user"]
```

### Diagram 3 — ACID Properties Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["Atomicity"]
        A1["All operations succeed together"]
        A2["OR all are rolled back together"]
    end
    subgraph C["Consistency"]
        C1["Valid state before"]
        C2["Valid state after"]
    end
    subgraph I["Isolation"]
        I1["Concurrent transactions"]
        I2["Do not interfere with each other"]
    end
    subgraph D["Durability"]
        D1["Committed data survives"]
        D2["System crashes and power failures"]
    end
```

### Diagram 4 — Trigger Execution Flow (Minimum Balance Enforcement)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["BEFORE UPDATE trigger on ACCOUNT"] --> Q["Capture OLD balance and NEW balance"]
    Q --> R["Calculate difference as withdraw amount"]
    R --> S{"NEW balance < 1000 MBR?"}
    S -- Yes --> T["Signal SQLSTATE 45000 error"]
    S -- No --> U["Allow UPDATE to proceed"]
    T --> V["ROLLBACK the offending UPDATE"]
```

### Diagram 5 — Normalization Hierarchy (Applied to Banking Schema)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UF["Unnormalized Form - repeating groups in transaction list"] --> N1["1NF - all attributes atomic"]
    N1 --> N2["2NF - remove partial dependencies"]
    N2 --> N3["3NF - remove transitive dependencies"]
    N3 --> BC["BCNF - every determinant is a candidate key"]
    BC --> N4["4NF - remove multi-valued dependencies"]
    N4 --> N5["5NF - remove join dependencies"]
```
</br>

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the ACID properties of a database transaction. Which property ensures that committed data is never lost even after a system crash?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The ACID properties of a database transaction are:

- **A – Atomicity**: A transaction is treated as a single, indivisible unit of work. Either **all** its operations execute successfully and are committed, or **none** of them take effect (rolled back).

- **C – Consistency**: A transaction transforms the database from one **valid state** to another valid state, preserving all defined integrity constraints (primary keys, foreign keys, `CHECK` constraints, triggers).

- **I – Isolation**: Concurrent transactions execute as if they were running **sequentially**. The intermediate state of one transaction is invisible to others until it commits.

- **D – Durability**: Once a transaction has been **committed**, its effects survive any subsequent system failure (crash, power loss). This is achieved via **write-ahead logs (WAL)** and database backups.

The property that ensures committed data is never lost after a system crash is **Durability**.

*(Award: 1 mark per property explanation + 1 mark for naming durability correctly + 1 mark for the WAL/backup mention = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any three anomalies that occur in an unnormalized banking database. Which normal form removes each of these anomalies?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Anomaly | Description | Normal Form that Removes It |
|---|---|---|
| **Update Anomaly** | Updating a customer's address in one row leaves stale values in other duplicate rows | **2NF** (removes partial dependencies) |
| **Insertion Anomaly** | Cannot record a new branch until at least one customer/employee is assigned to it | **3NF** (removes transitive dependencies) |
| **Deletion Anomaly** | Deleting the last account of a customer also deletes the customer's contact info | **BCNF / 3NF** (splits mixed-purpose tables) |

*(Award: 1 mark per anomaly + correct normal form mapping)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3a. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Design a relational database schema for a Bank Transactions Management System with at least 5 entities. Clearly specify primary keys, foreign keys, and referential integrity constraints. Write the SQL DDL statements to create the tables with appropriate data types. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The five core entities and their DDL statements are:

```sql
CREATE TABLE Branch (
    branch_id      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    branch_name    VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    city           VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE Customer (
    cust_id        INT PRIMARY KEY,
    cust_name      VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    address        VARCHAR(200),
    phone          VARCHAR(15) UNIQUE,
    email          VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE
);

CREATE TABLE Account (
    acc_no         INT PRIMARY KEY,
    cust_id        INT NOT NULL,
    branch_id      INT NOT NULL,
    acc_type       VARCHAR(20) CHECK (acc_type IN ('Savings','Current','Fixed')),
    balance        DECIMAL(15,2) NOT NULL DEFAULT 0.00
                   CHECK (balance >= 0),
    open_date      DATE NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (cust_id)   REFERENCES Customer(cust_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (branch_id) REFERENCES Branch(branch_id)
);

CREATE TABLE Transaction (
    trans_id       INT PRIMARY KEY,
    acc_no         INT NOT NULL,
    amount         DECIMAL(15,2) NOT NULL CHECK (amount > 0),
    trans_type     VARCHAR(10) CHECK (trans_type IN ('Deposit','Withdrawal')),
    trans_date     DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    remarks        VARCHAR(200),
    FOREIGN KEY (acc_no) REFERENCES Account(acc_no) ON DELETE CASCADE
);

CREATE TABLE Loan (
    loan_id        INT PRIMARY KEY,
    cust_id        INT NOT NULL,
    amount         DECIMAL(15,2) NOT NULL CHECK (amount > 0),
    interest_rate  DECIMAL(5,2) NOT NULL CHECK (interest_rate BETWEEN 0 AND 50),
    start_date     DATE NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (cust_id) REFERENCES Customer(cust_id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- ER identification (1 mark)
- Schema design with PKs and FKs clearly marked (2 marks)
- Correct SQL DDL syntax for 5 tables (3 marks)
- Appropriate data types and constraints (1 mark)

---

**Q3b. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Write SQL queries for the following requirements on the Bank schema above:*
*(i) Find the names of all customers who have a balance greater than ₹1,00,000 in any account. (3 marks)*
*(ii) Transfer ₹5,000 from Account 101 to Account 102 using a single transaction. (4 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Customers with balance > ₹1,00,000:**

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT C.cust_name
FROM Customer C
JOIN Account A ON C.cust_id = A.cust_id
WHERE A.balance > 100000;
```

Step-by-step explanation:
- `JOIN` combines the `Customer` and `Account` tables on `cust_id`.
- `WHERE A.balance > 100000` filters the rows.
- `DISTINCT` ensures each customer name appears only once even if they have multiple qualifying accounts.

**(ii) Fund Transfer Transaction:**

```sql
START TRANSACTION;

-- Check sufficient balance in source account
SELECT balance FROM Account WHERE acc_no = 101 FOR UPDATE;

UPDATE Account SET balance = balance - 5000 WHERE acc_no = 101;

UPDATE Account SET balance = balance + 5000 WHERE acc_no = 102;

-- Log both transactions
INSERT INTO Transaction (trans_id, acc_no, amount, trans_type, remarks)
VALUES (1001, 101, 5000, 'Withdrawal', 'Transfer to A/c 102');

INSERT INTO Transaction (trans_id, acc_no, amount, trans_type, remarks)
VALUES (1002, 102, 5000, 'Deposit', 'Transfer from A/c 101');

COMMIT;
```

Explanation:
- `START TRANSACTION` begins an atomic unit.
- `FOR UPDATE` acquires a row-level lock to prevent concurrent modifications.
- Two `UPDATE` statements modify the source and destination balances.
- Two `INSERT` statements record the audit trail.
- `COMMIT` makes all changes permanent; any failure before this point triggers an automatic `ROLLBACK`.

**Mark Distribution for 3b:**
- Correct query for (i) with proper join (3 marks)
- Complete transaction block for (ii) with lock + updates + log + commit (4 marks)

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4a. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain the concept of database triggers with a banking example. Write a BEFORE UPDATE trigger on the Account table that prevents a withdrawal if it would cause the balance to fall below ₹1,000 (Minimum Balance Requirement). (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A **trigger** is a stored procedure that automatically executes in response to specified events (`INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`) on a particular table. Triggers are used to enforce complex business rules that cannot be expressed via standard `CHECK` constraints.

**Trigger Implementation:**

```sql
DELIMITER $$

CREATE TRIGGER check_min_balance
BEFORE UPDATE ON Account
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    IF NEW.balance < 1000 THEN
        SIGNAL SQLSTATE '45000'
        SET MESSAGE_TEXT = 'ERROR: Balance cannot fall below Minimum Balance Requirement of Rs 1000';
    END IF;
END$$

DELIMITER ;
```

Explanation:
- `BEFORE UPDATE` ensures the trigger runs *before* the change is committed to the row.
- `NEW.balance` refers to the updated value; `OLD.balance` would refer to the existing value.
- `SIGNAL SQLSTATE '45000'` raises a custom error, causing the entire `UPDATE` to be rolled back automatically.
- The `DELIMITER $$ ... DELIMITER ;` syntax is MySQL-specific to allow `;` inside the trigger body.

Test case:
```sql
-- Suppose account 101 has balance 1500
UPDATE Account SET balance = balance - 600 WHERE acc_no = 101;
-- New balance = 900, which is < 1000 → trigger fires → UPDATE is rejected
```

**Mark Distribution for 4a:**
- Conceptual explanation of triggers (2 marks)
- Correct trigger syntax with proper keywords (3 marks)
- Correct logic for MBR enforcement (2 marks)

---

**Q4b. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Demonstrate the concept of a database view by creating a view `Customer_Account_Summary` that shows each customer's name, account number, balance, and account type. Write three different SQL queries that utilize this view. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A **view** is a virtual table defined by a `SELECT` query. It does not store data physically but dynamically retrieves results from underlying base tables when referenced.

**View Creation:**

```sql
CREATE VIEW Customer_Account_Summary AS
SELECT  C.cust_id,
        C.cust_name,
        A.acc_no,
        A.balance,
        A.acc_type
FROM Customer C
JOIN Account A ON C.cust_id = A.cust_id;
```

**Three Queries Using the View:**

**(i) Find all customers who have Savings accounts:**

```sql
SELECT cust_name, acc_no, balance
FROM Customer_Account_Summary
WHERE acc_type = 'Savings';
```

**(ii) Calculate the total balance held by each customer:**

```sql
SELECT cust_name, SUM(balance) AS total_balance
FROM Customer_Account_Summary
GROUP BY cust_id, cust_name
ORDER BY total_balance DESC;
```

**(iii) Find the average balance across all account types:**

```sql
SELECT acc_type, AVG(balance) AS avg_balance, COUNT(*) AS num_accounts
FROM Customer_Account_Summary
GROUP BY acc_type;
```

**Mark Distribution for 4b:**
- Correct view creation syntax with JOIN (2 marks)
- Three meaningful and correctly written queries using the view (1.5 marks each = 4.5 marks; round to 5)
- Brief explanation of what a view is (0.5 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using `FLOAT` for money**: Marks deducted if balance columns use `FLOAT`/`REAL` instead of `DECIMAL`.
> 2. **Forgetting `COMMIT` in transaction blocks**: The transfer query will appear "correct" syntactically but lacks transaction control — expect **2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Confusing `OLD` and `NEW` in triggers**: Mixing up these keywords is the most common error in trigger-based questions.
> 4. **Using `SELECT *` in view definitions**: Examiners prefer explicit column lists for clarity and to avoid breakage when base tables change.
> 5. **Missing `ON DELETE CASCADE`**: When a customer is deleted, orphaned accounts/loans violate referential integrity — KTU examiners specifically test this.
> 6. **Writing `WHERE balance >= 0` as a CHECK but allowing negative updates via triggers**: Always combine both layers of protection.

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Entities of BTMS**: Branch, Customer, Account, Transaction, Loan, Employee — must be in your memory verbatim.
- **Always use `DECIMAL(15,2)`** for monetary values — never `FLOAT`.
- **ACID = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability** — the four pillars of reliable transactions.
- **Transaction Control Commands**: `BEGIN/START TRANSACTION`, `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT`.
- **Normalization Hierarchy**: 1NF (atomic) → 2NF (no partial dep) → 3NF (no transitive dep) → BCNF → 4NF → 5NF.
- **Foreign Key Placement**: Always on the "many" side of a 1:N relationship.
- **Triggers**: `BEFORE`/`AFTER` + `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` + `FOR EACH ROW` — used for MBR, audit logs, etc.
- **Views**: Virtual tables; use them for security (column-level hiding) and query simplification.
- **Row Locking**: `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` prevents dirty reads during concurrent transactions.
- **Audit Logging**: Always `INSERT` into a `Transaction` table for every debit/credit — **non-negotiable** in banking systems.
- **Minimum Balance Rule**: Typically ₹1,000 for savings — enforced via both `CHECK` constraint AND trigger.
- **Compound Interest Master Formula**: $A = P \times (1 + R/n)^{n \times t}$ — frequently tested for 14-mark numerical problems.
- **EMI Formula**: $\text{EMI} = \frac{P \times r \times (1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$ — must know by heart for full marks.
- **Codd's Rules (12)**: Often tested as a 3-mark definition question — mention rule 0 (foundational) and rule 12 (non-subversion).
- **Referential Actions**: `CASCADE`, `SET NULL`, `RESTRICT`, `NO ACTION` — know when to use each.
- **KTU's favorite twist**: They will give you a partially normalized table and ask "normalize up to BCNF" — practice identifying transitive dependencies.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy**: Draw the ER diagram from memory, write all 5 DDL statements without looking, and recite ACID properties aloud. These three actions cover ~70% of any BTMS question KTU can ask.
